CROSSCURRENTS
Week of June 14–21, 2026 · Vol. 1 · Issue 23

Notable Quotes

"the press acts like the Strategic Petroleum Reserve is just something that America has because reasons, instead of an actual success of our democratic government that we chose to create and sustain." — Matt Stoller, BIG ("Why the Iran War Hasn't Crashed the Economy")

"We were promised that technology would make us more powerful, masters of our own lives, but instead you and your competitors have mostly given us only finer, more expensive instruments for managing our dependence." — Freddie deBoer, deBoer ("Google Made a Sad Boomer Mark Out of Me and There's Nothing I Can Do About It")

"The clenched fist replaced the extended hand as the symbol of social change." — Eboo Patel, Persuasion ("What Obama Meant")

"The Sahel has proven that exit is sayable. It has not yet proven that it is survivable." — Loic Gapin, African Arguments ("The CFA Franc Is Not a Currency. It Is a Constitutional Constraint")

"Catholic organization without Catholic doctrine, or in other words, Catholicism minus Christianity." […] Something similar could be said of Zemmour. — Michael C. Behrent, Compact ("A Jewish Frenchman's Case for Christian Nationalism")

Themes

The Iran Deal: Capitulation, Caution, and the Drone War's Lessons

A 14-point memorandum of understanding ended the U.S.-Iran war this week. It lifts the U.S. naval blockade and sanctions, restores Iran's oil exports, and defers the nuclear question to a 60-day negotiation. Almost every writer in the cluster, across the full political spectrum, reads the terms as favoring Iran.

That verdict is the cluster's organizing shape, and it is more robust than any single piece claims. National Review hawks, a returning Free Press anti-Islamist columnist, and a center-left Substacker reach it through incompatible lenses. The live argument is not whether the terms favor Iran but what follows — and one empirical question, whether the document hands Iran recognized governance over the Strait of Hormuz, now sits underneath it.

  • The "terms favor Iran" verdict survives across writers who disagree on whether Iran even won the war. A defeat-caller reads a decisive Iranian drone blockade that pushed U.S. inflation to ~4% Smith. A political-economy contrarian reads a near-shrugging economy, because fifty years of public oil stockpiles absorbed the shock Stoller. A deterrence hawk reads overwhelming U.S. tactical dominance — Iranian missile launches down 92%, 60-plus vessels destroyed Rothman. The deal-verdict does not depend on resolving any of this, which is what makes it the cluster's hardest finding to dislodge.

  • The deal's sharpest structural critique is that the concessions come first and the enforcement comes last. Iran banks cash and sanctions relief on signing; the nuclear promises are deferred, conditional, and on the regime's record reversible. The ~$300 billion reconstruction fund and full sanctions termination arrive now; "Iran reaffirms it shall not procure or develop nuclear weapons" is a deferred 60-day negotiation Murray. The same asymmetry, judged against Trump's own standard, makes the MOU materially worse than Obama's 2015 deal — which capped enrichment at 3.67% under inspection — that Trump spent years denouncing Fukuyama.

  • The MOU's nuclear language is read as a non-deal that resets the war's clock to its start. Every stated war aim — regime change, surrender of enriched uranium, an enrichment halt, an end to proxy support — is punted to future talks the regime is unlikely to concede, since they touch its core identity Fukuyama. The document mainly solves a problem the war itself created Heinrichs.

  • The Strait of Hormuz, not the nuclear file, is named as the war's true prize — and it may be going to Iran. The empirical hinge is one MOU provision: Iran "will conduct dialogue with the Sultanate of Oman to define the future administration and maritime services" of the Strait. Read literally, that converts a freely transited international waterway into one Iran has a recognized governance role over, with tolls rebranded as "fees" Heinrichs. A deterrence read extends the precedent: a middle power has shown that modest force can close a chokepoint indefinitely and let economic disruption do the negotiating — a lesson China is "almost certain to test" in the Taiwan Strait Rothman.

  • The disagreement axis is epistemic, not substantive: is it too soon to call this surrender? From one pole, a framework MOU is not a peace; its verdict depends on second- and third-order effects not yet visible (a cash-flush regime growing complacent, Putin weaker than known, China in free-fall), and the muted oil response owed more to the AI investment boom than to any Iranian victory Ferguson. From the other, the concessions are banked now while enforcement is deferred, so "wait and see" is precisely the posture that lets the regime survive to weapons-develop another day Murray. Both concede the terms are weak; they split only on whether the verdict can be rendered yet.

  • The drone war supplies the cluster's most durable lesson, and it cuts against the hawkish triumph. Cheap precision weapons let a middle power deny a great power its objectives: Iran dispersed and buried its missile and drone forces, kept ~70-75% of its arsenal through U.S.-Israeli strikes, then blockaded Hormuz with drone-boats, mines, and missiles Smith. The qualifier complicates the tidy "great powers are weak now" reading: Iran could resist only because it imports drone components — batteries, magnets, motors — from China, which Taiwan could not. So the open question is whether all great powers are now vulnerable, or only the ones whose economies still run on 20th-century combustion industries Smith.

  • The deal is also a marker of elite-conservative disaffection from Trump, distinct from the terms critique. Here the Iran deal is the occasion rather than the subject — the breaking point at which a Trump-voting National Review senior fellow publicly disavows the man he backed three times, calling the capitulation "among the worst foreign-policy blunders in American history" while defending the "binary choice" that produced his votes McCarthy. The register is confessional, not analytic; its value to the cluster is as evidence that the war has fractured the right's own coalition.

The cluster's deep structure is over-determination. A bad-deal verdict that holds whether you read the war as a decisive Iranian victory, a near-painless economic event, or an overwhelming U.S. tactical triumph is not a consensus reached by agreement — it is a conclusion robust to disagreement about the underlying facts. That robustness is itself the finding. When writers who cannot agree on whether Iran won, whether the costs were severe, or whether the U.S. military performed brilliantly nonetheless agree the terms are a giveaway, the giveaway is in the document, not in any contested reading of the war.

That is the move the project's running Iran thread predicted. For weeks the open disagreement over whether time favored the U.S. or Iran has been sliding toward a third resolution — that the framing is wrong, because both sides' institutional incentives produce identical "we are winning" rhetoric while costs migrate to third parties. The MOU is that prediction's artifact: Trump touts a historic win; the regime is more entrenched; the document, by several readings, resets the board to the February status quo Trump himself destroyed. The two camps that once disagreed about direction now agree about the terms and disagree only about the calendar — whether the verdict is rendered now or deferred.

Carry-forward: The earlier question of which side time favors in the Iran confrontation has outlived its usefulness. Across mutually exclusive accounts of the war, writers converge on the same verdict that the deal's terms favor Tehran — so "who does delay help?" no longer separates the readings. The sharper, checkable question the memorandum raises is whether its Oman-Hormuz provision cedes Iran internationally recognized governance over the Strait, a claim testable against the document text. Two near-term tells will settle it: whether Iran begins collecting transit "fees" within the 60-day window, and whether Beijing treats the arrangement as a precedent for a Taiwan-Strait chokepoint.

↩ Back to TOC

AI: Hype, Governance, and Who Controls Compute

The week's AI fight moved off capability and onto control. The operative question is no longer what the next model can do. It is who applies the rules, who owns the compute, and who can be cut off. A US export-control order forced Anthropic to pull a frontier model, and that single act reorganized the cluster around governance rather than benchmarks.

Two readings of that act run side by side. From the policy outside, a kill switch makes American AI a less trustworthy platform and accelerates fragmentation. From the policy inside, the same fact reads as a strategic reality labs must navigate: with no statute, regulation is now executive leverage over individual firms. Around this spine sit hype-deflation (a hyped scanner solves no real diagnostic gap), geopolitical gating (a Western assistant barred from China by speech rules), and an institutional-stress read (the university and everyday consumer accountability both buckling under AI).

  • A government can now switch off a frontier model, and the harm is the precedent, not the outage. A US order required Anthropic to withhold a high-powered model from non-citizens; the only way to comply was to take it down. The lasting cost is that countries and companies will treat American AI as revocable, so they slow adoption, drift toward Chinese or open-source models, and chase "AI sovereignty" that most states cannot achieve — sovereignty needs energy, data centers, grid capacity, permitting, and chips, not just a model (Cowen).
  • The same kill-switch fact, seen from inside policymaking, is governance-by-dispute rather than governance-by-statute. With Congress passing no AI law, all regulation flows from an administration hostile to particular labs, so "what the law says does not matter" and firms must "play nicely" or be kneecapped (Ball). This is the sharper diagnosis the Cowen piece treats as a liability: not "we need AI regulation" but a named mechanism — rule-by-dispute — that the export-control episode exemplifies. The same architect concedes the disruption's scale (trucking "end-to-end automated," ~3.7 million drivers displaced) while prescribing wait-and-see, leaving the gap between acknowledged scale and policy minimalism unresolved.
  • A Western AI product can be locked out of a market by political-speech rules, not market friction. Apple's AI Siri will skip mainland China while it "works through regulatory requirements" — meaning a model must reliably refuse content Beijing deems subversive before it can ship (SW/CDT). The concrete tell: a Chinese chatbot refusing to define the English phrase "It's my duty," which is linked to the 1989 Tiananmen protests. The "One Apple, Two Systems" meme names the receiving end of the same fragmentation the kill-switch drives from the US side.
  • Capability claims keep outrunning the binding constraint, which is rarely the model. A hyped whole-body ultrasound scanner is a cool device that fills no current diagnostic gap, because the limit on whole-body screening is not imaging hardware but the base-rate problem — screening asymptomatic people surfaces more harmless anomalies ("incidentalomas") than real cancers (Alexander). The mirror case: a working mathematician reports AI is already a real productivity gain when guided by a human, compressing the routine middle of research that once needed a school of grad students (the MR email). Same lesson read twice — what matters is whether AI relieves the actual bottleneck, not the headline capability.
  • AI is dissolving the institutions that certify knowledge and absorb consumer risk. For the university, AI is a "catalytic solvent" that renders obsolete the shared formats — book, lecture, problem set, written exam — that bundled research, teaching, and credentialing into one institution, forcing it to break into separate parts and return to a labor-intensive classical core (Gilman). For the consumer, an LLM interface strips away the context cues people use to spot fraud while terms-of-service waivers offload all liability — accountability laundering (deBoer). Both name the same move: AI removes the friction an institution relied on to do its job, and the institution declines to own the loss.
  • The compute boom's valuations may be financial engineering as much as capability. SpaceX's trillion-dollar valuation rests on under $20 billion of slow-growing, loss-making revenue priced at 94 times revenue; the alleged mechanism is self-dealing — Google, a ~5% owner, agreed to pay roughly $11 billion a year for compute, which at that multiple manufactures about $1 trillion of market cap and makes the firm profitable enough to enter the S&P 500 (Stoller). The checkable fraud allegation stands on its own; the morality-play framing — Musk as a believer in transcending human limits through technology, against Pope Leo XIV's defense of those limits — is persuasive packaging that the financial spine does not need.

The cluster's deeper move is that every piece locates the decisive lever somewhere other than the model's capabilities. The kill switch is about discretionary state power; the China lockout is about speech-compliance; the scanner is about screening epidemiology; the university breakup is about who certifies knowledge; the SpaceX valuation is about index-inclusion plumbing and a circular compute contract. A reader arriving from the capability-race framing — bigger model, smarter model — finds that none of the week's actual fights turn on it. The contest is over who applies the rules and who controls the distribution chokepoints wrapped around the technology, which is exactly where leverage now sits.

That reframing also exposes a shared blind spot. The writers who diagnose AI's institutional damage — Gilman on the university, deBoer on consumer accountability — and the writers who diagnose its governance crisis — Cowen on sovereignty, Ball on rule-by-dispute — all prescribe remedies that depend on the very institutions the technology is hollowing out. Gilman needs a more-labor-intensive professoriate funded at a moment when its credential is worth less. Cowen needs sovereignty most states cannot build. The remedies presuppose institutional capacity the same essays say AI is dissolving. Pope Leo XIV's encyclical, read through Stoller, supplies the only frame that sidesteps this trap by relocating the question entirely — from "how do we govern the optimization" to "should limits themselves be treated as defects to optimize away."

One load-bearing premise deserves a flag for downstream readers. Gilman's positive program rests on the claim that AI is ontologically unable to build trust, confer taste, or constitute goals — "not gaps that more compute will close." This is asserted, not argued, and it treats a claim about current architecture as a claim about the technology as such; if those limits are contingent rather than permanent, the "only curriculum with any hope of a stable foundation" loses its foundation. Do not import "AI structurally cannot constitute goals" as an established premise.

Carry-forward: The running thread on whether AI displaces a constituency no one represents gained its sharpest data point yet. Last week a national-conservative program named the jobs only humans should do (Hawley); this week the architect of the administration's AI strategy concedes ~3.7 million truckers will be displaced and prescribes wait-and-see while opposing the one transfer mechanism, universal basic income (Ball) — the displaced are still named faster by their opponents than by their representatives. The same piece reads anti-data-center populism sympathetically, as a demand for agency after two decades of unvoted-upon shocks: the local protest is "the thing you can stop." That build-out is the physical substrate Theme 03 (Energy) costs out from the atoms side.

↩ Back to TOC

Energy & the Physical Economy: Powering the Boom

The binding constraint on the AI build-out is physical, not regulatory. Data-center load, a scarcity of new gas turbines, and cheap grid batteries are now rewriting the economics of both coal and gas. An engineer with no political stake and the clean-energy trade press arrive at that same structural fact from opposite starting points this week.

The week's policy news sits downstream of it. The federal grid regulator issued its first rules for who pays when a data center wants to connect. The administration paid another developer to kill offshore wind near the most power-starved markets in the country. And the AI firm with the cleanest ethical brand bought future carbon removal while buying no clean power at all — a revealed preference about how AI companies actually rank climate against speed.

  • The same three forces — surging data-center demand, a multi-year turbine backlog, and cheap batteries — are scrambling fossil-plant economics from both the engineering and the trade-press side. Coal-to-gas plant conversions, once driven by tightening mercury rules plus cheap shale gas, have nearly run out of candidates; grid batteries now undercut the peaking plants that were the best conversion targets, so even new gas looks less attractive (Potter). Yet AI demand is keeping retirement-slated coal plants online and stacking a years-long wait for new gas turbines (Potter; FERC report). The physical picture, not the policy fight, is what determines what gets built.

  • The grid regulator made "who pays for the AI build-out" the central question — then handed the hard part to the regional markets. The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission ordered the six regional grid operators to write their own rules so that large loads "pay their fair share" and residential customers aren't stuck with the bill if a promised data center never shows up (FERC report). The governing principle was blunt: "Free-riding on other customers is not an option." The 5-0 bipartisan vote is the tell — the genuinely contested distributional choice, how to split grid costs between data centers and households, was deferred to a later, region-by-region stage.

  • Cancelling coastal wind and promising Midwestern gas is not an energy swap, because power doesn't move that way. The administration has now paid to terminate leases for more than 14 gigawatts of planned offshore wind, over half of it bound for the strained New York and New Jersey markets, plus 3.4 GW for high-cost Maine (Heatmap on wind). The latest deal swaps that for gas or geothermal plants in the middle of the country. Transmission constraints mean megawatts in Indiana don't relieve a New York summer peak — the cancelled coastal generation was both near the stressed load and closer to operation than the substitutes.

  • An AI firm buying carbon removal but no clean power is a revealed preference about the sector's real climate ranking. Anthropic joined a carbon-removal fund while having signed no clean-power purchase agreement, published no sustainability report, and run data centers with carbon intensity second-highest among AI competitors (Heatmap on Anthropic). Buying future removal before cutting present emissions inverts the order scientists recommend, since prevention is cheaper than later extraction. Recent clean-energy hires complicate the cynical read, but the order of operations is the finding: speed and compute come first, decarbonization later.

The four pieces describe one machine from four service hatches. Potter is inside the plant, the FERC reporting is at the rate-allocation table, the wind piece is at the coastline, and the Anthropic piece is at the buyer's ledger — and the thing they are all servicing is the same surge of electricity demand from AI compute. That demand is the load-bearing premise of the AI cluster too: Cowen's case that "AI sovereignty" is mostly unachievable rests on the claim that most countries lack the energy, data centers, and grid capacity to host frontier models (Cowen, in Theme 02). This theme supplies the physical accounting under that claim. The grid is the bottleneck, and the bottleneck is being rationed right now — by a regulator deciding who pays, by an administration deciding which generation lives, and by firms deciding what to build first.

The buried implication the cluster shares: every actor here is making a distributional choice while describing it as a technical or market one. FERC frames cost allocation as a market-design exercise and defers the actual split. The administration frames wind cancellation as energy policy while the geography says it is a transfer away from blue coastal grids. Anthropic frames its footprint as a leased-capacity accident while building toward gas-turbine-heavy data centers. The shared move is laundering a "who eats the cost" decision through the language of engineering and markets — which is exactly why the FERC line, "free-riding on other customers is not an option," is the week's sharpest sentence: it names the distributional stakes the other three actors keep implicit.

Carry-forward: The fight over data-center build-out has moved from where the centers get sited to who pays for the grid they need. Earlier weeks found hardening cross-coalitional opposition (71% national opposition, rural voters more opposed than urban) and GOP figures beginning to legislate against it (Hawley's "bring your own power, protect residential rates" ask). FERC's "large loads pay their fair share" orders are the institutional answer to the same who-pays anxiety, now routed through the federal grid regulator rather than a poll or a speech. What to watch: whether the region-by-region rules the grid operators return actually shift stranded-cost risk onto data centers, or whether the 5-0 bipartisan consensus dissolves once the concrete cost splits are drawn.

↩ Back to TOC

The Old Order Cracks: Gerontocracy, the Left's Decline, and Realignment

A single twentieth-century political order is cracking on two axes at once. One axis is generational: the cohort born in 1946 still holds the presidency, the Senate, and the housing market. The other is ideological: mid-century social democracy has lost its economic model, its working-class base, and its standing inside its own coalition.

The cluster's organizing shape is a series of post-mortems on dying formations. The aging elite, the mass-membership Left, the Obama-era faith in narrative, the parties' own internal control — each is diagnosed this week as already dead or visibly failing. The sharpest axis runs through Obama himself: one writer eulogizes "Obamaism" as a betrayed promise, another defends it as a governing philosophy the Left abandoned. The disagreement is over what, exactly, has died.

  • The boomer order is a gerontocracy of refusal, not of inherited wisdom — and the proposed cures split on whether the problem is economic or demographic. A traditional gerontocracy passes down accumulated authority; this one is a generation formed by 1960s radicalism that denies it has aged. The diagnosis is shared. The remedy is contested. Leary rejects the socialist redistribution Samuel Moyn (the Yale law-and-history professor whose book frames the elderly as hoarding wealth and power) prescribes, and counters with immigration restriction, family obligation, and means-testing Social Security. The piece leans on framing immigrants as an "invasion force" to do the work its economics doesn't — treat that causal claim as asserted, not established.

  • Social democracy collapsed on three fronts simultaneously, leaving an educated-elite "Brahmin Left" the working class never asked for. The economic model failed when 1970s stagflation broke Keynesianism. The base shrank as the industrial working class fell below a quarter of the workforce. The intra-Left balance tipped when college-educated professionals replaced workers as the activist core. Teixeira reads the result through Thomas Piketty's "Brahmin Left" (left parties run by the highly educated) — redistribution retained, but priorities shifted to race-conscious remedies, open immigration, gender ideology, and climate, four causes he says the working class never wanted. One platform-analysis finding anchors it: "growth" fell 32% in Democratic platforms since 2012 while "climate" rose 150% and "equity" rose 766%.

  • The Obama reckoning splits cleanly: a tombstone for "Obamaism" versus a defense of it as an abandoned governing philosophy. The disagreement is over what died. From one pole, Obamaism was always hollow — "a politics after politics," the faith that pleasant institutional bromides would bend the moral arc without structural change, now falsified by the return of material politics Zickgraf. From the other, it was a real and worthy philosophy — elevating the social sector's bridge-building into governance — that the Left killed when it swapped an expanding-freedom story for a slavery-legacy story after 2014 Patel. One eulogizes the symbol as never having had substance; the other eulogizes the substance as murdered by its own side. Patel's load-bearing causal claim — that the social sector's turn was a primary cause of Trump's two wins — is asserted, not demonstrated; treat its framing as contested.

  • Socialism's electoral revival reads as alarm from the right and as magical thinking from the center-left. The Democratic Socialists of America now hold the New York City, Seattle, and DC mayoralties, with favorability among Democrats (+17) running above congressional Democrats (+4). Fund treats this as a radicalizing machine — citing a platform to defund the Pentagon, zero out police budgets, and nationalize major corporations — poised to become the "secular religion" of disillusioned young voters. Yglesias attacks the cultural rather than the economic flank: the "Positive Good" theory of Graham Platner (the charismatic, scandal-dogged Maine Senate candidate who beat Gov. Janet Mills in the primary) — the claim that personal misconduct inconsistent with progressive values signals authenticity — is a substitute for the concrete policy moderation the theory's own boosters won't endorse.

  • Class capture now reaches even leisure: the rich own the good seats, literally and metaphorically. Courtside seats cost more than a year at the priciest universities; the cheapest NBA Finals seat ran over $4,000, roughly 8% of US median annual income. deBoer generalizes from the spectacle — ESPN cutting from the game to billionaires mugging for cameras — into a structural claim: "All good things, eventually, go to the rich," because money buys the rules that protect the moneyed. The newer cruelty is the dropped pretense — where wealth once playacted noblesse oblige, the good seats now smirk back.

  • Both the GOP's capture by Trump and the establishment's failure to predict it trace to the same source: voters who stopped deferring to party elites. The "Party Decides" model held that elite endorsements ultimately control nominations. Silver, calling his 2016 dismissal of Trump "the biggest mistake of my career," credits Republican voters — the establishment lost credibility after McCain and Romney lost, offered stale choices, and zigged center on immigration while Trump zagged. The capture also broke a mechanism: Trump's false stolen-2020 claim disabled the party's loss-learning reflex, leaving a "deeply unhealthy party."

The cluster's unifying move is that nearly every writer here is performing an autopsy and disagreeing only about cause of death. Gerontocracy, social democracy, Obamaism, and elite party control are all diagnosed as dead or dying — but the death certificates point in opposite directions. Leary blames demographic dilution where Moyn blames capital; Zickgraf blames Obama's emptiness where Patel blames the Left's repudiation of his fullness; Fund sees socialism rising where Teixeira sees the social-democratic Left already a corpse the educated class has reanimated for its own purposes.

The buried implication the autopsies share is that the replacement is not yet born. Teixeira hints a worker-oriented dynamic-capitalism politics "may have to come from elsewhere"; Patel hopes the nonprofit sector can revitalize rather than memorialize; Silver flags the open question of whether Democratic voters are now estranging from their own establishment the way Republican voters did. None can name what comes after. The order is cracking faster than any successor is forming, which is the structural condition under which both the Platner insurgency and the DSA mayoralties become legible — not as a coherent new movement, but as the energy that rushes into a vacuum no one has filled.

Carry-forward: The continuing read of the Democratic apparatus's enforcement decay extended along its doctrinal axis this week. The prior two weeks treated the Platner cascade as the party machinery failing its candidate-selection test — the 72-19 primary margin the coordinated revelations could not move. Yglesias now supplies the apparatus's remaining lever: if the insurgent left cannot convert candidate-selection wins into concrete policy moderation, its "Positive Good" aesthetics become the opening through which it gets reframed as unserious. The next 18 months turn on whether the post-2008 cohort builds operational doctrine before the order it is displacing finishes dying.

↩ Back to TOC

The Wonk Agenda: Abundance, Asset-Building, and Policy Design

Domestic-policy mechanics dominate this cluster. Each piece refuses the headline policy frame and asks what variable the frame is a proxy for. Transit "underfunding" is really a cost-per-mile problem; "gifted" schools' success is really a selection effect; an asset account's inequality is really a question about the right counterfactual.

The cluster splits into two threads. One is a supply-side abundance set — get more transit, more instruction, and more fiscal room per dollar spent by fixing the production function rather than the budget line. The other is a clean named disagreement over Trump Accounts (the $1,000-at-birth investment accounts created in the One Big Beautiful Bill for newborns): whether bottom-half security should come from owning capital or from wages plus social insurance.

  • The binding constraint on US transit is cost-per-mile, not funding — so cost-effectiveness reform builds more without spending more. America now leads the world in per-mile construction costs; New York's East Side Access ran $11.2B over 24 years, and California high-speed rail ballooned from $33B to $126B while China built 30,000 miles of high-speed rail. The Slow Boring/Institute for Progress "Transit Abundance Playbook" attributes the bloat to overdesign, weak procurement, too many veto points, and hollowed-out agency capacity, then compiles 16 named fixes — milestone-based planning grants, best-value (not lowest-bid) procurement with itemized cost databases as in Italy and Spain, European review standards, single-decision-maker permitting, and rebuilt in-house staff against consultant dependence. The framing is that cost-effectiveness is non-polarizing: it appeals to transit advocates, fiscal conservatives, environmentalists, and drivers at once. The body is largely a guest-authored playbook by Poff-Webster and Datta; Yglesias's contribution is the reframe.

  • "Gifted" programs' graduates succeed because high-ability kids are identifiable — which means the programs may teach nothing. Yglesias rejects a New York Magazine cover claim that gifted children can't be identified, citing a study of precocious youth where 12.3% reached "eminence" (full professors, Fortune 500 executives, distinguished judges). But identifiability is exactly the problem: causal-design research (regression-discontinuity plus lottery admission) finds gifted-and-talented entry changes the classroom yet produces no achievement gain, and NYC exam schools show little effect on test scores, college enrollment, or graduation. The diagnosis is that parents massively under-rate selection effects — chasing above-average peers rather than instructional value, in a zero-sum scramble auctioned through real estate and tuition. The prescription is to teach each child at their own readiness level, the way a gym with stronger members doesn't make you stronger.

  • Immigration is a fiscal lever because an above-average earner pays into a system whose spending skews to retirees and defense. Working a new Manhattan Institute calculator, Yglesias shows that pairing legalization with a fine turns ~10 million undocumented workers into a fiscal gain, and that tilting visas toward labor-market success and high-wage H-1B offers helps the debt path. He calls the mechanism a "double dividend": the worker pays in without near-term cost, and immigrants grow the debt-to-GDP denominator (more GDP shrinks the ratio). He concedes housing and grid constraints but calls immigration-restriction-as-housing-fix "loser behavior." The fiscal case is front-loaded, though — it leans on permanent residents arriving as prime-age earners and on AI-driven growth to carry the long-run numbers, so the near-term gain is the strongest part of the argument.

  • The Trump Accounts fork is whether bottom-half security comes from owning capital or from wages plus social insurance. This is the cluster's genuine disagreement, and the data can't settle it because the two sides are answering different questions. From the asset-ownership pole, the right question is whether a poor kid is better off with an account than without one — and the answer is yes. The bottom half holds about 2.5% of wealth and 1% of equities, so even modest stock exposure matters; accounts also pull in employer and philanthropic contributions. Nearly two decades of "asset effects" research finds that merely having an account raises maternal hope and a child's college-going identity even when families save little Boshara. From the wages-and-insurance pole, the premise itself is the error: in a well-designed society one reaches the middle class through wages plus universal social insurance, without needing to own income-generating capital. The accounts "delegitimize wage labor" and are too small to matter — about $5,389 by age 18 unmatched, against a $35,856 median first-time down payment. They were designed as a backdoor to privatize Social Security, with Treasury Secretary Bessent and Senator Cruz quoted admitting as much Lind. Lind coins "placebo populism" — cheap simulacra of elite status handed to workers instead of the real levers of higher wages and anti-monopoly price relief. Boshara concedes the regressivity outright (rich families max out, poor ones don't) and explicitly rejects the privatization use ("how kids start life, not how they end it"). Same accounts; opposite questions. The disagreement is what the cluster is.

  • Occupational licensing's promise is a guarantee it can't actually deliver. Tabarrok revisits his natural-experiment study of Colorado — the only state to delicense funeral directors (in 1983) — which found delicensing lowered prices and shifted demand toward cheaper cremation. Colorado is now relicensing after 2023 scandals over body-part sales and rotting corpses, but the abuses don't track what licensing trains for, comparable abuse occurs in licensed states, and a 1990 state review found no widespread post-delicensing harm. The closing claim is that people demand a guarantee of good behavior that licensing cannot supply, while criminal penalties (the worst offender got 40 years) already do the deterrence work.

  • A supply-side data point: TCJA tax cuts tracked higher participation and employment. A Dallas Fed study exploiting state-level variation in the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act finds that cuts equal to 1% of adjusted gross income coincided with a 0.7–1 point rise in labor-force participation and a 0.8–1.5% rise in payroll employment over two years. Cowen presents the abstract without commentary, as a supply-side data point.

Every piece in this cluster runs the same epistemic move: refuse the headline frame and ask what the frame is a proxy for. Transit funding is a proxy for the production function. Gifted-school quality is a proxy for peer selection. Licensing is a proxy for a guarantee. An asset account's measured inequality is a proxy for a counterfactual that depends on which baseline you choose. This is the abundance temperament generalized past abundance — the wonk's reflex to dissolve a political category into the underlying variable it tracks.

The Lind-Boshara fork is the one place the move stops working, and that is the cluster's most useful finding. The selection-effects discipline can adjudicate transit, schools, and licensing because each has a measurable target variable. It cannot adjudicate Trump Accounts, because the disagreement is not empirical at all — Lind and Boshara agree on nearly every fact, including the regressivity, and split on what bottom-half security is for. Boshara's "better off with than without" frame is unanswerable on its own terms (almost anything beats nothing), which is exactly why Lind reframes to "what should the route to security be."

Where the rest of the cluster converts a value-question into a variable, the asset-building debate exposes the value-question the variable-conversion was hiding. It connects to the project's standing finding from prior weeks that asset-price returns have outpaced wages since 2012, which makes "how the bottom half gets capital" the live distributional question of the decade.

Carry-forward: The Lind-Boshara split — asset ownership versus wages-plus-social-insurance as the route to bottom-half security — is the kind of disagreement that should resolve rather than simmer. The question is specific and forecastable, not a clash of values: does account ownership produce measurable downstream gains for poor kids? The two camps are credible and cross-orientation — a former Fed wealth-inequality director who spent two decades on child trust funds, against an anti-globalist populist who once helped build the same idea and now repudiates it. It resolves as asset-effects research either replicates at scale on the 14M+ Trump Account cohort or fails to, and as the accounts are used by 2028 to means-test or to privatize Social Security.

↩ Back to TOC

Europe's Strategic and Political Moment

Europe is reorganizing around strategic autonomy while Britain churns through a leadership crisis. The cluster's organizing finding is a recurring gap between the power to decide and the power to make a decision last. Every reform program here promises decisiveness; the live question is durability — whether a thing survives staffing, time, and the next election.

The sub-threads run in parallel. Britain debates how to fix a failing state (Cummings) and who should lead it (the Burnham succession). Europe debates how to defend itself without America (Luttwak on a hollowed-out British military, Macron's European nuclear gamble). And a third thread interrogates the framing itself: whether "the return of history" is realism or disorientation (Tooze), whether authoritarian "efficiency" is real (Møller), and whether one Nordic model validates a whole doctrine (Wooldridge via Cowen).

  • Britain's likely next prime minister is running on a brand that contradicts his own record. Andy Burnham — Manchester's mayor since 2017, now back in Parliament after a 20-point by-election rout — is set to challenge an unpopular Keir Starmer. His "Manchesterism" pitch claims regional devolution plus reindustrialization and the "end of neoliberalism." But Manchester's actual success predates him, was driven by foreign investment courted by pro-business elites, and is post-industrial and service-based. The continental press reads the contest as substance-free: a serial loyalist springboarding to power on persona, not program. (Valentin; Euro|topics.)

  • Two writers diagnose British state failure, and both locate the binding constraint in durability rather than decision. From the systems-reform pole, the British state is multiple failing bureaucracies "smashing into each other" — and the fix is a Farage-led purge using the prime minister's unusual constitutional powers to fire civil servants and bypass the courts (Cummings). From the defense pole, Britain's rare discipline of funding actual combat readiness over bases and procurement is being killed by welfare costs and an industry that prefers long weapons programs (Luttwak). Both name a real failure mode; neither supplies a mechanism for how repair survives the decayed institutions it must run through.

  • Macron's European nuclear hedge is real but structurally hollow — and may die before it sets. The break with French orthodoxy — a growing arsenal, ended disclosure, and "advanced deterrence" offered to eight allies — is forward presence, not nuclear sharing: French jets with French crews keep the trigger in French hands, where US arrangements train allied pilots to deliver American bombs. The reassurance gap is operational, not just political. And the 2027 election threatens the whole edifice — the far-right National Rally treats nuclear sovereignty as non-delegable, recalling de Gaulle killing a 1958 multilateral nuclear project. (Jaques-Apke and Wojtowicz.)

  • The "return of history" frame is a symptom, not a diagnosis. The fashionable story — that 2022 and Trump's NATO threats shook Europe into a "politics of events" requiring swift decision over "lawyerly wrangling" — treats the 2008 crash, the Ukraine crises, and the 2015 refugee wave as blind contingency. But those crises were "predictable and overdetermined," legible through conflicting structural logics. Treating them as irrational fortuna betrays technocratic disorientation dressed as realism, and excuses Europe from explaining why the "shocks" were baked in. (Tooze.)

  • "Authoritarian efficiency" is a mirage across war, growth, disaster-avoidance, and climate. The comparative-politics record favors democracies: over 80% of wars won since 1815, a modest but robust long-run growth edge, and — the most durable claim — the avoidance of man-made catastrophes (Mao's famine, Soviet collectivization) that unchecked authority periodically produces. The key distinction is between the capacity to decide and the likelihood of deciding well; authoritarian regimes move fast but frequently move wrong, with no error-correction. (Møller and Skaaning.)

  • One center-right Nordic government is being packaged as proof of a whole doctrine. Sweden's strong fiscal and innovation metrics (35% debt-to-GDP, Europe's most unicorns per capita) are bundled with a restrictionist turn — first net emigration in 50 years, refugee-departure payouts, a crime crackdown credited with cutting shootings 63%. The bundling is the ideological argument, not a finding: the fiscal strengths predate the restrictionist turn, and the short-run crime drop is the weakest-supported causal claim. (Cowen on Wooldridge.)

Read as a unit, the cluster keeps confusing one capacity for another — the power to act with the power to make an action endure. Cummings treats Britain's repair as a problem of insufficiently ruthless will, when his own 2019-20 tenure with the actual powers of No. 10 shows the binding constraint was coalition and durability, not constitutional permission. Macron is racing to bind allies into mechanisms precisely because he knows decision is cheap and entrenchment is dear. Burnham's fresh-face bump is temporary by the same logic that churned through five Tory prime ministers.

Møller supplies the name for the error the other pieces keep making. His authoritarian-efficiency teardown turns on the distinction between the capacity to decide and the likelihood of adopting and sustaining good policy — and that distinction is the whole cluster. The "decisive leader" fantasy (the Farage purge, the eco-authoritarian, the strongman who "simply acts") and the "decisive doctrine" fantasy (Macron's deterrent, Manchesterism, Swedish liberalism) are the same move: treating a hard problem of durable governance as a problem of will. Tooze names the intellectual version — "swift decision making" over institution-building is exactly the improvisation-worship the BIG program's own concrete proposals (common debt, a digital euro, a security council) quietly contradict. The strategic-autonomy moment's real test is not whether Europe can decide to defend itself, but whether anything it decides outlives the leader who decided it.

↩ Back to TOC

The Wider World: China, the Global South, and the Price of Sovereignty

Formal independence keeps showing up alongside deep structural dependence. Five pieces this week treat sovereignty as a constraint rather than a slogan. Each describes a state that is nominally in charge of its own economy or defense, yet bound by an arrangement it cannot easily exit.

The cluster's sharpest move recurs in two pieces: the binding arrangement endures because it serves the very actors empowered to change it. A currency peg protects the class that could vote to leave it; a sound macroeconomy and a hollowed-out politics turn out to be the same insulation seen from two sides. Around it sit two pieces on great-power strategy and decline — China's economy at risk of a Japan-style "zombie" trap, and the 1942 fall of Singapore as a warning about defending two theaters at once — plus Cuba conceding the case for markets under fiscal duress.

  • An arrangement survives not because outsiders coerce it but because the locals who could end it benefit from it. The CFA franc — the currency fourteen West and Central African states peg to the euro under a French convertibility guarantee — is best read as a constitutional question, not an economic one: by what right does an external power set sovereign nations' monetary policy (Gapin)? The answer is not French trickery. A guaranteed, fixed window into the euro is enormously valuable to the political-commercial class that holds savings and signs import contracts, and worthless to the food-seller. The constituency with the most power to exit has the most to lose from exit. That alignment of interests is more durable than any conspiracy, because exposing it does not dissolve it.

  • Exit became real policy only when actors outside the elite bargain forced the question. Elected governments declined for forty years to put franc-zone exit on the table; the 2024-onward Sahel juntas (Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, now the Alliance of Sahel States, planning a currency called the sira) did (Gapin). But the rhetoric runs ahead of reality: the three still use the CFA franc at the unchanged peg, still depend on the common market they technically quit. The Sahel has proven that exit is sayable; it has not proven that it is survivable.

  • A sound macroeconomy and a captured politics can be two faces of one arrangement, not a contradiction. Peru runs 2 percent inflation, ~4 percent growth, and a respected independent central bank, alongside 72 percent informal employment, serial removals of presidents via an elastic "moral incapacity" impeachment clause, and an illegal gold-mining sector now rivaling Colombia's cocaine trade (Rojas). The reframe: economic policy is successfully insulated from politics because politics has been hollowed out and the formal economy left to a minority. On that reading, a likely Keiko Fujimori win — carried by the overseas diaspora, against a domestic majority, by the dynasty most responsible for the decade of instability — consolidates the dysfunction as plausibly as it resolves it. The "best hope for stability" thesis rests mostly on her opponent being worse, not on any positive case that she will govern stably.

  • State control of the banks may make China's "zombie" problem worse, not better. After 1990, Japanese banks kept insolvent firms alive by lending them fresh cheap money to repay old loans ("evergreening"), which starved healthier upstarts of capital, talent, and resources (Smith). Smith argues China is doing the same — official non-performing-loan ratios have fallen since 2021 even as loss-making firms multiplied — and rebuts the reassurance that this is harmless where bank debt is effectively government debt. Even if zombies don't compete for capital, they still compete for finite labor, materials, land, and energy; redirecting bank lending from property to manufacturing just breeds manufacturing zombies that slash prices below cost ("involution"), collapsing margins economy-wide. The estimates of true bad-debt magnitude swing wildly (official 1.5 percent to "double" 10 percent), which is itself a tell that nobody knows the size.

  • Concentrating force in one theater silently decrements the capacity to fight in another. Read the 1942 fall of Singapore as a structured lens, not a prediction: Britain lost an "impregnable" fortress in 70 days because allied joint planning was thin, naval relief kept receding (the "period before relief" stretched from 28-42 days in the 1920s to 180 by 1941), and planners optimized for a preferred future rather than plausible ones (Rehman). The contemporary mapping is a Taipei-for-Hormuz tradeoff: since February's Operation Epic Fury, the US has burned through interceptors and high-end munitions fast enough to delay Foreign Military Sales deliveries (including ~400 Tomahawks to Japan) and prompt private fears Taiwan plans can't be executed near-term. The essay's sharpest turn runs the hubris parallel both ways — interwar Tokyo read the democracies as exhausted, a "partial and self-serving" reading, and Beijing may now read America as a "limping giant," absorbing only the half of the Iran war that confirms its narrative.

  • Cuba is conceding the case for markets under duress. A sweeping liberalization — private and foreign capital in fuel and banking, foreign direct hiring, 99-year surface rights, sale of state company shares, and a state so destitute it is offloading its zoos — amounts to a structural about-face six decades after Cuba banned even fried-food stands (Tabarrok). The directional claim (the regime concedes markets under pressure) is well-supported; the open question is whether announcement survives contact with the bureaucracy, since "case-by-case evaluation" language is the historical mechanism by which prior Cuban openings got throttled.

The cluster's center of gravity is a single insight that generalizes past either case: the most durable constraint on a state's autonomy is often not the external power that imposed it but the internal constituency the constraint protects. The franc zone "survives because it serves the people who are empowered to decide its fate" (Gapin). Peru is the same shape from the macro side, where central-bank independence and a captured legislature are not in tension but mutually reinforcing (Rojas): the insulation that keeps inflation at 2 percent is the same insulation that lets the gold mafias write their own immunity into law.

Read against the China and Singapore pieces, this reframes what "the price of sovereignty" means. Zombie-lending and overstretched theaters are usually filed under economic mismanagement and strategic error — agency failures. The internal-beneficiary reading suggests the harder problem is that the arrangements producing these outcomes have domestic beneficiaries who function as their immune system. China keeps the zombies because Beijing fears the unemployment shock Japan also refused to absorb; the constituency protected by forbearance is the workforce whose unrest the state most fears. Across all five cases, the binding constraint is held in place less by the party who benefits abroad than by the party who would bear the cost of removing it at home.

Carry-forward: Three pieces feed continuing threads on Chinese power and post-colonial sovereignty. Smith's zombification piece is direct evidence on the industrial-capacity side of the long-running dispute over whether China's strength is peaking or merely maturing — the banking reckoning a demographic-peak reading files under near-term fragility and an industrial-patience reading files under absorbable cost. Rehman's Fortress Singapore essay turns the Iran war into an empirical check on U.S. magazine depth and the "simultaneity challenge," with how Beijing reads America's burn rate as the load-bearing variable for Taiwan. And Gapin's franc-zone reframe plus the Sahel exit supply the missing mechanism for why post-colonial monetary arrangements outlast the colonial relationship — the domestic beneficiary, not the foreign one, holds them in place.

↩ Back to TOC

Faith, Ritual, and National Identity

The cluster asks one question across faith, sport, and nation. Can a shared symbolic order be rebuilt after belief in it is gone? Several pieces answer by proposing a substitute for belief — inheritance, comparison, submission, fellowship — and then run into the same wall.

The semiquincentennial supplies the occasion. Two essays argue America's founding creed survives only in chastened or comparative form; two read political spectacle as the new civic ritual; two locate the binding force not in doctrine but in a religion's institutional shell or an artist's bleak posture toward an inscrutable God. The organizing pattern is that almost every proposed reconstruction tries to keep the social effect of belief while discarding belief itself.

  • Political spectacle is being read as genuine civic ritual, and the contested question is whether ritual amounts to a transfer of power. Trump's White House cage fight gets framed as combat sport displacing both baseball's republican restraint (no single player dominates) and football's managerial empire (the quarterback coordinates a specialized machine) — a populist rebellion against elite management (Schmitz). The frame is contestable: its "anti-imperial" charge is borrowed from analyses of colonized peoples asserting dignity against an oppressor, and transposing it onto a sitting president — the most powerful office on earth, hosting a fight on the imperial lawn — inverts the analysis it cites. The likelier read, spectacle as legitimation, is precisely what "bread and circuses" names, and the piece denies that without arguing it.

  • The event's load-bearing detail was institutional, not aesthetic. Roughly a dozen military units lent the spectacle "faux dignity" — including an Armed Forces Full Honor Cordon, the ceremony normally reserved for visiting heads of state and dead presidents, deployed here for a prizefight weigh-in (Klippenstein). The norm-erosion benchmark is internal: in 2020 the nation's top officer apologized merely for appearing at a Trump photo-op, and now no senior officer has objected. The catalog inflates by lumping a real anomaly (the Cordon) with routine pageantry (color guard, national anthem), but the sharpest claim is that nobody noticed — the military is so embedded in civic life that the question never came up.

  • Two reconstructions of national identity converge on the same instability: faith repurposed as cultural cement, with the belief drained out. This is the cluster's clearest cross-piece pattern, surfaced from opposite directions.

    From the French right, a non-believing Jewish nationalist defends Catholic France as inheritance — order, law, and ritual without the resurrection — what one critic called "Catholicism minus Christianity" (Behrent on Éric Zemmour). The scaffolding is Ernest Renan's constructivist nation (a "daily plebiscite" resting on shared memory, not race or creed), which reframes Zemmour from an ancestry-and-territory nationalist to a paradoxical modernist who wants a deliberately built but still binding shared past. The self-undermining contradiction is named directly: if Catholicism is defended as institution-and-order rather than as truth, the order-based exclusion of Islam — which also offers order, law, and tradition — collapses, since Zemmour's own framework cannot supply the boundary it asserts.

    From the American center, the case for "progressive patriotism" rests on revealed preference (America is migrants' top destination) and on comparing America to the world as it actually is rather than to an imagined utopia (Europe's callback studies show more discrimination; a Black head of state elected before most neighbors) (Hughes). Treat its central claim as contested: the comparative method systematically benchmarks against worse performers while declining to benchmark against better ones — the same selective-comparator habit it faults progressives for — and migration tracks wealth, scale, and diaspora networks more than cultural virtue, so "best country by revealed preference" is an inference the piece asserts rather than establishes.

  • Belief, where a piece keeps it whole, does work the substitutes can't. Cohen's late "dark faith" is read as a specifically Jewish submission to an inscrutable, even violent God — the "Hineini" of Abraham at the binding of Isaac — bleak but not nihilistic, its darkness moral rather than merely moody against the Holocaust shadow of 1950s Montreal (Scott). The exceptionalism essay's real contribution is the parallel move: Lincoln's "almost chosen people" under providential judgment, with the Civil War as penalty for slavery, a creed that absorbs the national sins the left deploys against it because in that frame the sins are exactly what Providence judges (Watts). The catch the essay waves off is that "chastened exceptionalism" is unfalsifiable as a civic device — triumphs prove the destiny, atrocities prove the judgment — a paradox Lincoln could hold only because he believed in the judging God literally.

  • The civic-association diagnosis names the structural ground beneath the whole cluster, but its cure is voluntarist against a structural force. The atrophy of intermediary institutions leaves "the one" exposed directly to "the 99," dissolving the buffer (friend group, congregation, union) between the person and both the crowd and the state (Burgis). The weakness is a category-slide: porting Havel's "post-totalitarianism" — a one-party state delivering propaganda with the onions — onto the conformity that peers and algorithms produce inflates voluntary performance into the menace of life under the secret police. And if the cause is structural (platforms built to connect the one to the millions), "go build a small fellowship" is individual virtue against a systemic force.

A naturalistic counterpoint sits at the cluster's edge: a detailed optical-physiological account of the Fatima/Medjugorje "spinning sun" — dimmed-sun afterimages smeared by involuntary eye movements, plus UV chromatopsia — that explains the canonical mass religious vision without the supernatural (Alexander). Read against the rest of the theme, it is the limiting case: not faith reconstructed after belief, but the perception itself dissolved into mechanism, with priming — the crowd expecting a miracle — supplying the staring the divine was thought to supply.

The buried implication no single piece names is that the cluster has stumbled onto a resource problem. Zemmour wants ritual without doctrine, Hughes wants pride without myth, Burgis wants fellowship without a shared creed to bind it, and Cohen offers submission without consolation — each tries to keep the binding effect of belief while spending down the belief that produced it. The one reconstruction that holds, Lincoln's, holds only because Lincoln actually believed in a God who judges nations; the chastened-exceptionalism device works as theology and fails as a civic technique precisely at the point where the believing is removed. Across faith, sport, and nation, the recurring move is to treat belief as a sunk asset whose social yield can be harvested indefinitely after the principal is gone — and the recurring failure is that the yield turns out to depend on the principal.

This connects to the project's standing thread on symbolism substituting for structure, running this week through the Obama-Center-as-mausoleum reading (Zickgraf) and the autopsy of "Obamaism" as a faith that narrative could replace material change (Patel). The faith cluster supplies the inverse diagnosis: not symbolism replacing structure, but symbolism trying to survive the collapse of the belief that gave it meaning.

↩ Back to TOC

Cross-theme insights

Crosscurrents synthesis — June 14-21, 2026

A 14-point memorandum of understanding ended the U.S.-Iran war this week, and the document is the week's load-bearing fact. It lifts the U.S. naval blockade, restores Iran's oil exports, releases sanctions, commits ~$300 billion in regional reconstruction, and defers the nuclear question to a 60-day negotiation. Almost every writer who touched it, across the full political spectrum, read the terms as a giveaway to Tehran — and they reached that verdict through accounts of the war that cannot all be true at once. By week's end the deal was already fraying. Iran announced it had re-closed the Strait over continued Israeli strikes in Lebanon, Trump threatened to "hit Iran very hard again," and a Republican senator who voted for him three times called the capitulation "among the worst foreign-policy blunders in American history." The agreement's instability is not an accident of bad drafting. It is what happens when a document is signed to solve a domestic problem — gas prices and an inflation spike at 36% approval — rather than the conflict it nominally addresses. That gap, between what an arrangement says it is for and what actually holds it in place or tears it apart, ran through every theme this week.

How the themes interlock

The week's hardest findings are the ones that survive disagreement about the underlying facts. The Iran cluster's organizing verdict — the MOU's terms favor Iran — holds across three mutually exclusive readings of the war. One writer sees a decisive Iranian victory: a buried, dispersed drone-and-mine force that kept ~70% of its arsenal, then blockaded Hormuz and pushed U.S. inflation back to 4% (Smith). Another sees a near-painless economic event, because fifty years of public oil stockpiles absorbed the shock (Stoller). A third sees overwhelming U.S. tactical dominance — Iranian missile launches down 92%, sixty-plus vessels destroyed (Rothman). The deal-verdict needs none of these resolved. When National Review hawks, a returning anti-Islamist columnist (Murray), and a center-left Substacker agree the terms are a giveaway while disagreeing on whether Iran even won, the giveaway is in the document, not in any contested reading of the war. A conclusion robust to disagreement about its premises is sturdier than a consensus, because there is no shared premise to knock out from under it.

The thing that actually holds an arrangement together, or pulls it apart, is usually not the thing the arrangement is nominally about. This is the week's deepest cross-theme pattern, and it recurs in domains that share nothing else. The Iran deal is sold as a nuclear settlement, but its operative driver is domestic: rising oil prices forced the Strait reopened "at virtually any cost" (Fukuyama). Its sharpest contested prize is not the nuclear file at all but a provision letting Iran negotiate "future administration and maritime services" of Hormuz with Oman — converting a free waterway into one Iran has a recognized governance role over (Heinrichs). The CFA franc is debated as monetary economics but is really a constitutional question, and it survives not through French coercion but because guaranteed euro convertibility serves the local class with the power to exit and costs the food-seller nothing (Gapin). Peru's prized macroeconomic stability — 2% inflation, an independent central bank — is the same insulation that lets a captured legislature shield the gold mafias; sound policy and hollowed politics are two faces of one arrangement (Rojas). Even the AI fight has moved off the axis it claims to be about: not one of the week's actual AI disputes turns on model capability. The export-control kill switch is about discretionary state power; the China lockout is about speech-compliance; the hyped body scanner is about screening epidemiology; the SpaceX valuation is about index-inclusion plumbing and a circular compute contract (Cowen; Stoller). In each case the stated subject is a decoy. The operative variable sits one layer down — and the writers who find that layer are doing the analysis the headline frame forecloses.

Several clusters converge on a single structural insight: the binding constraint on a system is held by whoever bears the cost of changing it, not whoever benefits from keeping it. The franc zone "survives because it serves the people empowered to decide its fate." China keeps insolvent "zombie" firms alive not from confusion but because the alternative — Japan's 1990s reckoning — means an unemployment shock the state fears more than the productivity drag (Smith). Read together, these reframe what looks like dysfunction as a stable equilibrium with a domestic immune system. The energy cluster supplies the American instance. FERC made "who pays for the AI build-out" the central question, then deferred the genuinely contested distributional choice — how to split grid costs between data centers and households — to a later, region-by-region stage, behind a 5-0 bipartisan vote (FERC report). Every actor in that cluster is making a distributional choice while describing it as a technical or market one. The regulator frames cost allocation as market design. The administration frames cancelling 14 gigawatts of coastal wind as energy policy, while the transmission geography says it is a transfer away from blue grids. And the AI firm with the cleanest brand buys future carbon removal while buying no clean power at all (Heatmap on wind; Heatmap on Anthropic). The sharpest sentence of the week names exactly this — FERC's "free-riding on other customers is not an option" — because it makes the buried "who eats the cost" decision the other three actors keep implicit.

The error that recurs across Europe, the wonk agenda, and the Old Order autopsy is mistaking the power to decide for the power to make a decision last. Europe is reorganizing around strategic autonomy on the premise that what it lacked was decisiveness — but the cluster keeps confusing one capacity for another. Macron is racing to bind allies into treaty mechanisms precisely because decision is cheap and entrenchment is dear, and his European deterrent may die in the 2027 election before it sets (Jaques-Apke and Wojtowicz). The name for the error comes from the teardown of "authoritarian efficiency": the relevant distinction is between the capacity to decide and the likelihood of deciding well and sustaining it — democracies win because accountability lets them detect and correct mistakes, not because they decide faster (Møller and Skaaning). The same confusion runs the Obamaism autopsy. The reckoning at the Center's opening is that a "politics after politics" — the faith that narrative and pleasant institutions would bend the arc on their own — was falsified by the return of material politics (Zickgraf), with the rival diagnosis holding that the social sector's turn to a slavery-defined story replaced Obama's expanding-freedom story and helped produce Trump (Patel). Both treat symbolism standing in for structure as the failure mode — the inverse of the faith cluster's finding that symbolism is now trying to survive the collapse of the belief that gave it meaning.

↩ Back to TOC

What's missing

Wire check. Three significant stories in the same window drew no roster engagement. The DOJ Office of Legal Counsel memo on disability rights — an opinion holding that states need not provide in-home or community-based care, which advocates read as reopening the door to institutionalization (NPR, "DOJ memo stokes fear among disability advocates") — is a direct instance of the enforcement-and-rule-architecture thread the project has tracked for weeks, and no roster writer touched it. The investigation finding 93% of ICE arrests targeted Latinos (Guardian) is the granular evidence base under the regressive-enforcement diagnosis the roster narrates structurally but rarely documents at the arrest level. And a European heat emergency — half of France under red alert, records approaching, services disrupted (BBC) — is the kind of acute climate event the project's energy cluster costs out from the supply side but never reaches from the human-impact side.

Perspective check. The structural roster gaps hold. No roster writer is operating from inside an AI-displaced workforce, now a standing absence; the truckers Dean Ball concedes will be displaced are named by the policy architect, not by anyone who drives (Ball). Nobody on the roster has Middle East operational expertise to assess whether the Hormuz-governance provision means what Heinrichs fears, and nobody writes from inside the Iranian population that any settlement is made over. The Wider World cluster has the same shape: Gapin writes the franc zone as a Cameroonian, but no roster writer reports from inside the Sahel currency experiment that would test whether exit is survivable, not just sayable. The faith cluster is argued almost entirely by people reconstructing belief from the outside — a non-believing nationalist defending Catholicism as inheritance (Behrent), a centrist building patriotism on comparison (Hughes) — with the one piece that keeps belief whole treating it as biography rather than as the lived thing the others are missing.

↩ Back to TOC

What the mainstream press is getting wrong

Insight gap: the Iran deal as a domestic instrument, not a diplomatic achievement. The press is tracking the deal as a diplomatic process: will the talks hold, will the next clause be signed. BBC's "Trump and Iran's negotiator trade warnings as talks held in Switzerland," NPR's "Trump threatens to 'hit Iran very hard again,'" and CBS's coverage of Booker's "we have capitulated to the enemy" and Graham's "obliterate Iran" all frame the question as whether the diplomatic track survives. The roster supplies the gap the wire frame cannot reach: the deal's driver is the 36% approval rating and the inflation spike, not the negotiation (Fukuyama), and the structural critique is that concessions are banked now while enforcement is deferred to talks the regime is unlikely to concede (Murray). The press treats the MOU's instability as a story about whether two parties can agree; the roster shows the instability is built in, because a document signed to lower gas prices has no mechanism to compel anything once the prices fall. The single press item that confirms the roster read is Bloomberg's "Three Indian Tankers Reemerge, Pointing to Hormuz Traffic Uptick" — the Strait reopening as a commodity-flow event, with no one in the wire coverage asking the governance question Heinrichs names.

Insight gap: the Obama Center as a referendum on a governing philosophy, not a Trump-Obama feud. The Center's opening was front-page across the outlets, uniformly framed as the Obama-Trump relationship — NPR's "A look at the relationship between Obama and Trump," Fox's "Black excellence" framing, the four-living-presidents tableau. The roster reads the same event as the occasion for an autopsy of what the building represents: a "presidential library" that houses no archive, reads as a mausoleum for narrative-over-structure politics (Zickgraf), and sits inside a larger reckoning with the long decline of social democracy and its replacement by a college-educated activist core (Teixeira). The press covered a ceremony; the roster covered the death of a theory of politics that the ceremony commemorates.

Press absence: the Anthropic export-control kill switch. A U.S. order forced Anthropic to pull a frontier model from non-citizens — the first time Washington declared a model "too dangerous for unrestricted use" — and the only way to comply was to take it down. Three roster writers built the week's AI cluster on this single act (Cowen; Ball); the seven press-scan outlets ran nothing on the precedent it sets. The lasting cost is not the outage but the signal: countries and firms will treat American AI as revocable, slowing adoption and tilting toward Chinese or open-source models. That is a structural story about American technological reach, and it is currently legible only inside the roster.

↩ Back to TOC

Carry-forward

This week resolves one open disagreement, opens two, and advances two standing carry-forward entries.

  • iran-hormuz-governance-transfer (new) — The war's true prize may be the Strait of Hormuz, and the MOU may be ceding Iran an internationally recognized governance role over it.

    • Warrant: The provision letting Iran negotiate "future administration and maritime services" of the Strait with Oman is read literally as converting a free waterway into one Iran governs, with tolls rebranded as "fees" (Heinrichs); the deterrence read extends it — a middle power has shown modest force can close a chokepoint indefinitely and let economic disruption negotiate on its behalf (Rothman).
    • Implication: Over 1-5 years, if the provision means what its critics fear, the precedent travels — China is "almost certain to test" the same logic in the Taiwan Strait, extending the standing finding that what gets transferred at chokepoints is rule-setting position, not resource access.
    • Test: Whether Iran collects transit "fees" within the 60-day window. Whether the Oman talks produce a recognized governance role, and whether a Chinese chokepoint move on Taiwan cites the Hormuz precedent within 24 months.

  • distributional-choice-laundered-as-technical (new) — Across energy, AI governance, and grid regulation, actors are making contested "who eats the cost" decisions while describing them as technical or market-design exercises, and deferring the actual distributional split.

    • Warrant: FERC's 5-0 vote names "large loads pay their fair share" but defers the household-versus-data-center split to the regions (FERC report); the administration frames cancelling 14 GW of coastal wind as energy policy while the geography makes it a transfer away from blue grids (Heatmap on wind); an AI firm buys carbon removal before cutting emissions (Heatmap on Anthropic).
    • Implication: On a 1-3 year horizon, consensus visible in the abstract (a unanimous FERC vote, a signed framework MOU) dissolves once concrete splits are drawn. The most-cited "wins" of this period are the points where the contested distribution was deferred, not resolved.
    • Test: Whether the regional grid operators' returned rules actually shift stranded-cost risk onto data centers or quietly leave it with households. Whether the 5-0 consensus survives the cost-allocation stage.

  • iran-time-direction (resolved) — The standing open disagreement over whether time favored the U.S. or Iran is now resolved toward "the question is wrong." The cumulative cross-orientation convergence on a bad deal — reached across mutually exclusive accounts of the war — has passed the level at which the original time-favors-X framing remains useful. Both camps now agree the terms are a giveaway and disagree only on the calendar: whether the verdict can be rendered now or must wait for second-order effects (Ferguson; Murray). The successor empirical dispute is the Hormuz-governance question above.

  • datacenter-buildout-resistance (refined) — The thread advances for a third straight week, now into the cost-allocation stage. Prior weeks moved from NIMBY polling (71% national opposition) to a Republican-senator legislative ask (Hawley's "bring your own power, protect residential rates"). FERC's "large loads pay their fair share" orders are the federal grid regulator's institutional response to the same who-pays anxiety. The physical picture underneath it is the binding constraint, not the policy fight. A multi-year gas-turbine backlog, grid batteries undercutting peaking plants, and AI demand keeping retirement-slated coal online are what actually determine what gets built (Potter).

  • ai-displaces-no-represented-constituency (refined, week 10) — The administration's own AI-strategy architect concedes the scale of disruption (trucking "end-to-end automated," ~3.7 million drivers displaced) while prescribing wait-and-see and opposing the one transfer mechanism on the table (Ball). The constituency is still named by its opponents and its administrators faster than by anyone who represents it, confirming the no-constituency finding from the policy-architect's own mouth.

↩ Back to TOC

Meta-finding

The analytical mode that did the most work this week was the move that locates the operative variable one layer below the stated one — and refuses the frame the headline supplies. It is what makes the Iran deal-verdict hold across incompatible war-readings (the terms, not the war, are the object), what reframes the franc zone from economics to constituency-interest, what turns Peru's "stability" into the visible face of capture, and what shows every AI fight turning on control rather than capability. The mode's discipline is to ask what a frame is a proxy for: the wonk cluster runs it as a method, dissolving transit "funding" into cost-per-mile and gifted-school "quality" into peer selection. Its limit is also this week's sharpest finding. The Lind-Boshara debate over Trump Accounts is the one place the move stops working — because the disagreement is not empirical at all. The two agree on nearly every fact, including the accounts' regressivity, and split on what bottom-half security is for: owning capital, or wages plus social insurance (Lind; Boshara). When the layer below a frame turns out to be a value question rather than a hidden variable, the proxy-dissolving method exposes the value question instead of resolving it — which is the most honest thing it can do. Material data carried the energy cluster (the gigawatt and turbine-backlog numbers) and the SpaceX fraud allegation, where the financial spine stands on its own without the morality-play packaging. Interpretive framework did the load-bearing work everywhere the operative variable was not a number — Møller's distinction between deciding and making a decision last, Gapin's constitutional reframe, the faith cluster's reading of belief as a spent principal whose social yield still gets harvested — and none of those would have survived being reduced to a metric.

↩ Back to TOC

Optimist's take

Crosscurrents optimist's take — June 14 to June 21, 2026.

The synthesis reads the week as a catalog of arrangements that outlast the people who could change them: a deal that resets the Iran war's clock to its start, a kill switch that makes American AI revocable, monetary and security architectures held in place by the constituencies they protect, a civic-ritual collapse no substitute can refill. None of that rotates away. But the same evidence, read for what actually held this week rather than what failed, yields five world-facts that survive striking every writer's name from the page.

The biggest war story of the week is a non-event, and the non-event is the achievement.
