The post's evidential weight comes from its source position: a practicing mathematician reporting first-person workflow changes, not a commentator forecasting. That makes the specific mechanisms credible — conjecture-testing, draft-salvaging, and using AI as a faster path through standard technique are exactly the low-glamour productivity gains that show up first in a field, and the framing of "human-guided, not autonomous" is the right corrective to benchmark-centric AI-capability discourse. The observation that the lower-hanging fruit is "slightly off the beaten track, but not esoteric" is a genuinely useful structural claim: AI compresses the cost of the routine-but-laborious middle of research, which historically justified large grad-student labor.

Two claims are doing more rhetorical work than the email substantiates. The peer-review prediction — that running published papers past AI as a "control" will expose widespread error — assumes current models reliably detect subtle mathematical mistakes, which is precisely the capability still in question; the same systems that "referee each other" also confidently produce flawed proofs, and the post doesn't grapple with the false-positive cost of an AI dragnet over the literature. And the closing comparison to the post-Witten revolution is asserted, not argued ("buckle up"), which is the genre tell of an enthusiast email rather than a calibrated forecast. Cowen's choice to publish without comment functions as endorsement-by-curation of the optimistic read; the durable, defensible content is the texture of present-day workflow change, while the magnitude claims should be held as one informed practitioner's bet rather than established trajectory.

↩ Back to TOC

The University As We Know It Is Finished

Nils Gilman · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The question of how universities should respond to large language models, framed against the April 2026 Report of the Yale Committee on Trust in Higher Education, which Gilman faults for treating AI as a mere pedagogical inconvenience. Nils Gilman (Senior Advisor to the Berggruen Institute, former Associate Chancellor of UC Berkeley) builds his argument on Clark Kerr—the UC president who in 1963 coined "multiversity" to describe the postwar research university as a loosely-bundled conglomerate doing research, job-training, credentialing, and coming-of-age all at once, held together not by coherence but by competent management. Gilman's thesis is that Kerr's model is finished: decades of tuition hyperinflation, adjunctification, and vocational credentialism had already strained the bundle, and AI is the "catalytic solvent" that titrates out the incoherence by rendering obsolete the shared technologies—book, lecture, problem set, written exam—that made the components cohere. He argues this need not be catastrophe but opportunity: a return to the classical liberal-arts model. The "co-curricular dodge" (residential life as the irreducible value, à la Molly Worthen's "universities as monasteries") is real but insufficient because it leaves the curriculum untouched. His positive program centers on what AI structurally cannot do—build trust, confer taste/style, constitute goals—and rebuilds pedagogy around live assessment (oral exams, Socratic examination, design critique, decision logs) and faculty as interlocutors modeling real-time uncertainty-calibration rather than lecturers, which he predicts means demand for more professors, not fewer. The curriculum should be unabashedly classical (history, philosophy, literature, political theory) since those train exactly the AI-irreplaceable capacities. He concedes the central obstacle is scale—the tutorial model is labor-intensive and requires faculty skills that research-prestige incentives neither produce nor reward—and predicts the multiversity will ultimately disaggregate into separate research centers, residential facilities, and honest teaching systems, with even elite credentials losing signaling value.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a strong, unusually well-constructed long-form argument, and its central analytic move is genuinely clarifying: framing the multiversity as a bundle held together by a shared technological substrate (the formats of knowledge transmission) rather than by intellectual coherence, then arguing that AI doesn't attack any single function but dissolves the substrate that bundled them. That reframing—"AI as solvent, not competitor"—does real work because it explains why the response of patching individual courses misses the structural point. The Kerr scaffolding is not decorative; the irony Gilman lands (the research university incubated the technology now dissolving its inherited form) earns its place.

The argument's load-bearing weak point is the claim about what AI "structurally cannot do." Gilman asserts that trust, taste, and goal-constitution "follow from the ontology of the technology itself" and "are not gaps that more compute will close"—but this is stated, not argued. It's the hinge on which the entire positive program (rebuild the curriculum around AI's permanent limitations) rests, and if those limitations are contingent rather than ontological, the "only curriculum with any hope of a stable foundation" loses its foundation. He treats a claim about current architecture as a claim about the technology "as such," which is precisely the kind of iron-law overreach that ages badly. The summary flags this because downstream readers should not import "AI structurally cannot constitute goals" as an established premise; it is the most contestable sentence in a piece that is otherwise careful.

The second tension is internal and Gilman half-acknowledges it: he predicts AI will create demand for more professors (because tutorial pedagogy is labor-intensive) while also predicting the multiversity will disaggregate and credentials will lose signaling value. These can both be true, but the economics are in some friction—who pays for a more-labor-intensive model at a moment when the credential it produces is worth less? The "But Does It Scale?" section names the labor problem honestly but doesn't resolve the financing problem, and the closing "generational project" framing functions partly to defer that resolution. Read against the genre of AI-and-the-university think-pieces, this is well above the median: it resists both the doom register and the techno-utopian one, it engages a specific institutional document (the Yale report) rather than a strawman, and it concedes its own hardest objection. The piece would be even stronger if it treated the AI-limitations premise as a hypothesis to defend rather than an axiom to build on.

↩ Back to TOC

Google Made a Sad Boomer Mark Out of Me and There's Nothing I Can Do About It

Freddie deBoer · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: deBoer's own consumer ordeal with Google, written as an open letter (a "complaint letter" he sends to a "sad legal helpline") narrating the experience. Freddie deBoer (Marxist cultural critic, anti-credentialist Substacker) recounts a cascading failure: he bought five expensive ($800) Google Nest Protect smart smoke detectors during a stressful period of infertility; Google's installer-locator widget falsely reported no technicians in his suburban Connecticut area; after the baby arrived and time passed, Google discontinued the product (despite a 10-year service guarantee), an electrician revealed he needed a sixth unit that was now only available used at gouged prices; Google customer service ("Emily," possibly a bot) offered no refund and only repeated his problem back; and finally, when he asked Google's Gemini LLM to find a sixth unit, it linked him to a scam page through Google Pay, exposing his debit card. The narrative is the vehicle; the argument is the indictment. deBoer's thesis is that LLMs are becoming "an all-encompassing excuse for why massive corporations have literally zero accountability for anything"—their interfaces strip away the context clues users rely on to spot fraud (making the scam harder to detect than it would be on the open web), while terms-of-service waivers ensure no liability, even as the same companies aggressively market LLMs as shopping aids. He widens to two structural claims: that LLMs have made fraud "trivially easy, practically free" to manufacture, deluging everyone with scams and inducing defensive over-deletion (Type II errors—he now misses legitimate opportunities); and that the deeper grievance is powerlessness—technology promised mastery but delivered "finer, more expensive instruments for managing our dependence" on corporations that govern work, politics, and attention with no recourse for ordinary people. He skewers the AI discourse's heads-I-win-tails-you-lose structure: LLMs are world-transforming when hyped, but if you trust one and it fails, you're "a rube, a mark, a patsy" who should have known better.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer working in his comic-essay mode, and the form is doing serious argumentative work, not just entertaining. The open-letter structure (extended self-deprecating narrative, "[hits send]") lets him build the political claim inductively from lived humiliation rather than asserting it, which is more persuasive than a straight polemic would be—the reader has followed every step of the powerlessness before deBoer names it. The genuinely sharp observation, and the one most worth carrying past the comedy, is structural: LLM chat interfaces remove the context cues (URL inspection, page design, the friction of navigating to a site) that users have spent decades learning to read for fraud, so embedding commerce inside an LLM doesn't just add a failure point—it disables the user's existing fraud-detection apparatus while the company markets the tool as making shopping easier. That's a real and under-discussed UX/security claim, independent of whether deBoer's specific ordeal was representative.

The piece's analytical weakness is the one deBoer half-anticipates and waves off: causally, a lot of his story is ordinary corporate-customer-service rot (discontinued product, useless chatbot, no refund) that long predates LLMs, bolted onto one genuinely AI-specific failure (Gemini surfacing a scam link through Google Pay). He fuses them rhetorically into a single indictment of "LLMs as accountability-laundering," but the smoke-detector saga would have happened in 2015. The strongest part of the argument (the interface-disables-fraud-detection point) is somewhat diluted by being packaged with grievances that are about corporate scale and product abandonment generally. A skeptic would also note he can't actually establish the counterfactual—that he'd have caught the scam on the open web—since the whole premise is that he, a self-aware scam-spotter, got fooled; he attributes that to the interface, but it could be ordinary inattention with a one-year-old on his chest, which he also reports.

Read against deBoer's pattern, this is consonant with his recurring theme—that the promised democratization of technology actually deepens dependence on unaccountable institutions—and his class-inflected anti-Silicon-Valley politics (the closing image of "gurus crying into hundred dollar bills" on Joe Rogan). The Anthropic/OpenAI name-checks are scenery, not the target; the target is the accountability vacuum. The piece is best read as a vivid, structurally-real complaint that overreaches when it tries to make a single product saga carry a totalizing thesis about LLMs and corporate power. The interface-security insight is the durable contribution; the monocausal "LLMs cause this" framing is the rhetorical excess. Not fundamentally flawed—the core observation holds—but its scope claims run ahead of its single anecdote.

↩ Back to TOC

Monopoly Round-Up: The Pope and a Silicon Valley Trillionaire Fight Over God

Matt Stoller · 2026-06-15 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Elon Musk crossing a $1 trillion net worth after SpaceX's public share sale, and Pope Leo XIV's encyclical on artificial intelligence — read as opposing visions of whether technology should let humanity transcend its limits.

In the lead essay of his weekly monopoly round-up, Matt Stoller frames Musk and Pope Leo XIV (Robert Prevost, the first American-born pope) as avatars of two worldviews. Musk represents "trans-humanism" — the Silicon Valley belief that technology can transcend human limits (Neuralink "superpowers," ending aging, multi-planetary species) — which Stoller casts as the ultimate expression of Chicago School efficiency-worship, the same logic underlying Robert Bork's antitrust framework and Amazon's low-price promise. The Pope's encyclical ("On safeguarding the human person in the time of artificial intelligence") is the "anti-Musk argument": that AI is "statistical adaptation" that does not imply inner growth or a new stage of humanity, that stateless power "concentrated in the hands of a few" breeds dependencies and manipulation, and that social justice must shape technology's design from the outset, not be retrofitted (a position Stoller distinguishes from Bernie Sanders's merely-socialize-the-output view). The hard-news core is Stoller's claim that Musk's valuation is fraudulent in substance: SpaceX has under $20 billion in revenue, is growing slowly, and loses money, yet was valued at 94x revenue. He alleges the mechanism is self-dealing — Google (a ~5% SpaceX owner) agreed to pay SpaceX ~$11 billion/year for compute, which at 94x revenue manufactures roughly $1 trillion of market cap, turning a $10 billion outlay into a ~$50 billion gain for Google while making SpaceX GAAP-profitable so it can enter the S&P 500. He situates this in broader market manipulation (crypto, Tesla at $1.25 trillion selling fewer cars than the far-cheaper-valued Toyota) and argues Musk — unlike Page, Zuckerberg, or Bezos, whose companies generate real cash flow — will never produce the cash flow to justify his valuations and must keep telling "fables" to sustain them until it crashes. The round-up then flags follow-on items (a US-Iran Strait of Hormuz deal, Meta's "Gulag-like" AI culture, Paramount manipulating the Antitrust Division over its Warner bid, Spirit Air executive bonuses amid liquidation, Intel's turnaround) behind a paywall.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece operates on two registers that should be judged separately, because they are uneven. The financial-mechanics core — the Google-SpaceX compute deal as a circular self-dealing transaction engineered to manufacture valuation and force S&P 500 inclusion — is Stoller doing what he does best: tracing a specific, checkable corporate maneuver and naming the conflict of interest (Google as both customer and ~5% owner). The arithmetic he lays out (94x revenue multiple × $11 billion engineered revenue ≈ $1 trillion; Google's ~$10 billion outlay lifting a $50 billion stake) is a serious, falsifiable allegation, and the NASDAQ-100 rule change versus the S&P's GAAP-profitability requirement is the kind of plumbing detail that gives the claim teeth. This is the part of the essay worth carrying forward, and it converges with the Noah Smith zombie piece elsewhere in this cluster on a shared theme — capital markets (Chinese banks evergreening; US markets rewarding cash-flow-negative narrative stocks) sustaining valuations and firms disconnected from underlying productivity.

The Musk-versus-Pope framing is the weaker register and reflects Stoller's characteristic move of elevating a business-mechanics story into a civilizational morality play. The essay is built on a genuine homology — that Chicago School efficiency-maximization and Silicon Valley trans-humanism both treat limits as defects to be optimized away — and the encyclical quotes ("when efficiency becomes the ultimate measure of value, human beings are tempted to see themselves as a project to be optimized") do land the parallel. But the frame does analytic work it cannot quite support: it implies the case against Musk's valuation is of a piece with a metaphysical case against transhumanism, when in fact the strongest argument here (the deal is financially fraudulent) stands entirely on its own and needs no theology. Folding the empirical claim into the spiritual one risks making a checkable fraud allegation feel like an expression of Stoller's priors about Silicon Valley hubris, which is rhetorically powerful for his audience but epistemically a downgrade.

The strongest opposing view Stoller underweights: SpaceX's valuation, however inflated, is not only narrative — Starlink is a genuinely dominant, cash-generative, strategically critical asset (he concedes this, then waves it away as "just" a telecom monopoly), and reusable launch has structurally lowered the cost of access to orbit in a way NASA and the Soviets did not, which his "fundamentally just doing what NASA achieved in the 1950s, with more efficiency" line dismisses too glibly (efficiency at that scale is the whole product). A reader persuaded by the self-dealing allegation could still reasonably conclude the company is massively overvalued rather than substantively worthless — a distinction Stoller's all-or-nothing "fables" framing blurs. The valuation-manipulation thesis is the credible spine; the trans-humanist morality tale is the persuasive packaging, and they should not be granted equal evidentiary status.

↩ Back to TOC

Energy & the Physical Economy: Powering the Boom

Converting Coal Plants to Natural Gas

Brian Potter · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary (engineering explainer), prompted by the Trump administration's recent moves to revive coal — forcing plants to stay online (a $615,000/day order in Michigan) and funding new coal construction. Brian Potter, an engineer who writes deep cost-and-mechanics explainers of the built environment, traces coal from Newcomen's 18th-century engine through its long dominance of US electricity (roughly 50% for most of the 20th century, peaking ~57% in the mid-1980s) to its decline to ~16% today, with over 200 plants shut since 2008 and around 140 converted to natural gas. He attributes the conversion wave to two coinciding forces: tightening emissions regulation — culminating in the EPA's 2011 Mercury and Air Toxics Standards (MATS), which hit coal but exempted cleaner-burning gas — and the shale-gas boom that made gas cheap and projected to stay cheap. Faced with MATS, operators could shut down, install costly emissions-control equipment (219 surviving coal plants did), or convert; converters tended to be older, smaller, inefficient peaking plants. He details four conversion strategies of rising cost and efficiency: (1) modifying the existing boiler to burn gas (cheapest, most common, but inefficient); (2) swapping in a new gas boiler; (3) "repowering" with a gas turbine plus heat-recovery steam generator for a combined-cycle plant; (4) gutting and replacing everything with a new combined-cycle plant (~a third of conversions). He concludes the heyday is past: far fewer coal plants remain, the best conversion candidates are already converted, grid-scale batteries are eroding the economics of peaking plants generally, and while AI power demand and possible future regulation could prompt some conversions, the trend is unlikely to revive meaningfully.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Potter doing exactly what he is good at — a clear, well-sourced, mechanism-first explainer that makes a policy-relevant process legible without grinding an ideological axe. The piece's value is descriptive rather than argumentative: it answers "what actually drove coal-to-gas conversion and how is it physically done," and the answer (regulation + shale economics, executed via four distinct retrofit depths) is correct, properly caveated, and tied to specific named plants (Joliet, Big Bend, the TVA Allen plant) that ground the taxonomy in real cases. The framing device — using the Trump administration's coal-revival efforts as the reason to understand why operators left coal in the first place — is a quietly effective rhetorical move, because it lets the engineering facts do the political work: the reader infers that fighting the conversion tide requires overcoming the same regulatory and cost forces that produced it, without Potter having to editorialize.

The forward-looking claim — that the conversion era is largely behind us — is the only genuinely predictive part, and it rests on three reasonable but not airtight premises: that the stock of good candidates is depleted, that batteries are undercutting peaker economics, and that AI demand and re-regulation are weak countervailing forces. The first is solid (the numbers are simply smaller now). The second is well-grounded and links to his own prior battery work. The third is where the piece is appropriately hedged but arguably underweights its own counterevidence: Potter notes coal plants slated for retirement are already staying online due to AI-driven demand and the multi-year backlog for new gas turbines, which is precisely the condition that could make converting an existing coal plant (with grid interconnection and a site already in hand) attractive relative to waiting years for a greenfield gas turbine. He acknowledges this logic and then sets it aside as probably insufficient; that's a defensible judgment, but the strongest opposing view — that turbine scarcity plus surging load could revive conversions as a faster-than-greenfield stopgap — gets somewhat less weight than the engineering details he's just laid out (interconnection reuse being a real advantage) would justify. This is a soft spot, not a flaw: the claim is a suspicion ("I suspect"), not an assertion, and the descriptive core of the piece is unaffected.

↩ Back to TOC

FERC Has a New Plan for Data Centers

Matthew Zeitlin · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's (FERC) Thursday "show cause" orders responding to an October Trump-administration tasking (via Energy Secretary Chris Wright's advance notice of proposed rulemaking) to speed large-load interconnection without raising retail electricity costs. Heatmap's Matthew Zeitlin reports that rather than issue a prescriptive national rule, FERC handed the six regional transmission organizations and independent system operators (PJM, MISO, SPP, CAISO, ISO New England, NYISO) a menu of solutions and a tight timeline to implement them region by region — an approach former chair Neil Chatterjee called "very FERC-y" and that experts say is faster than a full notice-and-comment rulemaking. The five reform categories are: clearer transmission application/study rules for large loads (with emphasis on cheaper "grid-enhancing technologies" like reconductoring and dynamic line rating); cost-recovery safeguards so data centers pay their share and residential customers aren't stuck if a promised data center never materializes; rules enabling co-location and behind-the-meter generation; "load flexibility" rules letting data centers shave peak demand to avoid costly buildouts; and rules for generation sited "proximate" to new load ("bring your own new generation"). The decision was a bipartisan 5-0 vote and drew praise from the Sierra Club and clean-energy group Advanced Energy United. Zeitlin situates it against rising prices (electricity up 5.3% nationally over the past year; New Jersey bills up ~52% over five years) and Chair Laura Swett's pointed criticism that PJM may be "too big to function," with "free-riding on other customers is not an option" as the governing principle.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is competent energy trade reporting that does its core job well: it translates a procedurally arcane FERC action into its substantive stakes (who pays for the grid buildout that AI data centers require) and correctly identifies the decision's defining feature — that FERC chose a decentralized "show cause" route over a uniform federal rule, delegating the hard design work to the regional markets. The piece is strongest as explanation of the five reform buckets, which it renders concretely with commissioners' own framing, and at flagging the politically salient throughline: the repeated insistence that large loads "pay their fair share" and that residential ratepayers be protected from stranded-cost risk if a data center fails to show up. That cost-shifting anxiety is the real news, and Zeitlin keeps it central.

The reporting is appropriately sourced (named outside experts, direct quotes from the commissioners, trade-group reactions) and does not overclaim — it explicitly notes this is "not a formal rulemaking," which is the key caveat for any reader inclined to treat it as settled policy. What the piece can't yet assess, and doesn't pretend to, is whether decentralization actually delivers the promised speed: the "very FERC-y" framing is presented as a feature (regional tailoring, faster than rulemaking), but the obvious opposing view — that punting design to six separately-motivated RTOs invites inconsistency, litigation, and delay, and that "do whatever you have to, just make it work" with a one-month turnaround may produce thin or contested rules — gets only implicit airing through Swett's own frustration with PJM's dysfunction. The bipartisan 5-0 vote and cross-spectrum praise are reported as a strength, but they also signal that the orders mostly defer the genuinely contested distributional choices (how exactly to allocate costs between data centers and households) to a later stage, which is why even clean-energy and environmental groups could applaud. For a wire-style report that's the right call; the analytical question of whether this menu-and-deadline approach holds up is properly left open rather than prejudged.

↩ Back to TOC

Trump’s War on Gigawatts

Robinson Meyer · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The Trump administration's $765 million deal paying Chicago-based developer Invenergy to surrender four offshore wind leases off New York, California, and Maine — the latest in a series of such buyouts. In this short daily brief, Heatmap's Robinson Meyer notes the deals may be legally suspect (Democratic state attorneys general have sued) but that more are expected, reading the DOJ's "continued cooperation" language as a thinly veiled racket. By Heatmap's tally the administration has now killed leases for more than 14 gigawatts of planned offshore wind — roughly 6–7 million homes' worth — over half of it bound for the strained New York/New Jersey markets, plus 3.4 GW for high-cost Maine, 4 GW for California, and 1 GW for the Carolinas. Meyer's framing is that beyond the obvious anti-wind agenda, this is an attack on the economies of power-constrained coastal states: New York faces possible summertime blackouts by decade's end, and the cancelled tristate leases were closer to operation than Invenergy's other projects. He dismisses the deal's quasi-quid-pro-quo — Invenergy agreeing to build gas or geothermal plants in Midwestern states — citing pro-wind advocate Hilary Bright that cancelling stressed-market generation and replacing it with mid-continent plants is not a one-for-one swap. He closes by flagging the political stakes in Maine and North Carolina Senate races this November.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

As a short opinionated daily brief this is proportionate and makes one genuinely sharp analytical point: that the geographic mismatch between the cancelled generation (coastal, near power-constrained load centers, near-shovel-ready) and the substitute generation (Midwestern gas/geothermal, far from the stressed grids) means the "swap" framing is misleading even on the administration's own energy-adequacy terms. That's a real argument with a clear mechanism — transmission constraints mean megawatts in Indiana don't relieve a New York summer peak — and Bright's "not one-for-one swaps" line carries it. The blackout-risk and Senate-race framing give it stakes without overreaching for a piece of this length.

The limits are those of the genre: it's advocacy-inflected, sourced primarily to a pro-wind group, and reports the cancellation tallies and home-equivalents on Heatmap's own count rather than independently verified figures (the "6 million to 7 million homes" is a standard but elastic conversion). The reading of "continued cooperation" as menacing is a rhetorical flourish, fairly labeled as the author's interpretation. The strongest opposing view — that some of these leases faced their own cost and supply-chain headwinds and that developers may have been willing sellers rather than pure coercion victims — goes unaddressed, though the active state-AG litigation lends real weight to the coercion framing. Within the bounds of a clearly editorial brief these are acceptable; the core claim about geographic non-substitutability is the load-bearing and defensible one.

↩ Back to TOC

Anthropic Is Buying Carbon Removal — But Not Clean Power

Emily Pontecorvo · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Frontier Climate's Wednesday announcement of a $915 million advance-market-commitment growth fund for carbon removal, which named Anthropic (alongside Google, Stripe, Shopify, and others) as a participating buyer of future carbon-removal offtake. Heatmap's Emily Pontecorvo uses the deal to spotlight what she frames as a gap in Anthropic's record: despite a reputation as the "ethical AI" company (traced to CEO Dario Amodei's safety focus, its AI-regulation donations, and its restrictions on surveillance/weapons use), Anthropic has published no sustainability report, made no emissions-reduction or clean-energy commitments, signed no clean-power purchase agreement, and joined no clean-energy buyers' trade group. She reports, citing market-intelligence firm Cleanview, that Anthropic's data centers have among the highest average carbon intensity of any competitor, second only to xAI — a problem set to worsen as Anthropic shifts from leasing capacity from cleaner providers (Amazon, Google) to building its own and renting from xAI's gas-turbine-powered Colossus facility (itself facing an NAACP air-pollution suit). She balances this with countervailing signals: Anthropic's July 2025 "Building AI in America" report endorsed "all of the above" power plus geothermal/nuclear and subtly criticized the administration's anti-wind/solar stance; and the company recently hired several clean-power and sustainability-reporting specialists, suggesting it's preparing for mandatory California/EU emissions disclosure. The piece closes on the central critique, voiced via Frontier's head Hannah Bebbington Valori on Robinson Meyer's podcast: carbon removal matters only as a supplement to cutting emissions, not a substitute, and buyers' "feet should be held to the fire" on decarbonizing first.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a well-reported accountability piece that lands a fair and specific critique: that buying future carbon removal while making no commitment to clean power gets the order of operations backwards, since prevention is cheaper and less resource-intensive than later extraction. The reporting is careful to be even-handed in a way the project values — it doesn't simply indict Anthropic, but documents both the absence (no PPAs, no sustainability report, no trade-group membership, high carbon intensity per Cleanview) and the offsetting evidence (the policy-report clean-energy advocacy, the cluster of clean-power and emissions-accounting hires, the apparent preparation for mandatory disclosure). That two-sided structure is the piece's strength: it presents the carbon-removal buy as genuinely ambiguous — either greenwashing-via-future-promise or the leading edge of a belated but real climate program — and marshals concrete hiring evidence for the charitable reading rather than assuming the cynical one.

The analytical core — the "moral hazard" worry that the promise of future removal licenses delayed present cuts — is the right frame, and Pontecorvo sources it to a credible and notably non-partisan voice (Frontier's own head declining to indict Anthropic specifically while affirming that removal is "not a get-out-of-jail-free card"). The factual claims that matter are attributed (the carbon-intensity ranking to Cleanview; the hires to LinkedIn/job listings; the policy positions quoted directly from Anthropic's report), and the one unverifiable element — Anthropic "did not respond to my inquiry" — is disclosed. There is mild editorial coloring in the framing (the opening "$965 billion ... built on pirated model training material" and "good-coded" are loaded), but these are flagged as the author's voice and don't distort the substantive case.

The strongest opposing view, which the piece raises and partly credits via Searchlight Institute's Jane Flegal, is that individual corporate carbon-footprint accounting is the wrong unit of analysis entirely — that a company does more good financing a high-leverage geothermal plant, transmission line, or grid transformer than by chasing a clean number on its own ledger, and that Anthropic's leased-capacity-from-clean-providers history plus its policy advocacy may already constitute more real-world leverage than a symbolic PPA. Pontecorvo gives this its due rather than dismissing it, which keeps the piece honest: the critique is not "Anthropic is uniquely bad" but "for a company that markets itself on ethics, the silence and the high carbon intensity are a conspicuous and so-far-unexplained gap." That is a defensible, well-evidenced claim, appropriately hedged against the genuine uncertainty about whether the recent hires signal a turn.

↩ Back to TOC

The Old Order Cracks: Gerontocracy, the Left's Decline, and Realignment

What Not to Do About Gerontocracy

Declan Leary · 2026-06-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Samuel Moyn's book Gerontocracy in America: How the Old Are Hoarding Power and Wealth—and What to Do About It (Yale law/history professor; diagnoses an aging elite cutting the young off from political, financial, and institutional power). Leary, reviewing for the post-liberal magazine Commonplace, concedes Moyn's core diagnosis — an aging elite jealously clutching power, a tax system tilted toward seniors, an unsustainable Social Security (now 22.5% of the federal budget, with the worker-to-beneficiary ratio fallen from 40:1 in 1940 to 2.7:1) — but argues Moyn misnames the disease. The boomer order is not "conservative" or a traditional gerontocracy passing down inherited wisdom; it is a generation formed by 1960s radicalism that refuses to accept it has aged, denying decline and mortality. Leary's central counter-charge is that Moyn's preferred remedies (socialist economics, identity-based representation quotas, abolishing the Senate and Electoral College, age limits on voting and political donations, capacity-testing for workers, socialized elder care funded by higher taxes) are extreme, anti-constitutional, and "we already tried that" — handing power to a revolutionary young generation is what produced the boomers in the first place. He counterposes his own program: "remigration" (deporting ~50 million people he frames as a "foreign invasion force"), means-testing Social Security and raising the retirement age, a stronger targeted homestead exemption, and restoring familial obligation against welfare-state substitution. But the analysis identifies that Leary's central causal claim — that mass immigration and offshoring are "the most obvious cause" of young Americans' labor-market troubles, and that deporting 50 million people is the "humane" housing fix — is asserted rather than argued, and his framing of immigrants as an "invasion force" with "less right" to housing does the load-bearing work his economics cannot.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a polemical book review functioning as a Commonplace house-manifesto: it takes a left-liberal author's data, concedes the parts that fit a post-liberal fiscal critique (Social Security's unsustainability, senior tax carveouts, the property-tax tilt toward incumbent owners), and then weaponizes Moyn's own immoderation to discredit the whole program. The strongest move is the reframing of boomer rule as anti-traditional rather than conservative — "a collective fantasy of perpetual youth rooted in the formative experience of 1960s radicalism." This is a genuinely sharp observation: a gerontocracy that denies its own age behaves nothing like the elder-councils of myth, and Moyn's "delayed housing wealth tax levied on death" does indeed assume boomers accept mortality and care about heirs. Leary also lands the catch that Moyn, a Yale law professor, pretends ignorance of federalism when he treats the Senate's small-state weighting as mere "dilution of voting power" rather than a designed feature — and the catch that Moyn quotes Houellebecq's image of a neglected elder in a state facility and concludes the problem is too little welfare state, not too much.

The structural problem is that Leary's own program rests on assertions doing the work of arguments. "Mass migration is the most obvious cause of young Americans' challenges in the labor market, followed closely by offshoring" is stated three times in anaphora ("Never mind that…") without a single citation, even as he cites Moyn's numbers fastidiously elsewhere. The economics literature on immigration's wage effects is genuinely contested, but "most obvious cause" is a confidence the evidence does not license; the bigger drivers of youth labor-market difficulty (credential inflation, healthcare-cost crowd-out, the collapse of entry-level rungs, housing-supply restriction that Leary himself elsewhere blames on zoning) get displaced onto migration because migration is the venue's preferred villain. The "remigration" proposal — deport "roughly 50 million" people as the "humane" way to relieve housing pressure — is presented as obviously preferable to taxing boomers, but the framing ("foreign invasion force," "people who don't belong here," "far less right" to housing) is what carries it: strip the invasion metaphor and you are left with a claim that mass deportation is cheaper and gentler than means-testing, which is not defended on the merits. This is the load-bearing rhetorical substitution flagged in the summary.

Read against Commonplace's pattern (pro-family, communitarian-conservative, hostile to finance capitalism and to managerial liberalism), the review is internally coherent: the family-breakdown thesis ("the elderly's reliance on federal welfare programs is a direct replacement for what once had been familial obligations… 30 million women terrified of being known as 'grandma'") is the real Commonplace argument, and it is the part Leary spends least space defending. The line is rhetorically vivid but causally thin — it asserts that welfare crowded out family rather than that family decline (driven by many forces) created demand for welfare, and the direction of causation is exactly the kind of question Moyn-style empiricism would press. The strongest opposing view Leary doesn't engage: Moyn's actual point may be that the cohort is incidental and the structure (asset appreciation captured by whoever happens to be old at the right moment, plus age-skewed political turnout) would reproduce itself regardless of generational character — in which case Leary's "they refuse to age" psychology is a colorful epiphenomenon, not a refutation, and means-testing plus retirement-age adjustment (which Leary endorses) are closer to Moyn's structural remedies than the review's combative tone admits. The two writers may agree on more fiscal mechanics than the polemic lets on; the real divide is over migration and family, where Leary argues least and asserts most.

↩ Back to TOC

The Long, Slow Death of Social Democracy

Ruy Teixeira · 2026-06-14 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The historical question of why mid-century social democracy (the postwar Keynesian, working-class-rooted Left) declined, and what replaced it — written for the post-liberal magazine Commonplace. Teixeira, a center-left "common-sense" Democrat and longtime critic of his party's leftward cultural turn, offers a three-factor structural explanation. First, the economic model failed: the 1973 collapse of Bretton Woods and the OPEC oil shock produced stagflation that Keynesianism couldn't answer, and Milton Friedman's monetarist counterrevolution ("neoliberalism") filled the vacuum and was eventually accepted even by the Left. Second, the base shrank: the industrial working class fell from 40–50% of Western workforces to under 25%, with U.S. industrial employment now back to its 1820 share, and the remaining workers defected to right-populist parties. Third, social democracy's influence within the Left collapsed as educated professionals (college attainment rose from 5% of U.S. adults in 1940 to 39% in 2024) replaced workers as the activist core. The product is what Piketty called the "Brahmin Left" — parties dominated by highly educated elites who still favor redistribution but have shifted priorities to four sociocultural causes Teixeira says the working class never wanted: "neo-racialism" (color-conscious remedies replacing colorblind equal-opportunity), mass immigration treated as an unalloyed good, gender ideology (he asserts flatly that "sex is a binary" and "transwomen are not women"), and climate catastrophism (which he says working-class voters rightly rank as a third-tier issue). He cites the Deciding to Win report's finding that "growth" fell 32% in Democratic platforms since 2012 while "climate" rose 150%, "LGBTQ" 1,044%, and "equity" 766%, and closes by doubting social democracy can be revived from the Left, hinting a worker-oriented dynamic-capitalism politics may have to come from elsewhere.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a polished restatement of Teixeira's signature thesis (the argument of his and John Judis's recent work, and of his Liberal Patriot Substack), tailored for Commonplace's post-liberal, pro-worker, anti-managerial-class audience — which is itself notable, since Teixeira is a registered Democrat publishing a Democratic-Party autopsy in a magazine of the populist right, a placement that does some of the piece's rhetorical work. The historical-structural core (factors one and two) is the strongest and least contestable part: the stagflation-to-neoliberalism story and the deindustrialization-of-the-base story are well-documented, and the framing of all three factors as mutually reinforcing — model failure, base shrinkage, and intra-Left displacement happening together — is genuinely illuminating because it resists the lazier monocausal stories (it wasn't just that the Left went woke, nor just that the economy changed; the economic shift hollowed the base, which changed who staffed the party, which changed the priorities). The Piketty "Brahmin Left" frame is the right analytical anchor: parties of the educated that retain redistribution-on-paper while shedding the predistribution (better market outcomes for workers) that the old social democracy centered.

The piece's evidentiary character changes sharply between its first half and its second. The economic history is cited and quantified; the four-issue cultural indictment is asserted with much thinner support and considerable editorializing, and this is where critique-mode should match argument-mode. Teixeira presents contested normative claims as settled fact — most conspicuously "in reality, sex is a binary… transwomen are not women… males who choose a different lifestyle," delivered as a knockdown rather than an argument, with a single citation. Whatever one's view, this is the register of advocacy, not the structural analysis the first half promised, and it imports a culture-war conclusion into what is framed as social science. The immigration section similarly slides from the defensible claim (the Left erred by treating any concern about migration as racism, and there are real distributional and rule-of-law reasons working-class voters object) to a numbered list of "fundamentals" that are presented as self-evident "realities" rather than empirically litigated propositions — the claim that "too much immigration, particularly low-skill immigration" has "real" negative effects on workers is contested in the labor economics literature, and stating it as a "reality… ignored by the Brahmin Left" assumes the answer to a live empirical dispute.

The deeper structural weakness is a selection effect Teixeira doesn't address: he attributes the cultural turn entirely to the supply side (radical professional-class activists capturing the parties) and treats working-class preferences as a fixed, commonsense baseline the elite betrayed. But his own data cut against the tidiness of "none of these were supported by the working class" — the working class is not monolithic on race or immigration, public opinion on these issues moved over time rather than staying fixed, and some of the realignment he describes is voters sorting by education across all issues, not professionals imposing alien priorities on an unwilling base. The "Brahmin Left vs. authentic worker" binary is rhetorically powerful but flattens a messier reality in which the working-class coalition was always cross-pressured and the professional class's rise is partly an effect of the same deindustrialization that shrank the base, not simply a hostile takeover. The strongest opposing view he doesn't engage: a defender of the contemporary Left would argue that the economic abandonment came first and the cultural focus is a symptom, not the cause — that once neoliberalism foreclosed the predistributive economics (a point Teixeira himself makes), sociocultural commitments were what remained available to a party that had accepted it couldn't deliver materially. On that reading, the cultural turn is the consequence of the economic surrender Teixeira documents in his first half, which would make his fourth-quarter culture-war list less an independent cause of social democracy's death than an epitaph for a project already killed by the forces in his first three factors. The Deciding to Win word-frequency data is striking but is a measure of rhetorical salience, not of policy substance or of what actually lost elections; "growth" appearing 32% less often is consistent with several stories, only one of which is the one Teixeira tells.

↩ Back to TOC

What Obama Meant

Eboo Patel · 2026-06-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The June 2026 opening of the Barack Obama Presidential Center in Chicago, prompting a reckoning with what Obama meant. Eboo Patel (founder of Interfaith America, former Obama faith advisor, Persuasion contributing writer) defines "Obama-ism" as the belief that the social sector's work—talent development, character formation, and civic bridgebuilding across difference—could be elevated into a governing philosophy, rooted in Obama's own background as a community organizer and the early-2000s "golden era" of admired nonprofits (Teach for America, KIPP, City Year, Harlem Children's Zone). He argues the Obama narrative cast America as a story of expanding freedom (freedom-fighters defeating repression) and paired social investment with personal-responsibility exhortation. Patel's central claim is that this philosophy collapsed when, after Ta-Nehisi Coates's 2014 "The Case for Reparations," the social sector adopted a rival narrative—America as defined by the legacy of slavery—not as a critique of Obama but as a replacement: "the clenched fist replaced the extended hand." He contends this Coatesian frame is empirically incomplete (it ignores real, stunning if uneven minority progress—Coates's own rise being a case), psychologically damaging (victimhood as bad for mental health and creating perverse incentives to perform failure), and politically self-defeating, alienating the "deaths of despair" white working class cast as villains. His provocative throughline: the alternative to Obama-ism "turned out to be Donald Trump," and right-wing movements could not have won two elections "had the social sector not cannibalized itself." He closes optimistically—reporting that the nonprofit world is now retreating from cancellation culture and identity obsession (partly sobered by minorities voting for Trump in 2024), making the Obama Center's opening a moment to revitalize, not memorialize. But the analysis rests on a contested monocausal claim—that the social sector's ideological turn was a primary cause of Trump's rise—asserted rather than demonstrated.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a characteristic Patel essay—part memoir, part movement-history, part argument—and its real strength is the firsthand vantage: he was in the room (literally, a 25-person condo gathering in 2004), and his account of how a specific institutional milieu (the Obama-era social sector) experienced its own ideological turn from "Yes, we can" optimism to "dismantle the system" has the texture of insider testimony rather than punditry. The sharpest analytic observation is the boomerang: "the first system that your junior staff will try to disrupt is the institution that employs them," which names a real and well-documented dynamic of 2020-era nonprofit turmoil. As intellectual history of a sector, the piece is illuminating.

The fundamental weakness is causal overreach, and it's load-bearing for the essay's most memorable claim. Patel argues the social sector's self-cannibalization was a necessary condition for Trump's two victories ("I don't think those right-wing elements would have had the opportunity... had the social sector not cannibalized itself"). This is a sweeping monocausal counterfactual about presidential elections resting on a single sector's internal culture wars—a sector most American voters have no contact with. The mechanism connecting nonprofit HR practices to a national electoral realignment is never specified; it's asserted with the confidence of a conclusion the author reached before the evidence. The same overreach inflects his treatment of Coates: Patel concedes "what Coates wrote was true—systemic racism is very, very real," then proceeds to treat the reception of Coates (institutions adopting a victim-narrative) as if it were Coates's argument, eliding the gap between a magazine essay and a sector's organizational drift. The strongest opposing view is that the causation largely runs the other way—that economic dislocation, partisan media, and the structural features of the post-2008 economy drove both the right's rise and the left's radicalization, with the social sector a downstream symptom rather than an upstream cause.

There's also a self-flattering structure worth flagging: the narrative restores Patel's own milieu (the optimistic, bridgebuilding, faith-and-service social sector) to the role of protagonist and casts its ideological rivals as the agents of national catastrophe, then reports that the rivals are now in retreat and his side is vindicated. That arc is emotionally satisfying and may even be partly correct, but it should be read as the argument of a participant with a stake in which narrative wins, not as disinterested history. Because the central causal claim is asserted rather than demonstrated and is the piece's signature takeaway, downstream readers should treat "the woke turn caused Trump" as Patel's contestable thesis, not an established finding.

↩ Back to TOC

A tombstone for Obamaism

Ryan Zickgraf · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the opening of the Barack Obama Presidential Center on Chicago's South Side (Juneteenth 2026). Ryan Zickgraf (a Chicago-based left-leaning writer) uses the widely panned 225-foot windowless granite tower — mocked as "the Death Star," a litter box, and by Trump as a garbage can — to argue it best resembles a mausoleum for "Obamaism," which he defines as "a politics after politics": the belief that if institutions speak pleasant bromides in reassuring voices, "the arc of the moral universe" will bend on its own. He contends Obama campaigned in 2008 on a sharp break from the Bush-Clinton neoliberal consensus ("change you can believe in") but then spent his presidency arguing structural change was impossible against Republican opposition, offering instead "Obama as symbol" — the first Black president as proof America had already improved by recognizing him. Zickgraf finds Trump's planned Miami library (gilded Oval Office, golden statue, a plane in the lobby) more honest because it makes power look like power, whereas the Obama Center "launders" power through the institutions Democrats still control — museums, foundations, public art, nonprofits, celebrity culture — and conceals itself in inclusive first-person-plural language ("the single most powerful word in our democracy is the word we"). He notes it is the first "presidential library" since FDR's not to actually house the presidential archive (Obama's records sit in a federal warehouse or online, managed by the private Obama Foundation), and reads Obama's insistence on an "iconic" vertical tower — overriding the architects' restrained horizontal design — as barely concealed ego. The political claim: with Trump restored, Obamacare fraying, and Democrats reduced to relying on courts to preserve past wins, Obama's narrative-over-structure philosophy ("America's problems are fundamentally narrative rather than structural") has been falsified by the return of material politics, including Biden's post-COVID economic restructuring.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a left critique of Obama, not a right one, and that is what gives it bite: Zickgraf's complaint is not that Obama was too radical but that he substituted symbolism and cultural ascendance for the structural-economic change he'd promised — "the thin gruel of himself." The building-as-argument conceit is genuinely well-executed: the windowless tower the architects wanted low and horizontal but Obama wanted "iconic," the absent archive, the first-person-plural inscriptions that universalize what is in fact a monument to one man. The architectural reporting (Tod Williams and Billie Tsien's restrained original proposal, Tsien's quote that Obama "made a few not so good suggestions," the contrast with George Lucas's rejected Museum of Narrative Art on the same lakefront) is doing real evidentiary work rather than decoration, and the Lucas parallel — Chicago refused to surrender public space to "one man's mythmaking" only to accept "a more subtle form of fiction" — is the essay's sharpest single move.

The structural weakness is that the central thesis leans hard on the building to bear an argument about a presidency, and the building does not actually prove the political claim. That Obama wanted an iconic tower is consistent with vanity but also with wanting a durable civic landmark; the leap from "the architecture is egotistical" to "Obamaism was a substantive political failure" is asserted by juxtaposition rather than demonstrated. The strongest version of the political critique — that Obama oversold transformation in 2008 and then governed within tight constraints, leaving Democrats with cultural capital but thin material gains — is defensible, but Zickgraf states it as settled rather than arguing it against the obvious rejoinder: that the structural constraints (a filibustering Senate, the Tea Party, the courts) were real, not chosen, and that the ACA, Dodd-Frank, and the stimulus were exactly the kind of structural change he says Obama abandoned. By treating "massive structural change was impossible" as Obama's excuse rather than partly his accurate read of the votes he had, the essay does to Obama what it accuses Obama of doing to America — preferring the satisfying narrative (vanity, cowardice) to the messier structural account (constraint).

The piece is also doing genre work that the reader should clock: it is a "death of a paradigm" essay, and such essays are most persuasive when the corpse is freshest. Zickgraf's strongest empirical anchor is real — Trump restored, Obamacare fraying, Democrats litigating to preserve past wins, and even a Democratic president (Biden) governing through material economic restructuring rather than Obama-style narrative — which genuinely does suggest the symbolic mode has lost purchase. That makes the essay's verdict on the mode more convincing than its verdict on the man; the closing line ("Cemeteries can be a pleasant place to relax") nicely keeps the register ambivalent rather than triumphalist. Not fundamentally flawed, but readers should separate the persuasive cultural-criticism layer from the under-argued claim that the architecture settles the political ledger.

↩ Back to TOC

Socialism Is on the March

John Fund · 2026-06-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The post-2025 wave of DSA electoral wins (NYC, Seattle, DC mayoralties) and the rise of Mayor Zohran Mamdani, in the context of socialism's mainstreaming on the Democratic left. John Fund, National Review's national-affairs reporter, marshals evidence that the Democratic Socialists of America (DSA) is ascendant: DSA members now hold mayoralties in New York City, Seattle, and Washington DC, with Nithya Raman challenging Karen Bass in Los Angeles, and CNN analyst Harry Enten putting the DSA's net favorability among Democrats (+17) above that of congressional Democrats (+4). He casts NYC Mayor Mamdani as the group's "boss," endorsing primary challengers against Democratic incumbents and treating Tuesday's primaries as the start of a 2028 presidential race (with AOC floated). Fund's substantive charge is radicalization: he cites the DSA's "Red Rabbits Security Commission" street-security apparatus (reported in City Journal), an updated platform calling to abolish the Senate, defund the Pentagon, cut police budgets "annually to zero," transfer major corporations to public ownership, and subordinate the president and Supreme Court to Congress, plus a statement celebrating "the Cuban Revolution." He contrasts this with the DSA's 1982 founder Michael Harrington, who meant to keep it a "patriotic institution" working within the Democratic Party, and frames the modern DSA as a would-be Tammany Hall machine that has exploited the failure of both liberals and Republicans to govern cities — leaving socialism poised to become "the secular religion" of disillusioned young voters.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is an opinion-reportage hybrid in NR's standard adversarial-profile mode: a real trend (DSA electoral momentum, Mamdani's coalition-building) documented with checkable specifics, packaged inside an alarm frame. The factual spine is largely verifiable — the mayoral wins, Enten's favorability figures, the platform planks, the named challengers and endorsements — and to Fund's credit he sources the more contested claims (City Journal/Stu Smith for the Red Rabbits, Politico/Basil Smikle and Reynoso for the "machine" characterization). The platform quotations (Senate abolition, police budgets to zero, public ownership of major corporations, Cuba statement) are the load-bearing evidence for the radicalism charge and are presented as direct citations the reader could confirm against DSA documents.

The analytic move worth scrutinizing is the selection and framing rather than the facts. Fund builds the case by stacking the platform's maximalist planks and the most provocative statements (the Cuba "Long live the Cuban Revolution" line, juxtaposed pointedly with Cuba's own market reforms) to establish "ugly" radicalism, then reaches for the Tammany Hall analogy to import connotations of corruption and authoritarianism. The Tammany frame is doing rhetorical work the evidence doesn't fully earn: the Smikle/Reynoso "machine" quotes describe organizational discipline and patronage-style power, not the graft Plunkitt's "honest graft" boast evokes — Fund borrows the corruption association by adjacency. The strongest part of his argument is actually the structural concession near the end: that the DSA's rise is downstream of governance failure by both liberals and Republicans in cities. That diagnosis (a vacuum-filling account) is more persuasive than the radicalism-cataloging because it explains the +17 favorability without relying on the reader sharing his alarm.

The strongest opposing view is that platform maximalism is a poor predictor of governing behavior — activist platforms routinely contain planks the elected officials neither campaign on nor implement, and Mamdani-style candidates have generally run on affordability/material-conditions messaging ("managing decline instead of delivering material change"), not Senate abolition. Fund's piece cannot distinguish between "the DSA's foot soldiers believe X" and "DSA mayors will govern toward X," and treats the platform as a manifesto of intent. For the roundup, this is a useful right-of-center read on the Mamdani-era left and pairs naturally with any progressive or contrarian-left coverage of the same wins; its facts are reliable, its causal claim (governance-failure vacuum) is its real insight, and its threat framing should be read as one orientation's interpretation rather than established forecast.

↩ Back to TOC

Against the “Positive Good” theory of Platner

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-15 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The "Positive Good theory of Platnerism" advanced by left commentators Ken Klippenstein ("end of smoothgroin politicians") and Matt Stoller ("rejection of Dem H.R. lady politics") about Graham Platner, a charismatic Maine Democratic Senate candidate dogged by a questionable tattoo, extramarital sexting, and womanizing allegations who beat Gov. Janet Mills in the June 2026 primary and faces Susan Collins. Yglesias first issues a "disclaimer about Maine": the race is a bad proxy for the working-class-realignment debate everyone wants to have, because Maine is a D+14-to-D+16 state Harris won and Platner's home turf (Hancock County, Bar Harbor, Blue Hill) is rural-but-liberal — he only needs Harris voters, not Trump crossovers. He concedes the Positive Good theory is "in the neighborhood of something correct": Democrats should moderate on culture, and economic-left Democrats need even more cultural moderation than across-the-board moderates. But the theory's actual content — that personal misconduct inconsistent with stated progressive values somehow signals authenticity — is a substitute for the concrete policy moderation (à la Mary Peltola on resources, Platner himself on guns) that Stoller/Klippenstein won't actually endorse. Yglesias invokes Cal Cunningham's failed 2020 North Carolina Senate run (affair-tanked) as a disproof, and warns the party itself traffics in a "low-effort version" of the same magical thinking (a Newsom with good hair, a tattooed veteran) hoping aesthetics will paper over substantive disagreements. His prescription: Platner should "normalize" his campaign, distance himself from factionalists leveraging him, and run as "Mr. Normie Dem."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias doubling down on popularism (win by being substantively normal, not aesthetically edgy) while drawing a genuinely sharp distinction that elevates the piece above standard primary-discourse. The central move — separating policy moderation from persona moderation, then showing the Positive Good theory smuggles in persona as a fake substitute for policy — is analytically clean and lands. The Cunningham example is the load-bearing empirical anchor: a 2020 NC Democrat whose extramarital-affair scandal coincided with a winnable-seat loss, offered as the natural experiment for "scandal-as-authenticity," and it does real work because it's the closest available test of the exact claim. The strongest part is the meta-observation that the establishment runs its own degraded version of the same fallacy (casting for the right-looking candidate rather than fixing the substance), which generalizes the critique beyond two named lefties.

The structural weakness Yglesias half-acknowledges but doesn't resolve: the "disclaimer about Maine" concedes that Platner faces none of the conditions (red-state, crossover-dependent) that would make the realignment argument testable, which means the entire essay is litigating a theory on a case Yglesias admits is the wrong case. He wants it both ways — the race "has no broader implications for political strategy" and it's worth a full essay on national strategic theory. The honest version is that he's using Platner as an occasion to relitigate the Stoller/Klippenstein wing, which is fine, but it makes the "needed disclaimer" feel like an alibi rather than a constraint on his conclusions.

The strongest opposing view, which Yglesias gestures at via the people who reached out to him: the tattoo/sexting fight may be a proxy for a real disagreement about whether the party's gatekeeping norms (the "H.R. lady politics" Stoller clumsily names) themselves repel the working-class men Democrats have lost — in which case Platner's transgressiveness signals a willingness to defy those norms that is substantively legible to voters, not merely aesthetic. Yglesias dismisses this by noting Platner "adheres to orthodox progressive views," but that somewhat begs the question of whether voters read affect or read position papers. His own popularist corpus elsewhere leans heavily on the claim that voters read vibes, which sits in tension with the demand here that Platnerism must cash out in roll-call votes to count.

↩ Back to TOC

Like All Good Things, Sports Are Owned by the Rich Now

Freddie deBoer · 2026-06-15 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The New York Knicks winning the NBA championship (their first since 1973), and the spectacle of celebrity/billionaire courtside attendance during the finals. Freddie deBoer (Marxist cultural critic) opens by conceding the Knicks' title is a genuine gift to long-suffering fans, then pivots to what depressed him about watching: ESPN cutting away from the game to billionaires and celebrities (Taylor Swift, the Haim sisters, Jimmy Fallon) "mugging for the cameras," while courtside seats cost more than a year at the most expensive universities and the cheapest finals seats ran over $4,000—roughly 8% of US median annual income for one bad seat at one game. He briefly critiques the basketball itself (ugly, three-happy "slop," with Spurs star Victor Wembanyama lacking a real offensive game), but the essay's spine is a structural-inequality argument: there are "no seats for ordinary people anymore," literally and metaphorically. He generalizes—"All good things, eventually, go to the rich. Not most good things. All of them, given enough time"—naming the good seats, neighborhoods, colleges, doctors, lawyers, quiet, and air. New York City is doing to its residents what the Garden does to fans: the neighborhoods with "soul" sanded into "amenities for people who could live anywhere," the city becoming "a very expensive museum of the thing it used to be" (he notes NYC has lost 200,000 Black residents this century; cites his father living in the 1970s West Village on an NEA salary). He frames this as capitalism functioning as designed—inequality compounding because "money buys the rules that protect the moneyed," a political system responsive only to donors—with no small-bore policy fix available. His closing cultural observation: the rich have abandoned even the old hypocrisy of noblesse oblige; where wealth once "playacted feeling sheepish," courtside celebrities now wave for the camera, and "being smirked at from the good seats stings worse than being ignored." He ends: "Enjoy the parade, but watch from the curb. It's the only spot they left us."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is deBoer in his elegiac-polemic mode, and as a piece of writing it's among his more controlled—the structural argument is genuinely braided through the sports occasion rather than pasted onto it, with the courtside camera as a recurring concrete image that earns the abstraction. The strongest and most original observation is the closing one about the death of noblesse oblige: the claim that the meaningful shift isn't that the rich have the best seats (they always did) but that they no longer perform any shame about it—"the old hypocrisy... was at least an admission that privilege owed something to everyone else." That's a real cultural-sociological point about a change in the self-presentation of wealth, and it's sharper than the standard inequality lament because it identifies a specific, falsifiable shift (visible flaunting replacing performed restraint) rather than just "inequality is bad."

The central analytical weakness is the totalizing claim that does the most rhetorical work: "ALL good things, given enough time, go to the rich." Stated as a law it's both unfalsifiable and false on its own terms—many goods have broadened access over time (the very TV broadcast deBoer watches the game on, recorded music, the internet, vaccines), and the essay's own counterexample undercuts it: the championship itself, deBoer repeatedly concedes, brought real joy "to a lot of poor people." He's describing a real phenomenon (positional/scarce goods—courtside seats, prime real estate—concentrating with wealth as inequality rises) but inflates it into a universal law that the evidence doesn't support. The honest version is narrower and still damning: rivalrous, status-laden, location-fixed goods flow to the rich, which is most of what makes a place feel like itself. The universal phrasing is the kind of mood-driven overstatement the reader should discount.

The piece also makes a move worth flagging for downstream readers: it presents a sweeping diagnosis ("there's no obvious way out... you can't un-ring this bell with small-bore policy") as settled, which forecloses the actual policy disagreements (housing supply, ticketing regulation, antitrust on secondary markets) that the inequality it describes is in fact subject to. The strongest opposing view isn't that inequality is fine—it's that deBoer's fatalism is itself a choice that conveniently exempts the analysis from engaging concrete, contested remedies (e.g., the NYC affordability debate is precisely about whether the bell can be partly un-rung via supply). Read against his pattern, the class-first lens and the romantic-but-self-aware invocation of his father's bohemian 1970s New York are signature deBoer; the essay is most valuable as cultural criticism (the noblesse-oblige death, the "seen being there" performance economy) and least reliable as social science (the universal-law claim and the no-exit fatalism). Not fundamentally flawed—the core observation about positional-good concentration is sound—but the "ALL good things" framing is an overgeneralization the piece's own concessions refute.

↩ Back to TOC

How Trump conquered the Republican Party

Nate Silver · 2026-06-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Political scientist Seth Masket's new book The Elephants in the Room: How Trump Voters Seized the Party from Republican Leaders, which Silver discusses in a Substack Live interview. Nate Silver (data journalist, founder of FiveThirtyEight, now independent) frames the piece as a post-mortem on what he calls "the biggest mistake of my career"—dismissing Trump's 2016 nomination chances. His diagnosis is methodological: he over-extrapolated from the 2012 cycle (where insurgents like Cain, Santorum, and Gingrich surged then faded as the establishment's Romney prevailed) and over-trusted the academic "Party Decides" model, which holds that party elites, signaled through endorsements, ultimately control nominations. Silver argues he violated his own usual discipline—avoid declaring "iron laws of politics" from small samples (the modern primary system only dates to the 1972 McGovern-Fraser reforms) and never lean on a single indicator like endorsements. He concedes 2012 wasn't decisive (Santorum won 11 contests) and that he failed to ask "what if a populist 10x more charismatic than Santorum comes along?" Drawing on Masket's thesis, he credits Republican voters: the establishment lost credibility after McCain and Romney lost, offered stale choices (Jeb!), and zigged center on immigration while Trump zagged. But he adds that Trump also didn't play by the rules—breaking the party's loss-learning mechanism by falsely claiming 2020 was stolen, and behaving like a "mob boss" via threats and coercion (with Republicans sometimes fearing for their physical safety)—making the GOP a "deeply unhealthy party." He notes that 2020 ironically vindicated "The Party Decides" (Democratic elites rallying behind Biden) and flags the open question of whether Democratic voters are now similarly estranging from their own establishment.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Silver in a reflective, low-stakes register—essentially a teaser/context-setter for a podcast interview—and its value is mostly methodological rather than newsy. The genuinely useful content is the epistemological self-critique, which is more honest than most pundits manage: he names the specific failure modes (over-fitting to a small sample, treating a stylized narrative as a law, leaning on a single indicator) and these are transferable lessons, not just confession. The observation that the entire modern nomination system is a post-1972 invention with only a handful of cycles to learn from is a real and underrated point about the limits of political-science generalization, and it's the kind of calibration thinking the roundup values.

The analytical tension Silver gestures at but doesn't resolve is the dual causation: he credits both bottom-up voter rebellion (Masket's "voters seized the party") and top-down rule-breaking by Trump (the "mob boss" coercion, the stolen-election lie that broke the party's learning mechanism). These are somewhat different theories of how Trump consolidated control—one emphasizing democratic legitimacy (voters wanted this) and one emphasizing intimidation (voters and officials were coerced)—and the piece holds both without adjudicating their relative weight. That's defensible in a short framing post, but it leaves the strongest version of each unexamined: if voters genuinely seized the party, the "deeply unhealthy" verdict sits awkwardly with the populist-legitimacy story; if coercion was decisive, the "voters did this" framing understates the role of fear.

Read against Silver's pattern, the most interesting thread is the closing pivot to the Democrats—whether their base is now estranging from the establishment the way the GOP's did pre-Trump. That's the carry-forward-worthy question buried at the end: Silver is implicitly asking whether "The Party Decides" is failing on the left too, which would matter far more going forward than re-litigating 2016. The piece doesn't develop it, but it's the live analytical hook. Note this is largely a pointer to the Masket interview rather than a standalone argument, so its claims are appropriately modest; the substance is the methodological humility, which is real, and the unresolved voters-vs-coercion question, which is the piece's most interesting unfinished business.

↩ Back to TOC

The Wonk Agenda: Abundance, Asset-Building, and Policy Design

How to fix transit construction in America

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The chronic cost bloat of American transit construction; a Slow Boring/Institute for Progress (IFP) collaboration producing the "Transit Abundance Playbook," a menu of concrete reform proposals. Yglesias's intro reframes the diagnosis he himself once held wrongly: the U.S. doesn't under-fund transit, it builds very little transit per dollar spent, so improving cost-effectiveness gets more built without more spending and strengthens the political case for spending. The body, by Poff-Webster and Datta, documents the decline — the U.S. now leads the world in per-mile transit costs (NYC's East Side Access at $11.2B over 24 years as the most expensive project ever per mile; California HSR ballooning from $33B to $126B while China built 30,000 miles of high-speed rail) — and attributes it to overdesign/customization, poor planning and procurement, too many veto points, burdensome permitting, and anemic state capacity. The playbook compiles 16 named proposals across six categories: phased milestone-based planning grants (Eric Goldwyn); best-value rather than lowest-bid procurement and itemized component-cost databases as in Italy/Spain (Anthony Potts, Alon Levy); adopting European cross-passageway and review standards (Brian Potter, Jamey Tesler); a single-decision-maker "Conference of Services" model to end utility/local extraction (Anonymous); fast-tracking voter-approved projects past redundant environmental review (Hayden Clarkin); reforming the 1964 Urban Mass Transportation Act provision that blocks subway automation (Andrew Miller); and rebuilding in-house agency capacity against consultant dependence (Paul Lewis). The framing argues cost-effectiveness is non-polarizing — appealing to transit advocates, fiscal conservatives, environmentalists, and drivers alike.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias on his strongest and most characteristic ground (supply-side/abundance institutionalism), but the substance is largely a curated guest compilation rather than his own argument, so the analytical weight sits in the framing and in the playbook's diagnostic premise. That premise — that the binding constraint is cost-per-mile, not aggregate willingness to spend — is well-evidenced (the East Side Access and California HSR figures are real and damning) and is the correct frame; the Alon Levy line of research on comparative construction costs, which underlies much of this, has held up across years of scrutiny and is the intellectual backbone the playbook builds on.

The genuine strength is that the proposals are mechanism-specific rather than sloganeering: "adopt the NFPA-vs-European cross-passage standard," "require itemized bidding against a component database," "amend the 1964 UMTA labor-review provision to guarantee protections without a veto process." These are the kind of unglamorous, legible levers that the "American state capacity" literature keeps identifying, and assembling them into a single menu with named authors is real value. The non-polarizing pitch (everyone wins from cost discipline) is also Yglesias's recurring popularist tic applied to policy design.

The structural limitation, which the piece doesn't confront, is the politics-of-veto-points problem it diagnoses but then mostly proposes to engineer around. Nearly every item — single decision-maker over utilities and localities, exempting voter-approved projects from environmental review, automating away unionized operator jobs, best-value over lowest-bid — concentrates discretion and strips a constituency of leverage (local governments, environmental litigants, transit unions, small contractors). Those veto points exist because organized interests defend them, so a "menu" of technocratic fixes understates that each one is a distributive fight, not a Pareto improvement.
