The strongest opposing view: the same accountability mechanisms (competitive low-bid, environmental review, labor review, multi-party negotiation) that inflate costs also constrain capture and protect workers and communities, and a regime optimized purely for build-speed-and-cost can transfer the savings into worse labor outcomes, weaker environmental scrutiny, and consolidated power for whoever holds the "single decision-maker" seat. The playbook treats state capacity as a neutral good; whether concentrated capacity is well-used depends on who controls it, which the "non-polarizing" framing elides.

↩ Back to TOC

The real problem with “gifted” education

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Katie Arnold-Ratliff's New York Magazine cover story arguing there's either no such thing as a "gifted" child or no reliable way to identify one; and the broader politics of NYC's gifted-and-talented (G&T) programs. Yglesias first rejects Arnold-Ratliff's headline claim as "staggeringly wrong": he reads the Bernstein/Benbow/Lubinski study of precocious youth (which she cites for skepticism) as actually vindicating identification, since the 12.3% who reached "eminence" (full professors, Fortune 500 execs, distinguished judges) vastly exceeds baseline, and eminence is a ceiling far above ordinary success. But he then mounts the opposite critique: precisely because high-ability kids can be reliably identified, the fact that G&T graduates succeed tells you nothing about whether the program teaches anything. He marshals causal-design research — Chin and Kocks (regression-discontinuity + lottery) finding G&T admission changes the classroom but produces no achievement gain (<0.04σ); Dobbie and Fryer (2011) finding NYC exam schools have little effect on SAT, college enrollment, or graduation; Abdulkadiroğlu et al. finding parents' school preferences are unrelated to school effectiveness once peer quality is controlled. His synthesis: parents are "massively under-rating selection effects," chasing above-average peers (a real but modest benefit, per Card/Chyn/Giuliano on Broward County, where gains accrue mainly to disadvantaged boys) rather than instructional value, producing a zero-sum scramble auctioned through real estate and tuition (Sidwell, Georgetown Day are "the best" only in the sense of being hardest to get into). He closes with the constructive claim — echoing his anti-detracking position — that we should teach kids material appropriate to their readiness (the weightlifting/swimming analogy: you progress at your own pace, and the gym with the strongest average clients doesn't make you stronger).

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Yglesias at his evidence-marshaling best, and the piece is unusually disciplined: it makes a counterintuitive double move (Arnold-Ratliff is wrong that giftedness is unidentifiable; she's right to criticize G&T, but for the inverse reason) and backs each leg with named causal-inference studies rather than priors. The core insight — that program outcomes are uninformative about program value-add precisely when selection is effective — is a genuinely important and underappreciated point, and the convergence of RD designs, lottery designs, and revealed-preference studies all pointing at "peer composition, not instruction" is a strong evidentiary base. The reframing of the entire prestige hierarchy (private schools, exam schools, "good school districts") as a zero-sum auction for above-average peers rather than a market for teaching quality is the kind of selection-as-argument move that does real explanatory work.

The strong-but-overstated element is the "literally zero evidence" rhetoric about elite private schools providing better value-add. The honest claim from the literature is "no detected effect in the available causal studies," which Yglesias actually concedes mid-paragraph ("maybe the studies wouldn't be well-designed") before reverting to "literally zero." Absence of well-identified evidence on hard-to-randomize settings (you can't lottery admission to Sidwell) is weaker than the rhetorical "zero," and the networking benefit he grants for elite colleges (Gallego at Harvard) plausibly operates for elite K-12 too via the same peer-network channel he otherwise credits. He's also somewhat eliding that "peer effects are real but small" and "selection explains the outcomes" can both be true while still leaving room for parents to be rationally buying the small real peer effect plus insurance against bad peers — i.e., the parental behavior he calls irrational may be a defensible bet on a modest-but-real benefit, especially given his own Broward finding that peer effects matter most for exactly the disadvantaged kids.

The strongest opposing view, which the piece doesn't fully engage: if peer effects are real (as he concedes) and accrue disproportionately to disadvantaged students (Card/Chyn/Giuliano), then the policy implication isn't "parents are wasting capital on a selection illusion" but "access to high-achieving peers is a real, scarce, unequally distributed good" — which is closer to Arnold-Ratliff's equity concern than Yglesias's framing admits. His instructional-efficacy prescription (teach-to-readiness tracking) is orthogonal to the peer-composition mechanism his own evidence centers, so the constructive close doesn't actually follow from the diagnostic body as tightly as the rhetoric implies: better-targeted instruction wouldn't address the zero-sum peer scramble that he identifies as the core pathology.

↩ Back to TOC

Better immigration can help fix the debt

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-16 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Rising elite concern about the U.S. budget deficit, and a new Manhattan Institute interactive calculator that estimates the fiscal/debt impact of specific immigration-policy changes. Yglesias, author of "One Billion Americans," distinguishes his fairly conservative views on asylum/deportation procedure (which have led progressives to read him as anti-immigration) from his enduring case that a well-organized immigration system has underrated economic benefits. Working the Manhattan calculator, he shows that pairing a path to citizenship with a "fine" turns legalizing ~10 million undocumented workers into a fiscal boon; that shifting family visas toward labor-market-success criteria (while holding numbers constant) yields gains; and that prioritizing the highest-wage H-1B offers and enlarging the program both help. The mechanism is a "double dividend": under a progressive tax system where spending skews to retirees and defense, an above-average-income worker pays in without near-term cost, and immigrants grow the debt-to-GDP denominator (a Germany-vs-Portugal scaling point). He extends this to temporary migration: end the H-2A seasonal-worker payroll-tax exemption, add a one-point "solidarity fee" on both sides (the worker's portion refunded on timely departure with good conduct), creating a model for expanding vetted guest-work that's superior to Biden-era ad hoc parole. He concedes housing and grid constraints but dismisses immigration-restriction-as-housing-fix as "loser behavior," noting restrictionists never pair their position with the tax hikes real deficit reduction requires.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is core abundance/One-Billion-Americans Yglesias, executed competently and with an unusually concrete policy hook (the Manhattan calculator). The strongest element is the "fix the flaw, don't cut the program" framing applied serially to legalization, family visas, and H-1B — it reframes restrictionist talking points (family migration isn't merit-based; H-1B doesn't select the best) as arguments for redesign rather than reduction, which is rhetorically deft and analytically defensible. The double-dividend logic (progressive taxation + denominator growth) is the real intellectual content and is broadly sound as a directional claim: skilled immigrants are net fiscal contributors in most CBO-style scoring, and population growth does ease debt-to-GDP arithmetic.

The honest tension Yglesias names but underweights is the "kick the can" admission. He concedes you can't substitute population growth for budgeting because "spending commitments will catch up to you" — which is exactly the steady-state problem with relying on the denominator: today's high-earning immigrant is tomorrow's Social Security/Medicare claimant, so the fiscal win is front-loaded and the long-run scoring depends heavily on assumed productivity, fertility, and wage trajectories that the calculator's headline numbers can obscure. His escape hatch — "however far we kick the can, we raise the odds of being rescued by AI post-scarcity" — is a tell that the permanent-resident version of the argument is weaker than the temporary-worker version on pure fiscal sustainability.

The temporary-migration proposal is where the piece is most interesting and most exposed. Charging H-2A workers payroll taxes for benefits they can never collect, plus an extra refundable point conditioned on "good conduct" and timely exit, is a genuinely novel revenue-and-compliance design — but it openly constructs a class of workers who pay into the social-insurance system by design without coverage, which is the standard ethical objection to guest-worker programs (a permanent disenfranchised labor tier), and Yglesias treats the farm lobby's higher labor costs as the only real objection. The strongest opposing view he doesn't engage: that the fiscal "double dividend" is partly an accounting artifact of excluding the long-run and the dependents, and that a program optimized to extract maximum payroll contribution from non-citizens who must leave is fiscally elegant precisely because it offloads the human costs outside the measurement window.

↩ Back to TOC

The Case for Trump Accounts: A Debate

Ray Boshara · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Michael Lind's companion case AGAINST Trump Accounts (the paired half of this Commonplace debate), and the broader inequality-based critique that universal baby bonds disproportionately benefit the rich. Boshara — a former St. Louis Fed wealth-inequality center director who spent two decades pushing child trust funds — defends Trump Accounts (the $1,000-at-birth IRAs created in the One Big Beautiful Bill for 14M+ newborns 2025–2028, invested in a broad index fund, locked until 18, open to outside contributors). He concedes the central critique outright — yes, the accounts probably increase already-near-record wealth inequality, because rich families will contribute the $5,000 maximum and poor ones won't — but reframes the decision around a different question: are lower- and middle-income kids better off with an account than without one? He answers yes for three reasons: (1) stock-market exposure, since the bottom half holds only ~2.5% of wealth and ~1% of equities and the market has been the most reliable wealth-builder over time now that housing and college returns have weakened; (2) the accounts function as a "shared community asset" magnet, pulling in employer, philanthropic (e.g., the Dells' $6.25B pledge), and government contributions, building social capital as well as wealth; and (3) "asset effects" — nearly two decades of SEED OK / Child Development Account research (Sherraden, Huang, Elliott) showing that the mere presence of an account raises maternal hope, college-going identity, and child outcomes even when families save little or nothing. He explicitly rejects using the accounts to privatize Social Security ("how kids start life, not how they end it") and lists reforms he wants: automatic enrollment, progressive deposits for poor kids, protection of means-tested-benefit eligibility, and permanence past 2028.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is an unusually candid policy defense: rather than denying the regressivity charge, Boshara grants it ("that critique appears correct!") and relocates the argument to whether a universal-but-tilted policy still leaves the worst-off absolutely better off. That is the honest framing, and it is the part of his case Lind never directly answers — Lind argues the accounts won't reduce inequality, which Boshara has already conceded, but the operative question Boshara poses is about absolute, not relative, benefit. His strongest empirical lever is the stock-ownership gap: if the bottom half holds ~1% of corporate equities and homeownership/college have become unreliable wealth-builders (the St. Louis Fed work on falling college wealth-returns, the Wharton work on riskier housing returns for Black and Hispanic owners), then a default index-fund position for every newborn is a real on-ramp to an asset class the poor are otherwise locked out of, and "the best thing newborns have is time" is the correct point about compounding. The "asset effects" literature (SEED OK, the randomized Oklahoma Child Development Account experiment run by Washington University's Center for Social Development) is his most distinctive contribution to the debate, because it claims benefits decoupled from saving — the presence of the account itself changes maternal expectations and children's college-going "identity," reducing "wilt" (kids who want and can attend college but don't transition).

The structural weaknesses are real and Boshara half-acknowledges them. First, the asset-effects research is the load-bearing claim and also the most fragile: it generalizes from a single state's experimental program with intensive outreach and matched deposits to a national auto-IRA whose default form has none of those supports, and effect sizes from hope-and-expectation measures are exactly the kind that can shrink under scale and replication. He cites the favorable findings without noting that the SEED OK accounts were progressive and matched — the very features the actual Trump Accounts lack and that he has to list as future reforms. Second, the community-asset framing leans on marquee philanthropic pledges (Dell, Dalio, Gerstner) that are by nature concentrated and discretionary; "imagine a child knowing their employer and a big-name donor believe in them" is aspirational, and the children most likely to attract a billionaire's geographically-targeted pledge are not randomly distributed. Third — and this is the tension at the heart of his own piece — every fix he says is essential (auto-enrollment, progressive deposits, benefit-eligibility protection, permanence) describes a different program than the one Congress passed, so his defense is really of the idea's potential rather than the enacted policy. He notes six million-plus families opted in, "millions short" of those eligible, which concedes that without auto-enrollment the take-up gap will itself be regressive (engaged, higher-information families enroll; the target population doesn't).

The genuine disagreement with Lind, stated precisely: this is not a fight about fiscal cost, and only secondarily about regressivity — it is about whether the route to security for the bottom half should run through asset ownership at all. Boshara's entire frame assumes that broadening capital ownership is the answer to a world where wages and traditional wealth-builders have failed the poor; Lind's frame is that the answer is to fix wages and social insurance so that asset ownership is unnecessary for a middle-class life. Boshara's strongest counter to Lind, which he makes implicitly, is empirical realism: the high-wage, robust-social-insurance world Lind wants has not arrived for the bottom half, the stock market has in fact been the most reliable wealth engine, and refusing poor kids exposure to it on the principle that they shouldn't need it leaves them holding nothing while the principle goes unrealized. His weakest spot against Lind is that he cannot rebut the privatization concern with much beyond a quotation from Invest America's Matt Lira ("how kids start life, not how they end it") — Lind has Bessent and Cruz on record describing the accounts as Social Security privatization, and Boshara's reassurance that they "should never" be used that way is a statement of his own preference, not a feature of the law, which (as both writers note) converts every account into a retirement IRA at 18.

↩ Back to TOC

Against Trump Accounts: A Debate

Michael Lind · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Ray Boshara's companion case FOR Trump Accounts (the paired half of this Commonplace debate), and more broadly the "ownership society" / "baby bonds" idea Lind himself once helped promote at the New America think tank he co-founded. Lind, an idiosyncratic anti-globalist populist, argues that the premise beneath all child trust funds — that good wages and benefits are no longer enough and must be supplemented by capital income — is itself the error; in a well-designed society one should be able to be middle-class via wages plus universal social insurance without owning "income-generating capital." He then stacks six objections: the accounts (1) delegitimize wage labor by implying "working for a wage is for suckers," (2) incentivize illegal immigration and birth tourism since any U.S.-citizen newborn qualifies and $1,000 is a large sum in sending countries, (3) are too small to matter (an unmatched account reaches only ~$5,389 by 18, versus a $35,856 median first-time down payment), (4) impose paternalistic spending restrictions that prove the "wealth" is really a government voucher, (5) wastefully duplicate existing programs (Medicaid, SSI, adoption credits) that already serve the same ends, and (6) are designed as a "backdoor" Social Security privatization — quoting Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Senator Ted Cruz openly admitting this. He coins "placebo populism": handing workers cheap simulacra of elite status (mortgaged starter homes, low-ranked diplomas, tiny accounts) instead of the real levers — higher wages, stronger benefits, anti-monopoly price relief. He closes by noting the accounts' grotesque regressivity (the White House CEA's own model shows Richie Rich reaching $303,787 versus Tiny Tim's $5,389) and that they were smuggled into the One Big Beautiful Bill rather than debated openly.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Lind in characteristic form: a left-populist economic nationalist using a fiscal-policy debate to restate his standing thesis that the dignified path to mass prosperity runs through high wages and universal social insurance, not through turning everyone into a small-scale capitalist. The argument's spine is the wage-versus-capital distinction, and it is genuinely the cleanest articulation of the anti-asset-building case: if you believe a flourishing society should let people be securely middle-class on labor income alone, then any policy premised on "you also need capital income" concedes a defeat before it starts. The strongest individual points are the voucher critique (restricted-use money "handed to you" that can only be spent on six approved categories is not wealth but a government program wearing the costume of ownership) and the privatization catch, which is not speculation — Lind quotes Bessent calling it "a backdoor way for privatizing Social Security" and Cruz calling them "Social Security personal accounts," and notes the tell that every account auto-converts to an IRA at 18 with no childhood withdrawals allowed. That sequencing (no use as a child development account, full retirement-account behavior at adulthood) is real evidence that the retirement-privatization framing isn't a paranoid read.

Set directly against Boshara, the actual point of disagreement is sharp and worth naming precisely: it is NOT primarily about fiscal cost, and not even mainly about regressivity (Boshara concedes the accounts probably raise inequality). The real clash is over the theory of the middle class. Boshara argues that the bottom half owns almost no stock (2.5% of wealth, ~1% of corporate equities) and that, since homeownership and college have become unreliable wealth-builders, stock-market exposure is now the most reliable remaining on-ramp — so any account, however small, leaves a poor kid better off than none, and "asset effects" (the SEED OK / Sherraden research showing maternal hope, college-going identity, reduced "wilt") deliver benefits even without saving. Lind's reply is categorical: he doesn't want to broaden asset ownership as the route to security at all; he wants to make security independent of asset ownership. So Boshara's "better off with than without" framing simply doesn't engage Lind, and Lind's "wage income is dignified, capital chasing is a scam" framing doesn't engage Boshara's empirical claim that the wage-and-social-insurance world Lind wants has not in fact materialized for the bottom half. They are arguing past each other on a genuine fork, not a confusion.

Where Lind is weakest: the immigration objection is the soft point in an otherwise tight brief. It rests on the assumption that a $1,000 deposit (paid out only at 18, locked until then) would meaningfully drive border-crossing or birth tourism — but the incentive structure he describes elsewhere (money inaccessible for 18 years, restricted use) is exactly what makes it implausible as a near-term migration magnet, and he offers no evidence of the effect, only the relative-magnitude-in-Honduras framing. It reads as a concession to the Commonplace/restrictionist audience rather than a load-bearing economic point, and it sits awkwardly beside his much stronger structural arguments. His "duplication of existing programs" point also cuts both ways: Boshara's whole case is that the existing programs (and homeownership, and college) are precisely what have stopped working for the bottom half, so "we already have Medicaid and the adoption credit" answers the medical/adoption uses but not the core wealth-gap claim. The strongest version of Lind's overall position survives these gaps because his real target is the frame — the idea that the answer to wage stagnation is to deputize the poor as junior investors rather than to raise their wages — and on that frame he is making the more fundamental argument, while Boshara is making the more practically-grounded one about the world as it actually is.

↩ Back to TOC

Colorado’s Funeral Mistake

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Colorado's 2027 decision to relicense funeral-service workers, prompted by 2023 scandals over body-part sales and rotting corpses. Tabarrok revisits his 2017 natural-experiment study (with Brandon Pizzola) of Colorado's unusual 1983 decision to delicense funeral directors — the only US state without such licensing. The study found delicensing lowered wages and prices and shifted demand toward cheaper cremation. He argues the relicensing reaction is misguided: there is no evidence abuses were worse in Colorado than in heavily licensed states (he notes corpse abuse occurs in licensed states too), a 1990 Colorado sunrise review found no widespread pattern of abuse post-delicensing, and a 2015 CEA review found licensing yields little quality or safety gain. He contends the specific abuses are unrelated to what licensing trains for, and that criminal penalties (the worst offender got 40 years) already supply deterrence. His closing claim: people demand a guarantee of good behavior that licensing cannot actually deliver.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Tabarrok's signature occupational-licensing argument applied to a hard case, and it is methodologically the strongest version of it: a natural experiment (one state delicensing for 40 years) plus a contemporaneous state regulatory review plus a federal meta-assessment, all pointing the same way — that licensing raised the wage premium and prices without a demonstrated safety dividend. The core logical move is sound and underused in licensing debates: the salient scandal (selling body parts, abusing corpses) is not the kind of harm that mortuary-science training hours address, so even granting the abuses, the proposed remedy is poorly matched to the failure. The "how many hours of don't-have-sex-with-corpses training" line is rhetorically sharp but makes a real point about means-ends fit.

The argument's exposed flank is the inference from "no evidence abuses were worse in Colorado" to "delicensing was not a cause." Absence of a documented cross-state differential is weaker than evidence of equivalence; the 2023 Colorado cases were genuinely extreme, and licensing's plausible value isn't training quality but the registry/inspection/revocation apparatus that often rides along with it — a channel Tabarrok's "training hours" framing sidesteps. His own study measured wages, prices, and cremation share, not malpractice rates directly, so it can't fully adjudicate the safety question it's invoked against. Still, the burden-of-proof structure favors him: relicensing is being justified on a safety rationale for which the proponents have not produced comparative evidence, and Tabarrok correctly identifies the political mechanism — a demand for guaranteed safety that licensing symbolically supplies but cannot actually provide. The piece is a clean instance of regulation-by-anecdote, where a vivid scandal drives a structural policy reversal that the available evidence doesn't support.

↩ Back to TOC

Labor market effects of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A newly published paper by Dallas Fed economist Anil Kumar on the labor-market effects of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (TCJA). Cowen quotes the abstract of a study that exploits plausibly exogenous state-level variation in TCJA tax changes — built from NBER-TAXSIM and IRS Statistics of Income return data — to estimate effects via local projections with two-way fixed effects. The finding: tax cuts equal to 1 percent of Adjusted Gross Income are associated with a 0.7–1 percentage-point rise in the labor-force participation rate and a 0.8–1.5 percent rise in payroll employment over the two years post-enactment. Cowen offers no commentary beyond attribution, presenting the abstract as a supply-side data point.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The post is a near-verbatim abstract drop, so its value rests entirely on the study's identification strategy rather than on any Cowen argument. The headline appeal is that it sidesteps the usual problem with TCJA evaluations — pre-enactment forecasts and CBO-style scoring that bake in assumed elasticities — by using cross-state variation in how the law's interaction with state tax codes generated different effective shocks. That is a genuinely better design than ex-ante projection, and the AGI-normalized effect sizes are economically meaningful if they hold.

The caution a reader should carry forward is what the design can and cannot isolate. State-level tax-change variation under a single federal law is "plausibly exogenous" only to the degree that pre-existing state economic trajectories don't correlate with the size of the TCJA shock a state absorbed (high-tax, high-income states experienced different shocks for reasons tied to their economies). The two-year window also can't separate a one-time level shift from durable growth, and labor-supply responses concentrated in the immediate post-cut period may partly reflect timing rather than permanent participation gains. Cowen's silent placement signals endorsement of the supply-side reading without engaging the distributional or revenue side that dominates the political fight over TCJA extension — a single mechanism reported without the cost ledger that the policy debate turns on.

↩ Back to TOC

Europe's Strategic and Political Moment

How to end the Brexit shitshow

Dominic Cummings · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the renewed UK debate over rejoining the EU/single market, adapted from a podcast interview. Dominic Cummings (the Vote Leave campaign director and Boris Johnson's former chief adviser, who ran Downing Street in 2019-20) argues that the post-1945 international order (UN, NATO, WTO, IMF) and the EU alike have drifted out of alignment with reality, and that Britain's own institutions — Whitehall (the senior civil service), the major parties, universities, legacy media — are "knackered" and failing simultaneously. He claims the EU is technologically irrelevant relative to China and California, citing the Draghi report (the EU's own 2024 competitiveness review, by former ECB head Mario Draghi) as the bloc's own admission that the single market is overrated. The current "shitshow," he says, is multiple defunct bureaucracies failing at once and colliding horizontally (procurement, planning, energy, housing, the MoD, welfare, deportations), with no one holding authority over the interactions; rejoining the EU is a comfort-zone retreat by an elite determined not to face technology or core reform. His distinctive constitutional claim is that Britain is uniquely fixable because the PM, via the unwritten constitution, can abolish departments, fire civil servants, and bypass judicial review in ways no other Western leader can — so the Left is racing to lock in the status quo via proportional representation (PR) and statutory civil-service protections before a Nigel Farage (Reform UK leader) government can run a DOGE-style purge (referencing Trump's Department of Government Efficiency), repeal the Human Rights Act, Equality Act, and Climate Change Act, and threaten to flood the House of Lords with 600 new peers. But the piece is an adapted monologue offering essentially no mechanism for how a 15-bill year-one "blitzkrieg" survives implementation, staffing, or the very institutional decay it diagnoses.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Cummings in his purest register: the anti-establishment systems-thinker who diagnoses the British state as a set of interlocking failing subsystems with no integrating authority. The strongest part of the essay is exactly that systems framing — the observation that you no longer have "one defunct bureaucracy" but multiple bureaucracies "smashing into each other," with the deportation example (can't deport, so you release, so budgets break, so the next thing breaks) as a concrete illustration of cascade failure. This is a genuine analytic contribution that the rejoin-the-EU debate does tend to dodge: even a maximalist single-market case doesn't touch procurement, planning, energy, or MoD dysfunction. His point that the Draghi report is "basically ignored in London because it's super bad for the EU" is a fair selection-of-evidence observation, and his read of the Left's PR-plus-civil-service-entrenchment strategy as a rational attempt to make the current settlement un-reversible is the kind of incentive-clear analysis he does well.

The structural problem is that the essay is all diagnosis and assertion of constitutional possibility, with the entire mechanism of repair compressed into a fantasy of velocity: draft 15 bills in opposition, win on Thursday, ram them through Monday, threaten the Lords with 600 peers, fire all the permanent secretaries. The "fired, fired, fired" passage treats the PM's prerogative powers as a magic wand while ignoring that the same decayed Whitehall he diagnoses is the instrument that would have to execute the purge — you cannot simultaneously claim the civil service is too broken to deliver anything and that it will smoothly implement a year-one revolution once the right people shout at it. His own track record is the unacknowledged counter-evidence: he had the actual powers of No. 10 in 2019-20 and, by his own telling, "the Tories stopped it all," which suggests the binding constraint is political coalition and durability, not constitutional permission. The "blitzkrieg," "we're going straight at the King" rhetoric also reveals the move at the heart of the piece — converting a hard problem of governance into a problem of insufficiently ruthless will.

The strongest opposing view is the one Cummings caricatures: that the EU/single-market question and the domestic-reform question are not actually substitutes, and that a country can both reduce trade friction with its largest market and reform its planning system — the framing of "rejoin OR fix the state" is a false binary that lets him dismiss the rejoin case without engaging its economic merits (he asserts the single market is invisible "in the data" but cites only the Draghi report's intra-EU critique, not UK trade data). His casual conflation of genuine institutional reform with repealing the Human Rights Act, Equality Act, and Climate Change Act also smuggles a hard-right policy program in under the neutral banner of "making things work," and the claim that opponents now call him "a fascist who should be in jail" is asserted without instance. Read against his pattern, this is Cummings at his most rhetorically energized and least falsifiable: a real systems diagnosis welded to an unaccountable, mechanism-free theory of heroic executive action.

↩ Back to TOC

Can Britain's military be saved?

Edward Luttwak · 2026-06-15 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the resignation of UK Defence Secretary John Healey and the broader European defense-spending debate. Edward Luttwak (the strategist and author of "The Grand Strategy of the Roman Empire" and "Coup d'État," writing in his characteristic provocateur register) opens with Britain's "long recessional" — from three million in the Army alone in 1945 to a "trained strength" of 126,440 across all services at the start of 2026, a figure that has fallen since Putin's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. His central distinction is that British defence secretaries were historically envied because they cut everything else — warships, aircraft, bases — to conserve money for what actually produces combat power: "training, munitions and maintenance." He likens combat readiness to "buying cut flowers," which must be repurchased daily, versus "furniture that can last for decades," and argues most European ministries waste their shrinking budgets keeping empty bases open to preserve janitors and retired NCOs' housing, and sustaining bloated officer corps (the Spanish army shrank from 280,000 to 75,000 since 1990 yet kept every command, including a three-star general for the Canary Islands). He cites the elder Denis Healey (Labour defence secretary 1964-70, an Anzio beachmaster, no relation to John) as the model, and condemns UN peacekeeping (UNIFIL in Lebanon never once stopped Hezbollah; the Srebrenica failure, where Dutch commander Karremans let Serb forces kill ~8,000 and was still promoted) as corrosive of real capability. His evidence that British "cut flowers" spending buys something real: RAF Eurofighters bombing Houthi sites in Yemen from Cyprus (1,500+ miles), a mission another European Eurofighter air-force chief flatly said his pilots could not fly, plus SAS support to Ukraine. The thesis: John Healey resigned as a "wake-up call" because cabinet colleagues now prioritize aging-population welfare/healthcare spending and the defense industry — which hates short-term readiness spending because it diverts money from long, multinational weapons "programs" (the British-Italian-Japanese fighter project he predicts the UK will abandon for the American jet) — and if his sacrifice goes unheeded, "unpleasant consequences will ensue."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Luttwak doing what Luttwak does best: a counterintuitive, materially-grounded distinction that cuts against the dominant framing. The reigning European defense debate is almost entirely about how much to spend (the 2%/3.5%-of-GDP NATO targets, "rearmament"). Luttwak's intervention is that the spending-level frame is nearly irrelevant if the money buys the wrong things — that readiness is a recurring consumable ("cut flowers") while bases, headquarters, and weapons platforms are durable goods that politicians and industry prefer precisely because they last, employ constituents, and photograph well. The cut-flowers/furniture metaphor and the 24-hour-restaurant analogy are genuinely clarifying, and the Spanish-army example (a three-star general and full joint command for the Canary Islands after a 73% personnel cut) is the kind of concrete, faintly absurd particular that makes the abstract point land. The structural insight — that the defense industry is an opponent of readiness spending, not an ally, because munitions and maintenance compete with long multinational procurement programs for the same pounds — is the essay's most non-obvious and most valuable claim, and it reframes "defense lobby" pressure as a force that can actively degrade fighting capability.

The structural weaknesses are characteristic Luttwak as well: assertion in place of accounting, and anecdote standing in for distribution. The keystone empirical claim — that British forces "cost twice as much to operate as their European counterparts" yet deliver real combat capability — is stated without a source or a definition of the denominator, and the entire argument rests on Britain being the virtuous exception. Yet the single concrete capability he offers, the RAF Yemen strike, is thin evidence for a systemic claim about readiness-per-pound: one long-range strike package (heavily enabled by US/coalition tankers and ISR he doesn't mention) does not establish that the broader force of 126,440 is more combat-ready than peers, and the "another air chief said no without hesitation" anecdote is unverifiable hearsay deployed as proof of British martial superiority — closer to flattery of his subject than analysis. The Srebrenica and UNIFIL material is rhetorically powerful but does double duty it can't quite carry: the lesson "peacekeeping corrupts real soldiering" is asserted, not demonstrated, and conflates the political failure of mandates with the readiness of the forces assigned to them.

The strongest opposing view is that Luttwak has built a false binary between "real" combat spending and everything else. Bases, sustainable basing infrastructure, an officer corps, and even long-cycle procurement are not simply waste; modern high-intensity war (as Ukraine has shown) is overwhelmingly a contest of industrial mass — shells, drones, air defense, depth of munitions stocks and the production lines behind them — which is exactly the durable "furniture"/program spending he disparages. A force optimized for daily readiness of a small professional cadre but with no industrial base or magazine depth is precisely the British/Western vulnerability Ukraine exposed; his own datum that the trained-strength figure is too small and still falling cuts against the idea that the legacy "British model" is the answer rather than part of the problem. There is also a real tension he doesn't resolve: he praises ruthless cutting of platforms to fund readiness, then mourns the amalgamation of his own regiment (the Oxfordshire and Buckinghamshire Light Infantry into the Green Jackets) — done explicitly "to preserve funds for readiness," i.e., exactly the trade-off he otherwise endorses. Not fundamentally flawed — the core distinction between consumable readiness and durable capital is a genuine and useful corrective — but the quantitative backbone is unsupported and the binary undersells the industrial-mass dimension that current wars have made central.

↩ Back to TOC

An Actually Popular British Politician

François Valentin · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Andy Burnham's 20-point by-election win in Makerfield on June 18, 2026—his route back into Parliament and the launchpad for an expected leadership challenge to PM Keir Starmer. François Valentin (senior consultant at London Politica) explains for non-UK readers who Andy Burnham is: Mayor of Greater Manchester since 2017, former Gordon Brown cabinet member and Jeremy Corbyn shadow-cabinet member, and—at 35% net-positive opinion, twice Starmer's rating—"Britain's most popular politician." Because the UK system requires the PM to sit in Parliament, Burnham needed a seat; Starmer had blocked him from one by-election in January, but a friendly Labour MP stood down in safe-Labour Makerfield to open the path, and Burnham's surprise rout of the surging Reform Party (Nigel Farage's right-populist outfit, which had won over half the seat's vote in May locals) demonstrated real political talent. Valentin's central critical move is the gap between rhetoric and record: Burnham brands his platform "Manchesterism"—regional devolution against London's extreme fiscal centralization, plus reindustrialisation and the "end of neoliberalism/trickle-down"—but the actual Manchester success predates him, was driven by foreign direct investment courted by consistently pro-business elites, and is decisively post-industrial and service-based, leaving it unclear which Manchesterism (the rhetorical or the real) he would govern by. Valentin then notes Burnham would inherit a brutal fiscal situation (£110bn debt interest, ballooning welfare costs) and that, as the Tory PM churn showed, the fresh-face polling bump is temporary. He closes that Labour's lifeline is a fragmenting right (Reform's loss, the breakaway Restore Britain splitting the vote, a Tory revival in Aberdeen South), but that Burnham, while perhaps "the best Labour has to offer," is an uncertain fix for the party's deeper problem of lacking a credible narrative and tangible results.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is solid explanatory political journalism—the kind of source the roundup's discovery layer is meant to surface, a competent non-American writer giving Anglophone readers an inside view of a fast-moving foreign contest. Its strongest and most genuinely analytical contribution is the "two Manchesterisms" critique: Valentin doesn't just report Burnham's brand, he tests it against the city's actual economic history and finds the populist anti-neoliberal rhetoric in tension with a record built on FDI, pro-business governance, and a post-industrial service economy. That is the rare profile move that does evaluative work rather than stenography, and it generalizes a useful skeptical frame—politicians often run on a stylized version of a record that, examined closely, supports a different program than the one they're selling.

The limits are mostly those of timing and genre. The piece is written within hours of the by-election result and is frank about the contingency ("Starmer's fate will be decided in the next few weeks"), so its predictive claims (Burnham will challenge, will probably win) are reasonable but unhedged against the obvious scenario the article itself raises—Starmer "obstinately clinging on" and forcing a protracted, summer-long fight. There is also an unexamined tension the piece sets up but doesn't resolve: it credits Burnham's "real political talent" via the 20-point win, then argues his core brand is substantively incoherent, without quite reconciling whether the talent is the substance or the salesmanship of a contradictory program. For the roundup's purposes the durable takeaways are two: the structural one (Britain's fiscal box—£110bn in debt interest, rising welfare—will constrain any successor as it constrained Starmer, so the "fresh face" thesis is fragile) and the right-fragmentation dynamic (Reform vs. the breakaway Restore Britain potentially costing Reform up to 70 seats), which is the more consequential and less-covered finding than the personality contest itself. Valentin doesn't overclaim, which is appropriate; the piece's value is orientation, not prediction.

↩ Back to TOC

UK: Burnham one step further in power struggle

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Greater Manchester mayor Andy Burnham (Labour) winning the Makerfield by-election, giving him a Commons seat from which he can challenge embattled Prime Minister Keir Starmer in a leadership contest. This Euro|topics curated debate gathers three takes spanning skepticism to qualified hope. The Guardian's Tom Baldwin argues Burnham's "time has not yet come": his policy program looks "remarkably similar" to Starmer's despite the vibes, his voter rating has slipped to net negative since he signaled national ambitions, and a challenge risks alienating Starmer-loyal MPs who think the government is finally delivering. The Financial Times's Robert Shrimsley reads Burnham more favorably as "a beacon of hope to counter despair" — his genial persona gives voters "permission to take a second look at Labour" and signals that someone might finally listen to the politically neglected, with Shrimsley locating Starmer's core failure in his inability to inspire hope "in either words or deeds." Italy's Il Manifesto (Leonardo Clausi) is the most cynical, casting Burnham as an "all-weather politician" (having backed Blair, Brown, and Corbyn in turn) who will use Makerfield purely as a "springboard" to Downing Street and then forget the constituency's traffic, poverty, and migration problems.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The throughline across the three excerpts is that none of the commentators makes a substantive policy case for Burnham — the debate is conducted almost entirely on the axes of timing, persona, and motive, which is itself the diagnostic fact. Baldwin's pointed observation that Burnham's program is "remarkably similar to that being pursued by the current prime minister" and Shrimsley's asset for Burnham being "a genial public persona" rather than any distinct agenda converge, from opposite valences, on the same reading: the case for Burnham is a case about affect (hope, permission, empathy) rather than direction. That makes the contest a referendum on Starmer's charisma deficit, not a programmatic realignment — Shrimsley says as much when he roots Starmer's failure in an inability to inspire rather than in his policies.

The aggregation's value as a discovery pick is the outside-in vantage Il Manifesto supplies: where the two British papers debate whether Burnham's hope is bankable, the Italian left-wing daily reframes the entire episode as elite careerism (a serial loyalist to whichever leader is ascendant, treating a working-class seat as a vehicle). That is the cross-national contribution — the continental left reading British Labour's leadership churn as substance-free positioning — and it sharpens, rather than merely echoing, the Guardian's quieter worry about "vibes aside." For roundup purposes this is a brief, low-stakes item whose interest is comparative: a Westminster succession story refracted through how three different editorial cultures decide what the story is about.

↩ Back to TOC

Macron’s Nuclear Gamble: Building a European Deterrent Faster Than French Politics Can Tear Down

Amélie Jaques-Apke and Lily Wojtowicz · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Whether Emmanuel Macron's March 2026 break with French nuclear orthodoxy — growing the arsenal, ending disclosure of the force de frappe's size, and launching "advanced deterrence" for European partners — can become a durable hedge against a US withdrawal from NATO.

Amélie Jaques-Apke (Sciences Po; Friedrich Naumann Foundation) and Lily Wojtowicz (American University; Chicago Council) argue the initiative is the most consequential shift in European nuclear policy in decades but rests on a fragile foundation. They identify three structural problems. First, "advanced deterrence" is NATO insurance but operationally inferior to it: where US sharing trains allied (e.g., German) pilots to deliver American B61 bombs, giving them a direct operational role, France keeps delivery exclusively in French hands (French Rafales with French crews on rotation from allied airfields) — "forward presence, not nuclear sharing" — so the reassurance gap is operational, not merely political, and cannot be closed by exercises without a sovereign-doctrine change Paris has ruled out. Second, France's small counter-value arsenal (fewer than 300 warheads, designed to destroy an adversary's cities rather than match its forces) is poorly suited to the flexible, graduated escalation-ladder responses six decades of US extended deterrence has conditioned allies to expect. Third, and most damaging, the 2027 election threatens the whole edifice: the far-right Rassemblement National leads the polls, treats nuclear sovereignty as non-delegable (Le Pen: "sharing deterrence means abolishing it"), and could freeze the framework — exactly as de Gaulle killed the Mollet government's Franco-Italian-German nuclear program in 1958. The authors judge the Franco-German steering group — Germany's first nuclear coordination outside NATO, with German observers at the classified Poker exercise — the real structural breakthrough, but note Berlin is simultaneously doubling down on the US umbrella (procuring the F-35A, the only NATO-certified B61 platform), revealing it still views Washington as the safer bet; their prescription is to entrench the mechanisms in binding treaty-level agreements before Macron leaves office.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a tightly argued, well-sourced policy piece whose central analytic move — separating "forward presence" from "nuclear sharing" — is genuinely clarifying and is the distinction most popular coverage of Macron's announcement blurs. The authors are right that the operative question for allied reassurance is not whether French warheads physically sit on European soil but whether allies have a hand on the trigger, and the contrast with the German F-35A/B61 arrangement (host-nation pilots certified to deliver) makes the deficiency concrete rather than abstract. The piece reads correctly against the War on the Rocks pattern: it is the credible-specialist, institutions-and-doctrine register, skeptical of grand declarations and attentive to whether mechanisms survive personnel changes.

The strongest section is the historical-pattern argument, which functions like a compressed version of Rehman's applied-history method elsewhere in this cluster: France has a recurring habit of proposing European collective-defense architectures and then walking away (the Mollet nuclear project killed by de Gaulle in 1958; the European Defence Community; the just-abandoned Future Combat Air System fighter program). This is not mere throat-clearing — it is the load-bearing reason allies will hesitate to spend political capital, and the authors correctly read Macron's binding-eight-allies-into-formal-mechanisms strategy as a conscious attempt to create "institutional facts on the ground" that a successor would find costly to dismantle. The "too developed to ignore but too fragile to trust" formulation is the piece's sharpest line and an honest statement of the worst case.

The main limitation is that the piece evaluates the initiative almost entirely on durability and reassurance-symmetry, and somewhat understates the asymmetry in the deterrence logic itself. A counter-value French force that "cannot be withdrawn across the Atlantic" may be a more credible existential backstop than US graduated deterrence precisely because it is all-or-nothing and sovereign — a small unwithdrawable deterrent can be more believable for the core homeland-survival scenario than a large one whose owner might decouple. The authors gesture at this ("a nuclear deterrent that cannot be withdrawn across the Atlantic") but treat the lack of escalation-ladder flexibility mainly as a deficiency, when the strongest opposing view is that flexible counterforce options were always the part of US extended deterrence allies least able to rely on. Relatedly, the analysis takes the premise of a plausible US NATO withdrawal as a given backdrop rather than interrogating it; the entire value proposition of advanced deterrence is contingent on that probability, which the piece does not size. Neither gap is fatal — the durability critique stands on its own — but they mean the piece is more persuasive on "will it survive 2027?" than on "is it the right design even if it does?"

↩ Back to TOC

Chartbook 452 Europe's Machiavellian moment?

Adam Tooze · 2026-06-21 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: a New York Times profile (by Anton Jäger) of Luuk van Middelaar — historian, EU speechwriter, and head of the Brussels Institute for Geopolitics (BIG) — and the broader "Europe's Machiavellian moment" / "return of history" discourse around European strategic autonomy. Adam Tooze (the financial-history and global-political-economy historian, author of "Crashed" and "The Deluge") revisits and republishes his own January 2022 critique (Chartbook 75) of van Middelaar, finding it still apt in summer 2026. Van Middelaar, reading Machiavelli through J.G.A. Pocock's "The Machiavellian Moment" (1975), casts the EU's history as a Bildungsroman — a coming-of-age — in which Europe moves from a "rule-making machine" governing predictable events under a US security umbrella toward "a genuinely competent political agency capable of improvising in the face of history," understood as a stream of contingent, unpredictable, "irrational" events; on this view it took Russia's 2022 invasion and Trump's threats to NATO to shake Europeans from their "vacation from power" and demand a "politics of events" rather than "lawyerly wrangling." Tooze's core objection is epistemic: van Middelaar treats the 2008 financial crisis, the 2013-14 Ukraine crisis, and the 2015-16 refugee crisis as essentially contingent and irrational, when in fact "all three were predictable and overdetermined" — irrational only in being "shot through with multiple conflicting logics," i.e., a Keynesian "muddle" whose process was nonetheless decipherable through conflict, contradiction, and "uneven and combined development." He demonstrates a telling "slippage": van Middelaar quotes George Kennan's 1952 observation that Russians are acutely aware of "the interplay of action and reaction" and "long-range historical determination," then within sentences transmutes this into Putin's mere improvisational knack for "blind chance" — "Middelaar has transmuted Tolstoy into Machiavelli." For van Middelaar, order comes only from leaders' "compass" (virtù vs. fortuna), never from any legible logic inhering in historical processes themselves. Tooze concludes that the whole "return of history" trope — whether voiced as trauma or Schadenfreude — is less the hallmark of "deep realism" than a sign of technocratic "disorientation, disillusionment and despair," carrying too much of its own depoliticized moment of genesis; arriving at a genuinely realistic account "stripped of the existential histrionics" remains work to be done.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Tooze operating in his most explicitly methodological mode, and the piece is really a defense of a theory of history against a rival one — the kind of argument that sits beneath his usual financial-plumbing reportage. The opposition is clean and important: van Middelaar (and the broader European-autonomy discourse he tutors) holds that history is "radical contingency," a Machiavellian river of fortuna that competent leaders navigate by virtù; Tooze holds that the major crises were "predictable and overdetermined," products of legible if conflicting structural logics ("uneven and combined development," a Trotskyist/Marxian phrase he deploys to mean processes that are uneven across regions yet causally entangled). The strongest single move is the textual close-reading of the Kennan slippage: van Middelaar cites Kennan precisely because Kennan attributes to the Russians a sophisticated grasp of action-reaction and long-range determination — i.e., exactly the legible historical logic Tooze defends — and then, within a paragraph, recodes that as Putin's gift for improvising amid "blind chance." Catching a writer's own evidence contradicting his thesis ("transmuted Tolstoy into Machiavelli") is genuine analytic value, not mere assertion, and it exposes that the "contingency" framing is doing motivated work.

The deeper claim — and the most useful one for a reader tracking the European strategic-autonomy debate — is Tooze's diagnosis of the function of the "return of history" trope. His argument is that calling the crises "irrational" and "unpredictable" is not a neutral description but a symptom: it betrays that European technocrats genuinely experienced their end-of-history complacency as a metaphysical shock, and that they are now universalizing their own trauma into a general theory of the moment. "Whether as trauma or Schadenfreude it carries with it too much of its own moment of genesis" is the load-bearing sentence — the contingency-realism that presents itself as hard-headed is, on Tooze's read, the afterglow of a depoliticized era's disillusionment. This is a real and bracing point: the "we must learn to improvise, history is back" register flatters its speakers as newly mature while excusing them from the harder task of explaining why the supposedly-shocking crises were structurally baked in (and thus, implicitly, attributable to prior European policy choices rather than to fortuna).

The honest counter is that Tooze's "predictable and overdetermined" can prove too much, and he half-concedes it. If every crisis was foreseeable in principle, the analyst owes an account of why it was not in fact foreseen and averted — and "overdetermined" risks becoming an after-the-fact alibi that any outcome would have confirmed, the mirror image of the contingency it rejects. His escape hatch — that the crises were "irrational" only as Keynesian "muddles" of conflicting logics whose process was nonetheless decipherable — is plausible but does considerable work to reconcile "predictable" with manifest surprise, and a fair-minded van Middelaar could reply that "decipherable in hindsight" and "navigable in real time" are different claims, and that his "politics of events" is precisely about the latter. There is also a slight unfairness in litigating the 2026 European-autonomy moment via a 2022 text aimed at a slightly different target; Tooze flags the republication honestly, but the move lets him score against the trope without fully engaging what BIG's concrete 2025-26 program (common debt issuance, a digital euro, a European economic security council) actually is — which is institution-building, arguably the opposite of pure improvisation. Still, no fundamental flaw: this is a high-quality intervention whose central service is to deny the autonomy discourse its self-flattering "deep realism" credential and to insist that the structural, decipherable account — the one that assigns responsibility — is the realistic one.

↩ Back to TOC

The Myth of Authoritarian Efficiency

Jørgen Møller · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The recurring "autocratic temptation"—the belief, invoked by Beijing officials, some climate activists, and populists alike, that dictatorship is more efficient and decisive than democracy. Jørgen Møller and Svend-Erik Skaaning (both political scientists at Aarhus University, Denmark) argue that the large empirical literature comparing how democracies and autocracies actually perform refutes the efficiency claim across nearly every domain. On security: consolidated democracies face far lower risk of civil war (losers get future electoral chances), democracies have won over 80% of wars since 1815 (legitimacy lets them ask citizens for sacrifice; democratic alliances are more durable), and the "iron law" that no two modern democracies have warred holds for two centuries—with Putin's Ukraine miscalculation offered as a case of autocratic epistemic isolation as liability. On economics: democracies enjoy a modest but robust long-run growth advantage, protect the property rights and free idea-circulation that drive a knowledge economy, and—crucially—avoid the man-made catastrophes (Mao's Great Leap famine, Soviet collectivization) that unchecked authority periodically produces; every wealthy high-tech society except Singapore made the final leap under democracy. On climate: the authors argue freedom of expression enables rather than obstructs climate action and that the eco-authoritarian fantasy never answers the "who guards the guardians" problem. China is treated as the principal counter-exhibit and dismantled: manipulated growth statistics (night-light satellite data), a divergence from democratic Taiwan/South Korea that started equally poor in 1949, domestic-security spending exceeding the military budget, and a Covid record of suppressed early warnings and a botched zero-Covid exit. They close with Machiavelli's observation that the mechanism favoring republics is not virtue but accountability—the capacity to detect and correct mistakes.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a competent, well-sourced synthesis essay, and as a corrective to lazy "China runs trains on time" admiration it is effective—the authors do the valuable work of insisting on the right comparison ("actually existing democracies vs. actually existing autocracies," not democracies vs. an idealized benevolent dictator). The strongest section is the catastrophe argument: the claim that autocracy's distinctive failure mode is not lower average performance but the periodic, unchecked disaster (Great Leap Forward, collectivization) is more analytically durable than the growth-rate comparisons, because it targets variance and tail risk rather than means, and democratic accountability does plausibly truncate the worst outcomes.

The piece's principal vulnerability is selection and the war-of-citations method. It strings together a sequence of academic studies each of which supports the thesis, but a reader can't tell from the essay whether these represent settled consensus or the authors' favored side of live disputes—the "democracies win 80% of wars" finding, the democratic growth advantage, and the democratic-peace "iron law" are all real research findings but also all contested in their respective literatures (e.g., democratic-peace theory has well-known boundary disputes and the war-victory figure is sensitive to how "democracy," "war," and "initiation" are coded). Because every cited study points the same way, the essay reads as a brief for a verdict the authors held in advance, which is the genre's occupational hazard. This doesn't make the conclusion wrong—the weight of evidence does favor it—but the piece would be more credible if it engaged a single strong contrary finding on its own terms rather than only the strawman "benevolent dictator."

A secondary point: the China teardown is the most concrete and persuasive part, but it leans on the night-lights study (a real Economist-reported finding that authoritarian growth figures are inflated) as if it settles the magnitude question, when night-light estimation is itself a contested proxy. The deeper argument—that China's rise reflects non-replicable starting conditions plus reform plus global integration, and carries obscured costs—survives even if one discounts the statistics claim, so the essay's spine is sound. Read against the publication: this is exactly the house Persuasion piece (pro-liberal-democracy, evidence-forward, anti-strongman), well-executed within that lane, and the Machiavelli close—accountability not virtue as the operative mechanism—is the sharpest single idea, since it concedes democrats aren't wiser and locates the advantage in error-correction, the same move Fukuyama's companion piece makes about Trump's Iran reversal.

↩ Back to TOC

Adrian Wooldridge on Sweden and liberalism

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A Bloomberg column by Adrian Wooldridge (and his new book on liberalism) on Sweden's economic and social trajectory. Cowen excerpts Wooldridge approvingly. The economic picture: 1.8–1.9% projected GDP growth, 1.5% inflation, a debt-to-GDP ratio above 35% (among the world's lowest), and a diversified business sector with Europe's highest unicorns-per-capita (Spotify) alongside manufacturing and engineering buoyed by demand for server farms and military equipment — offset by recent stagnation, 9.4% unemployment, and high household debt. The social picture: Sweden's first net emigration in 50 years following higher minimum wages for labor visas, tougher citizenship tests, and payouts up to $37,000 for refugees who leave voluntarily, plus a crime crackdown (more police, lower age of criminal responsibility to 14, expanded stop-and-search) credited with cutting shootings 63%, from 390 in 2022 to 147 by end of 2025.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece's organizing move — and Wooldridge's, which Cowen ratifies via "self-recommending" — is to fold economic liberalism and a restrictionist immigration/crime agenda into a single coherent "liberalism," with Sweden as the proof of concept. That is the load-bearing and contestable claim: it treats fiscal discipline, pro-market reform, and tough-on-crime/immigration policy as one package whose success validates the whole, when each component has independent causes and the bundling is itself the ideological argument rather than an empirical finding.

The numbers cut in more than one direction, which the excerpt half-acknowledges. The fiscal and innovation metrics are genuinely strong and predate the recent restrictionist turn, so they can't be evidence for it. The 63% drop in shootings is the most striking causal claim and the weakest-supported here — it is presented as a policy effect, but gang-violence statistics swing on enforcement operations, gang-conflict cycles, and measurement changes, and a two-to-three-year decline is too short to attribute confidently to the listed reforms. The "first net emigration in 50 years" is framed as success, yet it sits next to 9.4% unemployment and high household debt; whether emigration reflects a healthy correction or labor-market weakness is exactly the kind of question the celebratory framing skips. The strongest reading to carry forward is descriptive: a center-right European government has paired market-friendly economics with a hard immigration reversal and is claiming results on both — a template other European parties are watching. The post's analytical move is to package these as one validated doctrine; the evidence supports the individual pieces unevenly and the causal crime claim least of all.

↩ Back to TOC

The Wider World: China, the Global South, and the Price of Sovereignty

Will China, Inc. be zombified?

Noah Smith · 2026-06-15 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The under-asked question of whether China's banks are keeping insolvent firms alive through "evergreening" the way Japan's did after 1990 — and whether China's state-directed financial system insulates it from the resulting productivity drag.

Noah Smith builds the analogy carefully despite his general skepticism of Japan-China comparisons. He explains "evergreening": after Japan's 1990 bust, banks lent failing borrowers (like the retailer Daiei) fresh cheap money to repay old loans, reclassifying bad debt as "good" to avoid recognizing losses and triggering capital-requirement and political trouble — keeping "zombie" firms alive that, per Caballero, Hoshi, and Kashyap (2008), starved healthier upstarts of capital, talent, and resources and helped cause Japan's productivity stagnation. He then marshals evidence that China is doing the same: official non-performing-loan ratios have fallen since 2021 even as the share of loss-making firms rose (Rhodium Group; China's National Audit Office found 16 of 43 audited banks had NPLs double the reported figure), below-benchmark lending has surged, and the Dallas Fed documents firms unable to service interest. Smith's central argument is against the reassurance that this is harmless in a state-directed economy where bank debt is effectively government debt: he counters that Japan's government was also deeply entangled with its banks and bailed them out, yet that did not solve the zombie problem — because even if zombies don't compete for capital, they still compete for labor, raw materials, land, and energy, which are finite even in China. He links this to "involution" (ruinous price-cutting): Beijing redirected bank lending from property to manufacturing, but keeping inefficient manufacturers alive as zombies (Garcia-Herrero estimates ~30% of listed green-tech firms) means they slash prices below cost to hold market share, collapsing margins even for productive firms. The conclusion: state-finance fusion may make zombification worse, and the cheap-export "victory" Westerners fear is a double-edged sword that lowers Chinese domestic productivity and long-run growth.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Smith working in his strongest mode — applying a specific, well-identified academic mechanism (the Caballero-Hoshi-Kashyap zombie-lending literature) to a contemporary case, with the comparison's limits stated up front rather than smuggled in. His self-awareness is the piece's main credibility asset: he opens by saying he is "very skeptical" of Japan-China analogies and that the bubbles, industrial policies, and trading relationships differ, then argues the parallel holds narrowly on the evergreening mechanism. That is the disciplined way to use an analogy, and it is the same methodological virtue on display in the Rehman and Macron pieces elsewhere in this cluster — claim the mechanism, not the whole historical rhyme.

The strongest part of the argument is the rebuttal to the "it doesn't matter in a state economy" objection, which is the smart reader's first response and which Smith pre-empts well. The financial-crisis channel (undercapitalized banks failing) genuinely is muted when the state backstops everything, so a naive zombie-warning would be vulnerable here. Smith's move — that the binding constraint is real-resource misallocation (labor, energy, materials, land), not the financial plumbing — is the correct and non-obvious point, and it neatly explains "involution" as a downstream symptom rather than a separate puzzle: subsidized-via-cheap-loans zombies that sell below cost are exactly what would drive economy-wide margin collapse. The footnote engaging the "it's just Chinese people owing other Chinese people" view shows he is steelmanning the opposing case rather than knocking down a strawman.

The honest caveats are about evidentiary load and direction of inference. The piece is heavily reliant on a small set of China-skeptical sources (Rhodium Group multiple times, the Dallas Fed, Garcia-Herrero, a Business Times story) whose estimates of "true" bad-debt ratios range wildly — from the official 1.5% to "about 10%" to "double that" — which is itself a tell that nobody actually knows the magnitude, and Smith treats the high-end estimates as roughly settled. The Garcia-Herrero "30% of listed green-tech firms are zombies" figure is doing a lot of work and is presented without its methodology. There is also a subtle direction-of-causation issue Smith glides over: some below-cost Chinese pricing in EVs, solar, and batteries reflects genuine scale economies and learning curves (real productivity gains), not only zombie subsidy, so the involution-from-zombies story and the China-is-actually-winning-on-cost story partly overlap and are hard to disentangle from the outside. The strongest opposing view — which the piece does not fully engage — is that China's state can do something Japan's could not: politically force consolidation and capacity cuts (it has begun pushing "anti-involution" supply-side discipline in steel, solar, and coal), so the Japanese trap may not bind if Beijing proves willing to absorb the unemployment shock Japan refused to. The analogy is well-built but its predictive force depends on assuming Chinese political economy will repeat Japan's specific unwillingness to let firms die, which is contestable.

↩ Back to TOC

The Fall of Fortress Singapore: Three Lessons from the Collapse of Britain’s Great Asian Bastion

Iskander Rehman · Read full article ↓

Responding to: What the 1942 collapse of "Fortress Singapore" can teach contemporary US planners facing a rising China, especially as the war against Iran ("Operation Epic Fury") drains Indo-Pacific munitions.

In this long "Applied History" essay, Iskander Rehman (Atlantic Council; incoming University of Texas at Austin) reconstructs the 70-day Malayan campaign that ended with Britain surrendering 100,000 men to a numerically inferior Japanese force, and extracts three lessons. First, the failure of British, Commonwealth, and American forces to do real pre-conflict joint planning — the US insisted on "parallel but independent" courses, no joint Pacific command emerged, and intelligence went unshared — left organizational seams that Japan's near-simultaneous strikes on Singapore, the Philippines, and Pearl Harbor brutally exploited; he urges Washington to build genuine joint logistics and command integration with Indo-Pacific allies now (citing the July 2025 US-Japan-Australia at-sea-reload arrangement and allied shipbuilding in Korea/Japan). Second, Singapore's defense rested on the receding promise of naval relief: the "period before relief" stretched from 28-42 days in the 1920s to 180 by 1941, and Churchill consciously prioritized homeland defense, the Mediterranean, and aid to the USSR over Malaya — a few squadrons of the ~500 Hurricanes sent to Russia might have saved it. Rehman maps this onto a Taipei-for-Hormuz tradeoff: since February's Operation Epic Fury, the US has burned through interceptors and high-end munitions at a rate that has delayed Foreign Military Sales deliveries to European and Asian allies (including ~400 Tomahawks to Japan) and prompted private fears that Taiwan contingency plans could not be fully executed near-term. Third, he warns against linear, single-pacing-threat planning: just as Britain failed to anticipate the fall of France and Japan's occupation of French Indochina, US planners should war-game "branching possibilities" (a Taiwan seizure, Russian basing for the PLA, a hostile Manila). His sharpest move is a two-edged hubris parallel: interwar Tokyo read the democracies as exhausted and lacking stomach for a long war — a "partial and self-serving" reading that ignored US industrial depth — and today Beijing may similarly read America as a "limping giant," absorbing only the half of the Iran war (depleted magazines) that confirms its narrative while discounting the demonstrated willingness to sustain high-tempo kinetic operations.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the strongest piece in the cluster and a model of how to do analogical strategic history: Rehman does not claim 1942 Malaya predicts a 2020s Pacific war, he uses it as a structured lens for a specific mechanism — that a globally overstretched maritime power's neatly ranked theater priorities "do not always survive contact with reality," and that the marginal resource diverted to an active theater is "a silent decrement" elsewhere. The right test for this kind of essay is whether the analogy's load-bearing joints actually hold, not whether it marshals data, and on that test it largely succeeds. The "period before relief" stretching from 28 days to 180 is a genuinely illuminating quantification of how a deterrent built on promised reinforcement hollows out as the gap grows — and the parallel to US power-projection-across-distance assumptions for a Taiwan fight is exact, not decorative. The Australia-as-canary thread (Canberra doubting the Singapore strategy as early as 1930, then pivoting "free of any pangs" to Washington in 1941) is deployed precisely, mapped onto regional allies more alarmed about China than the metropole.

The essay's most valuable contribution is the symmetrical hubris argument in the conclusion, which resists the genre's usual gravitational pull. The lazy version of this essay would simply warn Americans not to underestimate China the way Britain underestimated Japan. Rehman instead runs the analogy in the harder direction: the adversary's overconfidence is the 1941 echo. Tokyo's read of the exhausted democracies was "not wholly irrational" — grounded in the fall of France and a half-mobilized America — but fatally one-sided, and Beijing now has "ample reason" from Operation Epic Fury's visible costs to think the window is open, while the same war demonstrates American willingness to fight. That the contemporary parallel "cuts both ways" is exactly the discipline most applied-history pieces lack; it converts a comfort-the-hawks artifact into a genuine analytic claim about selective perception under strategic narrative.

Two structural caveats. First, the essay leans heavily on the counterfactual that "one month's supply of the aircraft sent to Russia would have saved Malaya" (Southby's 1942 parliamentary claim), and Rehman is admirably honest that even a major last-minute influx "would not have made up for" British inexperience and a broken command structure — but the rhetorical force of the Taipei-for-Hormuz frame depends on the marginal-aircraft argument being more decisive than his own caveats concede. If Singapore fell primarily on doctrine, morale, and command (the jungle-as-green-wall conceptual rigidity, Percival's paralysis), then the resource-husbandry lesson is real but secondary, and the implied prescription — forward-deploy more, husband interceptors — addresses the less decisive variable. Second, the China-as-revisionist-power framing and the casual treatment of "Operation Epic Fury" as settled fact reflect War on the Rocks' house consensus (great-power-competition, alliance-integration, replenish-the-magazine); the essay is a sophisticated articulation of that worldview rather than a challenge to it, and a reader should note that the entire edifice assumes a Sino-American war is the pacing scenario worth optimizing against. The strongest opposing view is the restraint critique the essay half-anticipates but does not engage: that the real 1941 lesson is not "coordinate better and pre-position more" but "do not embark on exorbitant wars of choice" (Rehman states this once, then moves on) — i.e., that the simultaneity problem is best solved by not opening the Iran theater at all, a conclusion the essay's framing forecloses.

↩ Back to TOC

The CFA Franc Is Not a Currency. It Is a Constitutional Constraint

Loic Gapin · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the perennial economic debate over the CFA franc (whether its euro peg delivers stability or traps fourteen West/Central African states at a European-calibrated exchange rate), which Gapin argues is conducted "on the wrong terrain." Gapin — a Cameroonian writer opening with his family's experience of the January 1994 fifty-percent overnight devaluation — contends the primary question about the CFA franc (a currency created in 1945 to preserve French economic influence over its colonies, pegged first to the French franc then the euro, with member states historically depositing reserves at the French Treasury) is constitutional, not economic: by what right does external power set fourteen nominally sovereign nations' monetary policy? He treats France's 2019 reforms (French reps leaving central-bank boards, ending the reserve-deposit obligation, renaming the West African zone) as real but cosmetic, since the peg and convertibility guarantee — the binding constraints on using monetary policy as a developmental tool — remain.
