His central, candid move is to reject the "France tricks them" account as both comforting and wrong: the arrangement survives because guaranteed euro convertibility benefits precisely the political-commercial class with the power to exit (holders of savings and import contracts) while costing the food-seller nothing, an alignment of interests "more durable than any conspiracy." He reads the 2024-onward Sahel juntas (Mali, Burkina Faso, Niger, now the Alliance of Sahel States, planning a currency called the sira) as proof that exit became sayable only when actors outside the elite bargain forced the question — while stressing exit remains unproven as survivable, since the three still use the CFA franc and depend on cross-border trade. He invokes economist Joseph Tchuindjang Pouemi's 1980 Monnaie, Servitude et Liberté throughout: "a currency that belongs to someone else is sovereignty that belongs to no one." He pre-empts the strongest objection — does the zone even matter to France in 2026? — by conceding it matters far less than in 1960 (Europe takes only ~a fifth of the union's exports, the Netherlands ahead of France) but reframing: the guarantee is cheap to keep and offers France residual monetary influence as its regional standing collapses, so France isn't clinging to the zone, it's "simply not paying enough to want to let it go."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece's signature strength is its refusal of the easy nationalist story, which it names and dismantles in the same breath. The "France tricks them" account is comforting, Gapin writes, precisely because "it locates all agency in Paris and none in Yaoundé or Abidjan or Dakar" — and wrong for the same reason. By relocating the explanation from external coercion to a domestic alignment of interests (the constituency with the most political power is the one with the most to lose from a floating, depreciation-prone national currency), he produces an account that survives its own strongest counter-question — "so why has no one left?" — instead of dodging it. This is the move a weaker version of the argument skips, and Gapin foregrounds it as a structural section ("So why has no one left?"). The convertibility-as-elite-insurance mechanism is the analytical core: a fixed window into the euro is "enormously valuable… not to the food seller in Biyem-assi, but to the political and commercial class that holds savings, signs import contracts, and may one day wish to move wealth abroad without exchange rate risk."

The "constitutional, not economic" framing is the title's bid and is genuinely useful as a reframing — it separates the question of whose authority sets policy from the question of whether the policy is good — but it is also doing rhetorical work the author should be watched on. Gapin concedes the peg has delivered "real benefits such as inflation control and reduced exchange rate uncertainty," and concedes that monetary sovereignty is "necessary but not sufficient for development" with "no shortage of African currencies mismanaged into ruin." Having conceded both, the constitutional framing lets him bracket the economic ledger as merely "the second question" rather than have to win it. That is defensible as a claim about priority (sovereignty is logically prior to its uses), but a reader should notice that it also conveniently insulates the sovereignty case from the empirical record of post-exit currency mismanagement he himself acknowledges. The argument is honest about this — it does not claim sovereignty guarantees good outcomes — but the title's confidence ("It Is a Constitutional Constraint") runs slightly ahead of the body's hedges.

The Sahel treatment is the most disciplined part: Gapin resists the temptation to read the juntas as vindication. He insists "the rhetoric runs far ahead of the reality" — the three still use the CFA franc at the unchanged 655.957 peg, remain in the West African monetary union even after quitting ECOWAS (the Economic Community of West African States) because ~60% of activity depends on the common market, and "the new currency is announced, not born." His formulation — "The Sahel has proven that exit is sayable. It has not yet proven that it is survivable" — is the piece's sharpest line and its most defensible empirical claim. The deeper observation, that exit became real policy only when "actors outside the elite bargain forced the question," ties the Sahel section back to the domestic-interest mechanism and gives the essay a unified causal spine rather than two loosely-joined topics. The closing pivot — that "importance" to France is the wrong measure, because the asymmetry (trivial for France to keep, ruinous for members to live inside) is the whole point — is a clean reframing that answers the "but does France even care?" deflation without overclaiming French malice. No fundamental flaw; the argument is unusually self-aware about its own limits.

↩ Back to TOC

The Fujimori Restoration

Juan David Rojas · 2026-06-16 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Peru's razor-thin June 2026 presidential run-off (Keiko Fujimori at 50.05 percent to leftist Roberto Sánchez's 49.95 percent), with a formal winner not expected until mid-July. Juan David Rojas — a South Florida writer covering Latin America and the Iberian Peninsula for American Affairs — frames Keiko as the "anointed heir" of her father Alberto Fujimori, Peru's president-turned-dictator (1990–2000) whose defeat of the Maoist Shining Path insurgency built an enduring base but whose autocratic self-coup, war crimes against indigenous Peruvians, and corruption built equally durable opposition. Rojas notes the bleak irony that Keiko's own party, Fuerza Popular (Popular Force), bears heavy responsibility for the instability she now promises to fix: after winning a 2016 legislative majority it helped drive out five of the eight presidents who have governed since, while the region-wide Odebrecht (Brazilian construction conglomerate) bribery scandal convicted four former presidents and indicted Keiko herself, who has eluded conviction. He judges her opponent Sánchez — a conventional Lima politician allied to jailed former president Pedro Castillo (the Andean schoolteacher who attempted a failed self-coup) and to indigenous strongman Antauro Humala — too tied to Castillo's chaos to be the stabilizer. The strongest material in the piece is Rojas's portrait of Peru's "neoliberal utopia" pathology: a genuinely sound macroeconomy (2 percent inflation, ~4 percent growth, a respected independent central bank under twenty-year incumbent Julio Velarde) coexisting with 72 percent informal employment and a booming illegal gold-mining sector now rivaling Colombia's cocaine trade in value, with Congress across the spectrum passing laws that shield the mafias. He prescribes constitutional reform — a more conventional presidential system, higher impeachment thresholds, excision of the abused "moral incapacity" clause — and forecasts Keiko aligning Peru with Trump's Washington while still deepening ties with China (the Chancay megaport) where Beijing offers the better deal, concluding national interest may trump ideological alignment.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is exactly the kind of area-expertise piece the roundup prizes from Compact: a tightly reported, non-ideological dispatch on a country the Anglophone press barely covers, written by someone who clearly tracks the region closely. Judged as analytic journalism rather than essay, its strength is the central diagnostic reframe — that Peru's headline "economic miracle" (sound money, central-bank independence) and its governance catastrophe (serial presidential removals via the elastic "moral incapacity" clause, 72 percent informality, state-captured illegal mining) are not in tension but are two faces of the same arrangement: economic policy successfully insulated from politics precisely because politics has been hollowed out and the formal economy left to a minority. That is a real insight, and the gold-mining-equals-Colombian-cocaine comparison, backed by the specific 2024 legislative rollbacks (barring prosecution of crimes under six-year terms, restricting raids, blocking explosive and weapon seizures), is the piece's most load-bearing and least replaceable reporting.

The honesty about Keiko is what keeps the "best hope for stability" thesis from collapsing into endorsement. Rojas does not launder her: he names her family's autocratic record, her own indictment, and Fuerza Popular's role as the prime engine of the instability she now promises to cure, and he flags the legitimacy problem head-on — her likely win rests on the overseas diaspora while Sánchez took a narrow majority inside Peru and the left posted its best legislative result in decades. That is the structural weakness he is candid about: a "stabilizer" installed against the domestic vote, by the dynasty most responsible for the destabilization, is a fragile basis for stability, and the essay's own caveat — "It remains an open question whether Keiko will attempt to recreate a version of her father's autocracy" — concedes the thesis is a bet, not a forecast. The "best hope" claim is doing a lot of work on thin evidence; it rests mostly on Sánchez being worse rather than on any positive case that Keiko will govern stably, and the constitutional-reform prescription (sound on the merits) presumes a reformist disposition the rest of the piece gives no reason to expect from her.

The strongest opposing view is that the framing quietly accepts the legitimacy of serial congressional removals and a Fujimori restoration as the price of "stability," when an equally plausible reading is that Peru's crisis is precisely the capture of the impeachment mechanism by Keiko's faction — making her victory the consolidation of the dysfunction, not its resolution. Rojas half-grants this (he wants the "moral incapacity" clause excised, the very tool her party wielded), which makes the piece internally honest even where its top-line judgment is contestable. The China-versus-US closing — that even Peru's most pro-American right will deepen ties with Beijing where the deal is better (Chancay port, the canceled F-16 purchase) — is a useful, unsentimental corrective to assumptions that ideological alignment determines great-power positioning in the region. No fundamental flaw; the central reporting is sound and the speculative thesis is appropriately hedged.

↩ Back to TOC

Cuba

Alex Tabarrok · 2026-06-20 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A Miami Herald report on economic reforms announced by Cuban Prime Minister Manuel Marrero. Tabarrok reproduces the Herald's list of newly permitted activities, which together amount to a dramatic opening: private and foreign capital in fuel sales, private corporate banking, ownership of multiple companies and hiring beyond 100 workers, foreign direct hiring, foreign investment in telecom and data infrastructure, 99-year surface rights and 50-year leases, real-estate development in tourism, unlimited foreign wholesale and retail trade, and the sale of state assets and company shares to private and foreign buyers. He frames the package as expanding the private sector six decades after Cuba banned all private business — down to fried-food stands — and notes the government is now so destitute it is seeking to hand off management of its zoos and aquariums.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The post is curation with a thesis: Tabarrok lets the reform list do the arguing, and the implicit claim is straightforward and stated plainly in his closer — that the scale of the about-face is itself the verdict on central planning. As a piece of political economy the framing is strong because the items are not cosmetic tinkering; allowing foreign entities unlimited wholesale/retail trade, private banking, and the sale of state companies' shares are structural concessions that cut against the regime's founding commitments, and the zoo-privatization detail is a vivid index of fiscal collapse.

What the post leaves a reader to supply is the gap between announcement and implementation, which is where Cuban reform cycles have repeatedly stalled — the 2010s cuentapropista openings under Raúl Castro were partially rolled back, and "case-by-case evaluation" language (as on tourism property sales) is the historical mechanism by which liberalization gets throttled in practice. Tabarrok's reading is also clean on causation in a way the situation may not be: the reforms are plausibly driven as much by the post-2020 economic implosion, lost Venezuelan subsidies, and US sanctions pressure as by ideological surrender, and distinguishing "central planning failed" from "the external props were kicked away" matters for the lesson drawn. The directional claim — that the regime is conceding the case for markets under duress — is well-supported; the open question is whether these announcements survive contact with the bureaucracy that has gutted prior openings.

↩ Back to TOC

Faith, Ritual, and National Identity

The Revolutionary Significance of Trump’s White House UFC Fight

Matthew Schmitz · 2026-06-15 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Donald Trump hosting a UFC (Ultimate Fighting Championship) mixed-martial-arts fight on the White House lawn, an event Schmitz argues would have been unthinkable in an earlier GOP era. Matthew Schmitz, an editor of Compact, builds a sports-as-political-philosophy typology borrowed largely from columnist George Will: baseball as the republican ideal (no single player dominates; even the best teams lose a third of the time, so you can't love it "if winning is everything") embodying bourgeois fair play and the rule of law; football as the sport of postwar managerial empire (the quarterback coordinates a coordinated, specialized machine — "violence, punctuated by committee meetings"). Against both, Schmitz positions combat sports — individualist, agonistic, refusing live-and-let-live — as the rising form, evidenced by Joe Rogan's podcast dominance and now a UFC card at the White House, with the broadcast intercutting images of Lincoln and FDR with fight footage and Dana White's 2016 RNC framing of Trump as "a fighter." He invokes John McCain (who once sought to ban MMA, and who believed one could "lose with dignity" — a belief Schmitz says Trump does not share) as the genteel old GOP being displaced. The interpretive turn enlists Norman Mailer's romance of boxing ("The White Negro") and historian Ilene O'Malley's argument that combat sport's masculine display answers the "emasculation" of class and racial oppression, noting that left nationalists like Fidel Castro embraced boxing to assert national dignity; Schmitz claims Trump does the same with MMA — masculinity as vindication of "a people in need" — concluding the fight was not "bread and circuses" but "part of Trump's very own American revolution."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a short, deliberately provocative interpretive essay and should be judged on explanatory coherence, not on data; on that register it is more clever than persuasive, and it confirms the predictable post-liberal Compact register more than it surprises. The sport-typology is genuinely engaging and the Will borrowing is apt — baseball-as-republican-restraint versus football-as-managerial-empire is a real and useful contrast, and extending it to combat sport as the form of a post-managerial populism is a clean conceptual move. The strongest insight is structural rather than celebratory: that the same logic which made football overtake baseball in the managerial-imperial postwar era could now make individualist combat sport ascendant as that managerial order loses legitimacy. As a reading of cultural-political mood, that has some force.

The essay's central weakness is that it elevates ("revolutionary significance") what is at least as legibly a regression, and it does so by selectively recruiting its evidence. The Castro/Mailer/O'Malley apparatus is doing heavy lifting to dignify the event: the claim that combat-sport masculinity is "anti-imperial and revolutionary" was developed about colonized and racially oppressed populations asserting dignity against an oppressor — transposing it onto a sitting US president, the single most powerful office on earth, hosting a fight on the lawn of the imperial mansion, inverts the very analysis it cites. Trump is not the emasculated colonial subject of O'Malley's argument; he is the state. The essay never confronts that the "anti-elite" frame sits awkwardly on a billionaire incumbent president, and the Butler "Fight! Fight! Fight!" image is offered as authentic populist energy without acknowledging it equally reads as the strongman aesthetic the piece's own McCain foil ("lose with dignity") was meant to warn against. Schmitz's strongest line — that this was "not bread and circuses ... but part of Trump's very own American revolution" — is asserted, not argued; the more obvious reading (spectacle as legitimation, the leader staging dominance) is precisely what "bread and circuses" names, and the essay's denial of it is the claim most in need of defense and least defended.

The strongest opposing view is that the piece mistakes aesthetics for politics: that whether a president likes MMA tells us about brand and affect, not about a transfer of power away from "elite management of society," which has demonstrably not occurred — the managerial state Schmitz says is being displaced still administers the country. A reader sympathetic to the cultural diagnosis could still object that "revolution" is the wrong word for a change in the symbolic register of an unchanged power structure. The essay is intellectually nimble and worth reading as a mood-reading, but its thesis is a category claim (ritual = revolution) that it gestures at rather than earns, and its anti-elite framing is in tension with the elite incumbency of its subject. Not fundamentally flawed in its empirical claims (the typology and the event are real), but its central interpretive judgment — that the fight is "revolutionary" rather than spectacular legitimation — is the contestable hinge, and it is the part the essay leaves unsupported.

↩ Back to TOC

Why Military Was at the UFC White House Event

Ken Klippenstein · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Press coverage of the White House "UFC Freedom 250" event that framed it as a question of taste (cage matches on the South Lawn, a vulgar remark about Michelle Obama) while ignoring what Klippenstein sees as the actual story — the US military's pervasive participation.

Ken Klippenstein, a national-security investigative blogger, catalogs roughly a dozen military elements at the event: an Armed Forces Full Honor Cordon (a ceremony normally reserved for visiting heads of state and dead presidents) for the fighters' weigh-in, the Marine Corps Silent Drill Platoon, a joint color guard, the 160-piece Marine Band playing walkout music, the Old Guard Fife and Drum Corps, the Joint Armed Forces Chorus, Thunderbirds and Blue Angels flyovers as Trump saluted, and some 1,200 active-duty and reserve troops filling the seats. His argument is that this violated both the letter and spirit of military apolitical norms: Pentagon Directive 1344.10 bars associating the Defense Department with partisan politics, and the Joint Ethics Regulation bars lending the armed forces' name, uniforms, or insignia to endorse a private enterprise (the UFC). He rebuts the two standard defenses — that Trump as commander-in-chief makes ceremonial use legitimate, and that it was a recruiting event — as unconvincing, arguing the military "let itself be used." He contrasts this with 2020, when Gen. Mark Milley publicly apologized for merely appearing at Trump's Lafayette Square photo-op, and notes that not a single current or former senior officer has criticized the UFC involvement. His closing claim is that the military's pristine public-trust reputation (why there is little outcry over a $1.5 trillion Pentagon budget request) depends on an apolitical image it is now eroding — and that the most alarming part is that the question apparently never occurred to anyone, including a press that did not raise it.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Klippenstein in his characteristic mode: the selection-as-argument move, where the reporting is the enumerated list and the analytical claim is that everyone else looked at the wrong thing. The list is the piece's real value and its strongest element — it is concrete, specific, and converts a vague unease into a documented inventory of which units did what, with the Full Honor Cordon detail (a head-of-state/funeral honor used for a prizefight weigh-in) doing the most persuasive work. The normative frame is well-anchored: he cites the actual governing instruments (Directive 1344.10, the Joint Ethics Regulation) rather than gesturing at norms in the abstract, and the Milley/Lafayette Square contrast is an effective internal benchmark — the same institution that recoiled from a photo-op in 2020 is silent now, which is genuine evidence of a shifted baseline.

The piece reads cleanly against Klippenstein's pattern (national-security accountability, anti-institutional, attentive to the militarization of civic life), and its sharpest insight is the closing inversion: the danger is not the outrage but the absence of it — "the military is so embedded in American political life that we don't even notice anymore." That is a real and non-obvious claim, and it connects to a defensible structural point about how the military's polled trustworthiness functions as political capital that insulates its budget from scrutiny.

The honest weaknesses are tonal and analytical rather than factual. First, the argument elides a distinction it should engage: ceremonial military participation in presidential events (color guards, the Marine Band, flyovers at the national anthem) is genuinely routine and long-predates Trump, so the strongest version of the opposing case is not "the C-in-C says so" but "which of these dozen elements actually crossed the partisan/commercial line versus which are standard presidential pageantry?" Klippenstein lumps the Full Honor Cordon (a real anomaly) together with a color guard and the national anthem (largely routine), which weakens the inventory by inflating it; a more discriminating list would be more damning. Second, the UFC-as-private-enterprise endorsement point is the legally novel part and arguably his best line of attack, but he develops it less than the partisanship point. Third, the prose leans on contempt ("too wimpy to enter the Octagon of integrity," the January 6 dig at the National Guard) in places where the catalog already carries the argument; the editorializing adds heat without adding evidence and makes it easier for a skeptical reader to discount the substantive core. The piece is strongest read as a documented norm-erosion datapoint and weakest where it treats every uniform present as equally scandalous.

↩ Back to TOC

A Jewish Frenchman’s Case for Christian Nationalism

Michael C. Behrent · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The forthcoming English translation of Éric Zemmour's 2014 book The Suicide of France, and the puzzle of why Zemmour — a Jewish, Berber-descended French nationalist who does not believe in Christ's resurrection — insists Catholicism is integral to French identity. Michael C. Behrent, a historian of French thought at Appalachian State, draws on an email interview with Zemmour to reconstruct his "catholicisme du dehors" ("Catholicism from the outside") — defending a faith's social and cultural value without practicing it. Zemmour (a far-right journalist who founded the party Reconquête! and ran for president in 2022) defines France as "Judeo-Christian," excluding Islam from the civilizational nexus he says Judaism and Christianity share, and positions Catholic-Jewish solidarity against twin enemies: a borderless globalizing elite and Islam. Behrent shows the intellectual scaffolding: Simone Weil's distinction between Catholicism (law, order, institution) and Christianity (universal love), which Zemmour inverts to embrace Catholicism-as-order while disowning Christian universalism, which he blames for the left's "insane machine for loving the Other." The deeper source is Ernest Renan — the nineteenth-century historian (author of the scandalous Life of Jesus, which called Christ an "incomparable man" rather than divine) whose 1882 lecture defined the nation not by race, language, or religion but as a "daily plebiscite," a chosen project resting on a shared legacy of memories. Zemmour also draws on antisemitism-tinged nationalists Maurice Barrès ("the earth and the dead") and Charles Maurras (royalism plus Catholicism-as-social-order), and on René Girard's mimetic theory to explain Catholic hatred of Jews as rivalrous imitation of an older faith the monarchy modeled itself upon. Behrent's diagnosis: Zemmour's is a constructivist, almost modernist nationalism — "Catholicism minus Christianity" (Huxley's line on Comte's positivism) — in which faith functions as cultural cement rather than belief. But the analysis surfaces a central instability Behrent presses on directly: Zemmour qualifies "there is no France without Catholicism" so heavily (no belief in the resurrection, Catholicism reduced to institution and order) that critics may judge the Christianity has been emptied out of his Christian nationalism, leaving an instrumentalized faith whose exclusion of Islam rests on assertion rather than the order-and-law criteria Islam would also satisfy.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is the strongest of the week's Compact pieces and a genuine departure from predictable post-liberal copy: Behrent is a scholar-translator of French thought writing about a French subject he plainly finds repellent in his politics but fascinating in his intellectual architecture, and the result is analysis rather than advocacy. The venue tendency would be to present Zemmour's Judeo-Christian nationalism approvingly as a model for the Anglophone right; Behrent instead does the harder thing — he reconstructs the argument at full strength, sources it precisely (Weil, Renan, Barrès, Maurras, Taine, Girard), and lets its internal contradictions emerge from the reconstruction rather than from editorializing. The Renan thread is the analytical payoff: showing that Zemmour, who can "talk like Barrès," whose nationalism sounds blood-and-soil ("the earth and the dead"), in fact rests on Renan's explicitly anti-racial, constructivist "daily plebiscite" is a real interpretive insight, and it reframes Zemmour from atavist to a kind of paradoxical modernist who wants a manufactured-but-binding shared past. The Weil inversion (Christian-not-Catholic flipped to Catholic-not-Christian) is the cleanest single key to the whole position.

The essay's honesty about the load-bearing instability is what makes it credible. Behrent repeatedly presses the question that breaks the position — "How Christian is Zemmour's cultural Catholicism?" — and surfaces the damning self-quotation ("I do not believe in Christ's resurrection ... but ... one cannot be French without being deeply steeped in Catholicism"). The Huxley epigram, "Catholicism minus Christianity," is devastating precisely because Behrent earns it through reconstruction rather than asserting it. He also lands the sharpest internal contradiction directly: Behrent tells Zemmour that Islam has the very qualities he prizes in Catholicism — order, primacy of law, respect for tradition — and Zemmour's reply (the Renan quip about pork and dogma; the claim that the Christian world accepts a human Jesus while Islam does not) does not actually meet the objection. If Catholicism is to be defended as institution-and-order rather than as truth, the order-based exclusion of Islam collapses, and Zemmour is left defending a civilizational boundary on grounds his own framework cannot supply. Behrent sees this and lets it stand exposed.

The strongest opposing view — which Behrent the analyst would likely grant — is that reconstructing a position this carefully risks dignifying it, and that the Girard-inflected reading of Catholic antisemitism as "admiration" and "rivalry" can soften a record of expulsion and persecution into something almost flattering (the monarchy's borrowing of Davidic anointing presented as creative tension rather than supersessionist appropriation). A reader could also object that the essay's elegant framing of Zemmour-as-modernist understates the operative political function: whatever its Renanian pedigree, the "Judeo-Christian France" frame exists to license the exclusion of French Muslims, and the intellectual genealogy is partly a respectability-laundering of that aim. But Behrent does not hide this — the piece's last third is explicit that Zemmour's Judeo-Christianity "informs his view that Islam can have no place in French society" — so the essay equips the reader to draw the harsher conclusion the author's restraint declines to state. As intellectual history of a dangerous figure, it is exemplary; it neither flatters nor caricatures, and it identifies the exact joint at which the argument fails.

↩ Back to TOC

Coleman Hughes: The Case for Progressive Patriotism

Coleman Hughes · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the difficulty progressives have with American patriotism around the country's 250th birthday (indexed by the recurring July 4 circulation of Frederick Douglass's 1852 "What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?" and by polls showing 92% of Republicans but only 36% of Democrats are "extremely or very" proud to be American). Hughes — a centrist Black writer associated with colorblind/anti-identitarian arguments — opens by dismantling Nikole Hannah-Jones's 1619 Project lead essay as a "bizarre and twisted" backdoor patriotism: its claim that Black Americans "fought back alone" to make the founding ideals real erases white and Jewish abolitionists and civil-rights workers (Garrison, the Grimké sisters, the disproportionately Jewish NAACP founders and Freedom Summer volunteers, the murdered Goodman and Schwerner) and rests, he says, on "pure racial chauvinism." He then diagnoses why progressives struggle with patriotism: their framework treats societal wealth, law and order, and political rights as expected defaults requiring no celebration, while every shortfall "cries out for a special explanation." His affirmative case rests on revealed preference and comparison-to-reality rather than comparison-to-utopia: America is the world's most-desired migrant destination (Gallup 2025: 15% of would-be migrants choose it, vs. 9% Canada and 5% Germany; 24% of sub-Saharan and 28% of Latin American aspirants), and on the metrics progressives value it outperforms peers — a million-résumé callback meta-analysis finding Europe more discriminatory against Black applicants than the US, the US electing a Black head of state before most Latin American neighbors, and far greater LGBT tolerance and absence of anti-Black slurs than much of Africa and the Middle East. He closes by rebutting the famous Newsroom "greatest country" monologue as "a dumb person's idea of a smart argument," arguing international rankings compare apples to oranges and miss the "larger river" of overall societal flourishing into which they feed.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

The piece's strongest move is methodological: the "compare America to the world as it actually is, not to an imagined utopia" frame is a genuinely useful corrective to a real progressive habit (treating the absence of perfection as evidence of failure), and the revealed-preference argument — "people vote with their feet" — is a clean way to make the patriotic case in terms a progressive should accept on its own premises, since it privileges the actual choices of exactly the groups (the poor, people of color, the global South) progressives say they center. The comparative evidence is well-chosen for that audience: callback studies, head-of-state precedent, and tolerance surveys are all metrics drawn from the progressive value-set rather than imposed from outside, which is what makes the argument rhetorically disciplined rather than merely a conservative talking-point in progressive clothing. The 1619 critique's central empirical point — that the "fought back alone" claim is false, and demonstrably so given the documented role of white and Jewish abolitionists and civil-rights workers — is correct and fairly damaging to that specific essay's framing.

The argument's principal weakness is a recurring asymmetry in its comparative method that mirrors, in reverse, the progressive bias it diagnoses. Hughes faults progressives for treating American goods as "expected" and American faults as needing "special explanation," but his own affirmative case systematically benchmarks the US against worse performers (Europe's higher callback discrimination, Latin America's lack of a Black head of state, the Middle East's anti-Black slurs, Africa's LGBT intolerance) while declining to benchmark against better ones — a "compared to whom" that is selected to flatter. Revealed-preference-by-migration is also weaker than it looks: people migrate toward wealth, opportunity, and existing diaspora networks, which a country can possess for reasons (size, geography, the dollar's reserve status, historical accident) largely orthogonal to whether its culture is "a force for good" — the very causal claim Hughes wants the data to license. The leap from "most-desired destination" to "Americanism is a force for good" needs the intervening step that why people want to come tracks American virtue rather than American scale, and the piece asserts rather than establishes it. The Newsroom-monologue rebuttal is rhetorically effective but partly evasive: dismissing rankings as apples-to-oranges and invoking an unmeasured "larger river" of flourishing is itself a move toward the unfalsifiable — it lets the author wave away unfavorable metrics ("22nd in science … does not tell you anything") while keeping favorable ones (migration share, callback gaps). This is not a fundamental flaw — the descriptive claims are largely accurate and the core reframe is sound — but the comparative case is built on a selectively favorable choice of comparators, and a reader should treat "best country by revealed preference" as a contestable inference rather than the demonstrated conclusion the piece presents.

↩ Back to TOC

Leonard Cohen’s Dark Faith

M.G. Scott · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The author's personal inheritance of the Leonard Cohen "mythos" — Cohen's friendship with her grandfather, the poet and ex-diplomat Peter Dale Scott (coiner of the term "deep state") — and the question of how to reckon with the darkness at the core of Cohen's art and faith. M.G. Scott, an actress and writer, narrates the two men's relationship from its origins in 1950s Montreal, where both belonged to the literary circle around F.R. Scott (Peter's father, a McGill law dean and co-founder of Canada's first social-democratic party) alongside mentors like Irving Layton. The essay traces their divergence — Cohen to Hydra, drugs, and rock stardom (the "yin path" of mysticism and the irrational), Peter to Berkeley, Dante, the Free Speech Movement, and CIA-drug-war scholarship (the "yang path" of Enlightenment rationalism) — using Peter's own taxonomy of Western culture as a yin/yang dialectic in which "when something is progressing, something else is regressing." After forty years' estrangement they reconnected in the late 1990s. The emotional center is their final poetic exchange in October 2016, days before Cohen's death: against Cohen's album You Want It Darker (whose title track invokes "Hineini," Abraham's "Here I am" before the demanded sacrifice of Isaac), Peter sent a poem insisting "I have always wanted it lighter / And I think God does too," and Cohen replied with what may be his last poem ("who says 'i' want it darker?"), depicting a survivor "saved in your harbor / while millions died at sea" and a God who "will make it darker / he will make it light / according to his torah / which leonard did not write." Scott reads Cohen's faith as a Jewish submission to an inscrutable, even violent divine power (the Bat Kol, the divine voice) — bleak but not nihilistic — contrasted with Peter's "postsecular," Merton-inflected refusal to deny darkness while declining to surrender to it ("I am suggesting that each of us respond to voices that shape the reality that we want to live in").

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a small, genuinely affecting cultural-biographical essay, and it should be judged on what it sets out to do — illuminate the character of one artist's faith through an intimate relationship — not on argumentative rigor it never claims. Its singular asset is access: the previously unpublished (or little-known) final poetic correspondence between Cohen and Peter Dale Scott is the kind of primary material no amount of criticism can substitute for, and the author's first-hand inheritance ("over Chinese food on Tuesday evenings") gives the piece a texture and authority of intimacy that earns the personal framing rather than indulging it. The yin/yang and "ethogeny" apparatus is borrowed wholesale from her grandfather's idiosyncratic cosmology, and the essay is honest that it is his frame, not a neutral one — which is the right move, since it presents the lens as one man's way of seeing rather than as an established interpretive key.

The reading of Cohen's "dark faith" is the strongest interpretive content: Scott correctly resists the easy "godfather of gloom" caricature and locates Cohen's darkness in a specifically Jewish theological posture — the "Hineini" of Abraham at the binding of Isaac, submission to a God whose demands exceed comprehension — rather than in mere aesthetic morbidity or despair. That the title-track "Hineini" rhymes Cohen's imminent death with the Akedah is a real reading, and the survivor's-guilt note in his last poem ("god saved you in your harbor / while millions died at sea," set against the Holocaust shadow that hangs over the 1950s Montreal milieu) gives the darkness a moral rather than merely moody content. The essay is at its best when it lets the two men's actual words carry the contrast — Cohen's submission against Peter's "I have always wanted it lighter" — rather than glossing them.

The structural weakness is the one the essay half-acknowledges: by routing Cohen so completely through Peter, it sometimes tells us more about the grandfather's framework than about Cohen himself, and the yin/yang scheme is asserted as profound rather than tested — it is capacious enough to absorb any observation, which makes it more a mood than an analysis. A skeptic's strongest objection is that the piece risks a familiar move in artist-hagiography: aestheticizing real moral darkness (Cohen's predatory adolescent hypnosis anecdote, his "notoriously polygynous" life) into "generative dialectic," such that the darkness becomes evidence of depth rather than something to reckon with morally. Scott flags this tension explicitly in the close — Peter's worry that Cohen's permissive darkness ("if the world is so dark and there is no settled order ... then you can live the life that Leonard lived") was license as much as wisdom — which is the essay's most intellectually serious moment and keeps it from collapsing into pure veneration. No fundamental flaw; the personal-source access is the value, and the author is appropriately candid that the interpretive frame is inherited, not proven.

↩ Back to TOC

The Death and Life of American Exceptionalism

Steven Watts · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: The approaching 250th anniversary of US independence (July 4, 2026) and Jean Twenge's Atlantic data showing the collapse of "American exceptionalism" — the belief that the US holds a unique world-historical place as a beacon of liberty — among the young. Steven Watts, an emeritus historian at the University of Missouri, traces the idea from its near-universal acceptance (endorsed by figures from George Washington to FDR, with even critics like Frederick Douglass-era reformers and Martin Luther King demanding the country fulfill rather than abandon its ideals) through its post-1960s repudiation by the left. He locates the founding tension in the late-life Adams–Jefferson correspondence: Jefferson's expansive "empire of liberty" optimism versus John Adams's worry that human selfishness and faction would subvert the republic. He narrates the creed's twentieth-century peak (Wilson, FDR, Cold War presidents, the 1960 Nixon–Kennedy contest, Reagan's "shining city") and then its dismantling — Vietnam and Watergate disillusionment, Daniel Bell's 1975 "End of American Exceptionalism," Chomsky's "rogue state," and the historians' turn toward social, transnational, and oppression-centered narratives (Howard Zinn's People's History, the 1619 Project). Citing polls showing agreement that the US "system of doing things is still the best" fell from 67 percent (early 1980s) to 27 percent (2022), and 40 percent of Zoomers calling the Founders villains, he concedes real erosion but rejects wholesale dismissal as "historical amnesia," pointing to the orderly American Revolution (versus France's and Russia's bloodbaths), immigration demand, and the defeats of Nazism and Soviet communism. His constructive proposal is Lincoln's "chastened" exceptionalism — the "almost chosen people" under providential judgment, with the Civil War as penalty for slavery — fusing Jeffersonian hope and Adamsian skepticism into a blessing that must be continually earned.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is a competent, gracefully written work of intellectual-history synthesis, and as an essay it should be judged on coherence and explanatory reach rather than on novel data — Watts is restating a familiar declension narrative (golden-age creed → 1960s rupture → academic dismantling → contemporary youth disaffection) and his contribution is the Lincoln synthesis at the close, not the survey that precedes it. On its own terms the Lincoln move is the strongest part: by routing exceptionalism through the Second Inaugural's theology of judgment ("the almost chosen people," the war as penalty for slavery), Watts genuinely answers the essay's framing question — he offers a version of the creed that can absorb the very catalogue of national sins (slavery, conquest, imperial adventure) that the left deploys against it, because in Lincoln's frame those sins are exactly what Providence judges. That makes the piece more interesting than the predictable Compact-adjacent register would suggest; it is closer to a chastened liberal patriotism than to post-liberal triumphalism, and it explicitly disowns the "chest-thumping" manifest-destiny strain.

The structural weakness is that the surrounding survey does the rhetorical work the argument should do, and it loads the dice. Watts presents the post-1960s critique almost entirely through its least careful spokespeople and most contestable claims — Chomsky's "rogue state," AOC's "concentration camps," the 1619 Project's most-criticized framing — while the exceptionalist tradition is represented by its most eloquent (Lincoln, Tocqueville, Webster). The transnational-history turn he treats as ideological capitulation ("the historiographical coup de grâce") was substantially a methodological argument about comparative and connective analysis, not simply a verdict that America is "no better, no worse"; collapsing method into politics lets him dismiss it without engaging it. Most tellingly for a historian, the empirical defense — orderly revolution, immigration demand, defeating two tyrannies — is asserted rather than weighed against the counter-ledger (the Revolution preserved slavery the French and Haitian revolutions attacked; immigration tracks economic opportunity and proximity more than creedal magnetism; the Cold War "victory" included coups and proxy wars). The piece never tests whether these facts establish that the US "stands apart" or merely that it has been powerful and prosperous, which is the actual hinge of the dispute.

The strongest opposing view is the one Watts gestures at and then waves off: that "chastened exceptionalism" is unfalsifiable in a way that drains it of content. If the nation's triumphs prove its special destiny and its atrocities prove Providence is judging a chosen people, then no historical fact can ever count against the thesis — every outcome confirms it. Lincoln could hold that paradox because he believed in the judging God literally; an emeritus historian invoking "Providence" as a national narrative device in 2026 is borrowing the consolation of the theology without its accountability. The essay is most persuasive read not as historical argument but as a civic exhortation for the semiquincentennial — and on that register, as a plea for a humble, self-critical national story rather than either triumphalism or repudiation, it lands.

↩ Back to TOC

How America lost the art of association

Luke Burgis · 2026-06-16 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the loneliness epidemic and decline of American civic life; adapted from the author's new book. Luke Burgis (a writer who interprets culture through René Girard's mimetic theory — the idea that human desire is imitated, "borrowed" from others — and author of "Wanting") opens from Tocqueville's claim that in democracies "the science of association is the mother science," then catalogs the decline: fewer congregations, clubs, unions, and lodges; fewer close friends; record time spent alone; a Surgeon General's loneliness emergency. He frames this as "the one and the 99" — an unnatural direct relationship between the individual and a mass, accentuated because platforms connect "the one" to millions while hollowing out the mediating associations (friend groups, congregations) that once buffered the encounter, leaving "the self set against the state" with no intermediary institutions. Drawing on Václav Havel's 1978 samizdat essay "The Power of the Powerless" and its image of the greengrocer who hangs a "WORKERS OF THE WORLD, UNITE!" sign not from belief but as the path of least resistance, Burgis defines "post-totalitarianism" as voluntary performative conformity, which he equates with unchecked "social contagion" — the unconscious transmission of belief and desire (a Girardian/mimetic claim) that today takes the form of the reflexive repost or the slogan-in-bio. Citing mid-century sociologist Robert Nisbet ("it is in the basic associations of men that the real consequences of political power reveal themselves"), he argues the crisis of modern politics is "not too much government, but too little community." The prescription, drawn from Havel's Charter 77 (a 1977 Czech dissident moral fellowship with 240 initial signatories), is to build modest, accountable associations — invoking the Oxford Inklings (the small writers' circle including C.S. Lewis and Tolkien) and Jesus's twelve — large enough to test ideas against resistance, intimate enough that "your association will notice if you stop coming."

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

This is Burgis applying his standard interpretive engine — Girardian mimetic desire, here renamed "social contagion" — to the well-worn Bowling Alone terrain of declining American associational life, and the synthesis is the essay's genuine value-add. The strongest move is the structural diagnosis of the missing middle: that the danger of platform-mediated life is not connection per se but the dissolution of the layer between the individual and the mass, leaving the self "naked and unmediated" before both the crowd and the state. The stadium metaphor — losing your friends in a crowd of 100,000 and instantly becoming "a stranger in an anonymous crowd" — is an unusually concrete rendering of an abstract point, and the Nisbet citation does real work connecting atomization to political vulnerability rather than just to private unhappiness. The reframe of Havel's greengrocer for the social-media age (the sign migrates into "the reflexive repost, the slogan in the bio") is apt and earns its place.

The structural weakness is a category-slide that the essay never quite owns. Havel's "post-totalitarianism" described a specific apparatus — a one-party state that had replaced overt terror with ritualized public lying, where the greengrocer's sign was delivered with the onions by enterprise headquarters. Burgis ports the term wholesale onto contemporary liberal democracies where the "pressure to perform" comes from peers and algorithms, not a Politburo, and where defection carries social cost but not a secret-police file. That is a real and interesting phenomenon, but calling it "post-totalitarianism" inflates voluntary conformism into something with the moral weight of life under the Husák regime, and the equation "post-totalitarianism is simply social contagion left unchecked" quietly does the heavy lifting — it lets every instance of online conformity inherit the menace of a dissident's Prague. The high-blood-pressure metaphor (treat the disease with the "exercise" of association rather than medication) is rhetorically tidy but obscures that some of what he calls contagion is just ordinary social influence, which no amount of small-group fellowship eliminates and which is not obviously pathological.

The strongest opposing view is that the prescription, while admirable, is under-powered against the diagnosis. If the problem is genuinely structural — platforms engineered to connect the one to the millions, congregations and unions hollowed out by decades of economic and technological change — then "go build a small fellowship" is a counsel of individual virtue against a systemic force, the associational equivalent of telling someone with hypertension to exercise while ignoring the diet the whole society is on. Burgis gestures at this (his point that intermediary institutions atrophied is structural) but his way out is entirely voluntarist and small-scale: Charter 77, the Inklings, the twelve. Those examples also flatter the thesis by selection — they are the famous fellowships that worked and changed history; the vastly more numerous small associations that dissolved without trace don't appear, so "the most transformative associations are rarely massive at the start" is true but survivorship-biased. The essay is a good, humane, well-read piece of cultural criticism whose conceptual machinery (Tocqueville, Havel, Nisbet, Girard) is more impressive than its accounting for why the structures it mourns collapsed — and therefore than its confidence that they can be rebuilt by will. Not fundamentally flawed, but the "post-totalitarianism" framing is the load-bearing overreach a careful reader should discount.

↩ Back to TOC

Waiting For The Miracle

Scott Alexander · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary — Alexander's long-running personal investigation into the 1917 Fatima "sun miracle" (where ~100,000 pilgrims reported the sun spinning and changing color), which he says is the only well-documented case of tens of thousands of people simultaneously reporting the same impossible event. Alexander notes the phenomenon recurs at Medjugorje, Bosnia, where six visionaries have reported daily 6:40 apparitions of the Virgin Mary since 1981, so he and his wife traveled there hoping to settle whether the sun miracle is real or hallucinated. He summarizes the Medjugorje backstory from visionary Mirjana Soldo's memoir (the apparitions, Communist persecution, the village's transformation into a wealthy pilgrimage-tourism economy, the visionaries' later millionaire status, and several theological "heresies" and embarrassments that count as evidence against). His attempt to gather statistics on who sees the miracle failed (pilgrims hate being accosted), so he fell back on staring at the sun himself. His wife — an unusually skilled lifelong meditator — saw the miracle vividly while Alexander, who is constitutionally resistant to hallucination, saw almost nothing; this divergence let them "triangulate." Their resulting four-step mechanism: (1) the sun must sit in a narrow brightness band (~10,000 nits) where surrounding bright clouds make it appear dim enough to stare at but it remains bright enough to damage; (2) it produces a strong afterimage superimposed on itself, read not as an afterimage but as the sun flashing colors; (3) involuntary eye movements (microsaccades and drift) smear that afterimage into a writhing, multicolored halo that the visual system interprets as spinning; (4) prolonged UV exposure causes "chromatopsia" and retinal-bleaching color sequences ("flight of colors"/Plateau's Sequence), explaining Fatima's whole-world color-tinting. He then rebuts five objections (midday vs. evening conditions, absence of reported afterimages/eye-pain at Fatima, distant witnesses, the profusion of other reported phenomena, and the fine-tuning/predicted-in-advance problem), the last by arguing such cloud conditions occur every few weeks but people almost never stare at the sun long enough except when primed to expect a miracle. He confirms it later replicated the effect in his own California backyard. He concedes the rotation step is his weakest ("a non-explanation") and admits the final theory is essentially "afterimages and eye damage" dressed in epicycles.

↩ Back to TOC

Claude's Response:

As a long-form rationalist set-piece this is Alexander at near his best: a genuinely hard anomaly (mass simultaneous identical perception, which most debunking frames handle poorly), approached with self-experiment, a falsifiable mechanistic model, and a serious attempt to pre-empt the strongest objections rather than the weakest. The argument's spine — that the phenomenon is a perceptual artifact requiring a specific stimulus condition (dim-but-damaging sun) plus a behavioral trigger (sustained staring, which essentially never happens except when a crowd is primed to expect a miracle) — is the key insight, because it simultaneously explains why the effect is real (lots of consistent reports), why it's not supernatural (it's reproducible eye physiology), and why it clusters at predicted times and places (priming, not the divine, supplies the staring). The decisive piece of internal evidence is the wife-versus-Alexander asymmetry: a true external optical event should look the same to two people standing together, whereas a true contagious-suggestion hallucination should have spread when she announced it; neither happened, which points squarely at an idiosyncratic per-observer perceptual process. That's a clean, almost experimental discrimination between hypotheses, and the later unprompted backyard replication strengthens it.

The honest limits are the ones Alexander names. The rotation/spinning step is admittedly hand-waved ("the general tendency of the visual system to interpret complex radial stimuli as rotating"), and the Fatima color-tinting is stitched together from two distinct ophthalmological phenomena (UV chromatopsia and retinal-bleaching color sequences) that he concedes he can't cleanly say co-occur — he leans on "staring at the sun for ten minutes is outside the domain ophthalmology usually studies" to license the combination, which is a plausibility argument, not a demonstration. The Beer-Lambert optical-depth digression, sourced to LLM output, is doing real work in the Objection-1 rebuttal (explaining how midday sun after a rainstorm could be dimmed to stare-able levels by an unnoticed thin moisture layer); it's quantitatively reasonable but is the kind of borrowed-authority block a reader should treat as illustrative rather than verified. And the appeal to eyewitness-confabulation literature to dismiss the "too much other weird stuff" (Virgin-in-the-sun, sun zooming earthward, clothes drying) is legitimate but conveniently selective — it lets the theory keep the parts it explains (spinning, color) and discard the rest as stress-induced confabulation.

Crucially, none of this rises to a fundamental flaw, because Alexander's claims are appropriately scoped to match his evidence-mode. He is not asserting a proven physical model; he is offering a "working hypothesis" that the phenomenon belongs to "the same family" as known afterimage/chromatopsia effects, and he repeatedly invites perceptual psychologists to improve the rotation account. The strongest opposing view — that a purely afterimage-based theory was rejected early on precisely because it seemed too trivial to move thousands of sophisticated witnesses, and that the model only survives by accreting enough special conditions (exact nit range, exact cloud optical depth, priming, prolonged staring, individual perceptual idiosyncrasy) to be near-unfalsifiable — is one Alexander explicitly raises against himself ("I've added so many lovely epicycles"). His reply, that the conditions are independently plausible and that he reproduced the effect twice, is reasonable. The piece earns its conclusion: not a debunking by assertion, but a mechanistic candidate explanation that pays its way against the predict-in-advance and mass-witness features that make the miracle genuinely interesting.

↩ Back to TOC

Standalone Pieces

Title Author Publication Summary
Never Cross a River Four Feet Deep on Average Scott Alexander ACT Putilin shows a hyped 2023 brain-entrainment learning study fails to replicate and was probably never real — its headline effect driven by a few bored participants and obscured by averaging.
Does anything I write matter anymore? Noah Smith Noahpinion Smith reflects that punditry's influence is waning along three axes — populism makes leaders deaf to ideas, Substack monetization silos writers away from each other, and AI's flood of good-enough content reduces every post to skimmable noise — while holding out hope that writing "for the AIs" is a new channel of influence.
What New York used to be Matthew Yglesias Slow Boring In a wide-ranging mailbag, Yglesias argues the Sanders/Mamdani left has won the discourse and personnel battles while achieving almost nothing in durable policy, and that polarization is a real, elite-driven constraint that justifies partisan voting heuristics except in low-stakes local races.
EU eases rules on GMO plants: a good move? Euro|topics (curated debate) Euro|topics (discovery) Euro|topics aggregates French and German press on the EU Parliament deregulating gene-edited (NGT) crops, splitting along a competitiveness/innovation-versus-precaution axis: French papers cheer it as a strategic-autonomy paradigm shift, German papers split between cautious approval and an ignored-ecological-risk warning.
Grading the internet’s dating advice Matthew Yglesias Slow Boring In a podcast transcript, Yglesias and Jerusalem Demsas debate online dating advice — he argues liberals' aversion to stereotypes cedes "practical advice-giving" to right-wing figures, she counters that gender-stereotype-based advice is both unkind and ineffective because you date individuals, not averages.

Never Cross a River Four Feet Deep on Average

Scott Alexander · 2026-06-16 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: Original commentary — a guest post by 2024 ACX Grants recipient Sasha Putilin reporting his $32,000 funded attempt to replicate the 2023 study "Learning at your brain's rhythm: individualized entrainment boosts learning for perceptual decisions" (Michael & Kourtzi). The original study claimed that reading a person's individual peak alpha frequency (the 8–12 Hz brain rhythm) and flashing light at that frequency timed to the rhythm's trough ("T-match" condition) made them learn a noisy radial-vs-concentric pattern-discrimination task three times faster — implying a buildable "learning helmet." Putilin replicated it on a tight budget: 12 London volunteers instead of 80, a ~$2,000 consumer OpenBCI headset instead of a $50k–$100k 63-channel rig, and a within-participant crossover design (each subject got T-match one day, P-match the next). The headline 3× effect did not replicate; if anything the P-match group learned faster on Day 1. Disaggregating the original per-participant data revealed the effect was fragile — removing any one of 17 of 40 data points pushed it past p=0.05 — and was driven almost entirely by a few P-match participants with sharply negative "learning rates" (people who got worse, most likely from boredom/fatigue on the 800-trial task), who by coincidence all landed in the comparison group. The authors had obscured this by reporting group-averaged learning rates and curve-fitting through cross-participant-averaged accuracies, making a few-outlier artifact look like a uniform group effect. Putilin generalizes to "cargo-cult statistics" (ritually performing t-tests without interrogating the underlying data) and argues AI now lets amateurs cheaply re-audit published papers, forecasting a more "democratic" meta-science. He is careful to call the obscuring most likely unintentional, notes the original authors stopped responding once he sent results, and concedes he too missed the flaw before spending $32k.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is a strong, intellectually honest piece of meta-science, and its central empirical claim — that the original effect is an averaging artifact driven by a handful of disengaged participants — is well-supported by the fragility analysis (17/40 points individually decisive) and the exclusion test (dropping large-negative learning rates collapses the 3× difference to p≈0.44). The crossover design is a genuine improvement over the original's between-groups-only analysis, and replicating on cheap consumer hardware doubles as a real-world test of the "buildable learning device" premise, which is the actual reason anyone cared. The reasoning chain holds together: the effect was fragile, the fragility traces to specific outliers, the outliers have a mundane explanation (boredom), and the replication with the same task on different people produced no such outliers. That's about as clean a "probably not real" verdict as a 12-person replication can deliver.

The piece's honesty is its best feature. Putilin foregrounds his own limitations — the tiny n, the cheaper headset, the several procedural deviations (longer feedback duration, breaks every 100 rather than 200 trials, a widened alpha band, a variable-refresh monitor that may have produced cleaner flicker) — and the appendix is unusually candid about which deviations were intentional versus accidental. Crucially, a skeptic's best counterargument is anticipated and partly conceded: with only 12 participants and meaningful methodological drift, a failure to replicate is itself weak evidence; the replication could have washed out a real-but-small effect through ceiling effects (both groups maxed out by Day 2) or imprecise consumer-grade IAF estimation. Putilin doesn't lean on the null replication alone — he leans on the re-analysis of the original data, which is the more decisive contribution and doesn't depend on his sample at all. That sequencing is what keeps the conclusion credible.

Where the piece overreaches is the closing "democratic meta-science with AI" thesis, which is a sociological extrapolation rather than a demonstrated claim. The case study shows that one motivated grantee, given funding, hundreds of hours, and an LLM for data wrangling, can credibly audit a paper whose data was (however imperfectly) public — but that's a long way from "your local blogger may have better meta-science skills than top universities" as a general phenomenon. The bottleneck the post itself documents — the original authors' incomplete, poorly-labelled data and unreleased code — is precisely the obstacle that AI does not dissolve; Putilin still couldn't fully rebuild their charts and had to approximate. The strongest version of the optimistic thesis is narrower and more defensible: AI lowers the cost of re-analyzing already-released data enough that post-publication scrutiny becomes economical for non-specialists, which raises the expected cost of publishing fragile work. That's a real and worthwhile claim; the priesthood-versus-network rhetoric inflates it past what the evidence here carries.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Does anything I write matter anymore?

Noah Smith · 2026-06-19 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: original meta-commentary — Smith's self-examination of whether opinion writing still matters, prompted by a felt decline in his own influence over the past two years. Smith — economist-turned-full-time-blogger — first restates why he chose blogging as the "highest-leverage" path: it lets him inject ideas into the discourse with speed and breadth (invoking Keynes on "madmen in authority… distilling their frenzy from some academic scribbler") and embeds him in a cross-disciplinary "republic of letters." He then names three forces eroding that influence. (1) Populism: with Trump's second administration run as "a cult of personality" rather than an intellectual movement (tariffs he credits to no adviser, MAHA antivax, the Iran war), "there's no one to argue with, because nothing that's happening is based on an argument" — and the left is "fundamentally a factional movement disguised as an ideological one" (Mamdani-style candidates listening only to their clique). (2) Monetization: Substack's email-distribution model rewards serving paying subscribers over talking to other writers, siloing intellectuals and drying up the cross-pollination from which new ideas actually emerge; he suggests platform fixes (e.g., link-back notifications like 2006-era Blogger). (3) AI: he judges AI writing genuinely good (citing an Asness/Brown/Mendelson "affordability" essay he believes ~50% AI-generated yet "great"), but warns that an effectively infinite supply of good content pushes readers to skim 100 posts rather than read 10 closely, so "even if my blog… make[s] me plenty of money, what I do becomes less important." He ends optimistically: human writing remains valuable as training data for future models ("writing for the AIs," per Cowen), and he notes his own outsized presence in AI-citation rankings.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

This is explicitly meta-commentary about the discourse rather than analysis of the world, which per the project's optimist/synthesis discipline (green shoots must be about the world, not the corpus's own functioning) caps its roundup value: its central subject is the health and influence of punditry itself, the most inward-facing topic a roundup of pundits can contain. The genuinely portable claims are two. The "populism = no one to argue with" diagnosis is sharp and generalizes: Smith's point is not that he disagrees with current policy but that policy has detached from argument entirely (a "cult of personality" on the right, a "factional movement disguised as an ideological one" on the left), which would make persuasion-by-ideas structurally ineffective regardless of the idea's quality. That is a real structural claim about the current political-epistemic environment and is the piece's most carry-worthy observation. The monetization-silo mechanism is also a clean, falsifiable account of an incentive change — Substack rewards serving subscribers over conversing with peers, and ideas emerge from dialogue, so the medium that revived the blogosphere may also be starving it of cross-pollination.

The piece's weaknesses are the ones the assignment anticipated: a good deal of it is self-locating ("top 2% of contributors," Yglesias and Demsas "riffing on my post," Claude citing him) that, however candidly framed as complaint-not-brag, makes the essay's evidence base largely the author's own career trajectory. The AI section is the analytically thinnest — it concedes the worry (skimming, attention fragmentation) and then half-retracts it ("Maybe AI can't make this problem worse because it was already maximally bad"), landing on the consoling "writing for the AIs" idea that is more hope than argument. There is also an unexamined tension Smith doesn't resolve: he laments that monetization siloes writers and that AI floods the zone, yet his proposed personal response (possibly starting a YouTube channel, continuing to monetize, optimizing for AI citation) accepts every one of those incentives rather than resisting them — the essay diagnoses a collective-action problem and then reasons individually within it.

Roundup verdict: skippable-leaning. The two structural claims (argument-detached politics; monetization-driven siloing) are worth a brief mention and could feed a discourse-health or AI-and-knowledge thread, but the piece is substantially navel-gazing with a thin payload for a roundup whose value is perspectives on the world. The composer should treat it as a candidate for the skippable pass or fold its two portable claims into a neighboring AI/discourse theme rather than giving it a standalone slot; on its own it adds little beyond what the Cowen and Dean Ball pieces already establish about AI reshaping the information economy.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

What New York used to be

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: A reader mailbag spanning best NYC songs, whether the Sanders left "won" the post-2016 era, pressuring Graham Platner to drop out, the loneliness crisis, Nate Silver's "vote blue no matter who" critique, Dean Baker on abundance/patents, and Ottoman Empire counterfactuals. Opening on nostalgia, Yglesias — a self-described YIMBY (yes-in-my-backyard pro-development) and abundance believer — picks The Kills' "What New York Used to Be" over the standard New-York-as-destination anthems, reflecting that his Greenwich Village hometown's distinctive features (bohemian cafes, gay visibility) have dissolved precisely because the things that once marked them have become universal; he adds a "cranky middle-aged" defense of Giuliani-era data-driven proactive policing as the durable basis of the 1990s crime drop. On the left's trajectory, his sharpest analytic claim: the left-faction "wins when Democrats lose" (defeats discredit moderation) and won when they governed (Biden staffed and governed further left than expected), yet the actual policy legacy is thin — taxes lower than end-of-Obama, the antitrust push leaving "zero legacy," blue states retreating from climate goals they never implemented, no realized alternative to policing. He concludes "the left is struggling": Mamdani in Gracie Mansion delivers emotional confirmation but little policy change, and replacing liberal Democrats with further-left ones in safe-blue seats is a weak lever, whereas beating Republicans with moderates (John Bel Edwards's Medicaid expansion in Louisiana) is powerful. On Platner, he argues the factional enterprise structurally can't be shamed out (a loss against "establishment whining" serves the cause as well as a win), so only pro-Platner factionalists swapping in Troy Jackson could remove him. On Silver's voting heuristic, he distinguishes "is raw-partisanship-over-candidate a bad heuristic?" (less clearly yes) from "is it bad for America that it's a good heuristic?" (clearly yes), tracing the disappearance of cross-pressured legislators (pro-life Democrats, pro-choice Republicans) and concluding the heuristic rationally tracks reality at the federal level but breaks down in local/state races where majority control isn't at stake. He partly concedes to Dean Baker that prize-based pharma-innovation financing beats patents, but rejects Baker's "if you really cared about deadweight loss" gambit as a reason to deprioritize the far larger housing/transport/energy regulatory stakes. The Ottoman counterfactual argues the empire was a shakier polity than the Habsburgs (poorer, no industrial cities for non-Turks, Turkish nationalism rising, Arabic's prestige making Turkification untenable), so a polyglot Ottoman state would have come under heavy pressure regardless of WWI.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The mailbag's analytic payload is concentrated in three answers, and the strongest is the diagnosis of the left's "heads-I-win-tails-I-win" dynamic decoupled from policy output. Yglesias's claim that the faction has captured discourse and personnel while producing almost no durable policy legacy is a genuinely useful frame, and the specific receipts (taxes below end-of-Obama before the "Big Beautiful Bill," the antitrust push's "zero legacy," blue-state climate retreat) are concrete and falsifiable rather than vibes. The move that gives it teeth is separating emotional wins (Mamdani as "their city" confirmation, analogized to Trump-as-owning-the-libs) from policy wins, which lets him grant the left its cultural ascendancy while denying it the thing it claims to want. This is consistent popularism: the lever that moves policy is beating Republicans with moderates (the Edwards/Medicaid example), not purifying safe-blue seats.

The Silver-heuristic answer is the most intellectually careful thing in the piece: the (1)-vs-(2) distinction (a heuristic can be individually rational and a symptom of something bad for the system) is the right decomposition, and the historical texture (Ralph Hall/Charles Stenholm as conservative Texas Democrats; Waxman-Markey coal-country defectors; Riordan/Giuliani as genuinely-to-the-left-on-some-issues GOP mayors) substantiates the claim that issue-level crossover has evaporated. The honest tension he half-acknowledges: he "constantly argues polarization is an elite-driven choice, not something that just happened," yet his practical advice is to use heuristics that "acknowledge reality" — i.e., to behave as if the elite-driven choice were a natural fact. That's defensible (you optimize within the world you're in) but it sits uneasily with his broader project of treating polarization as a contingent, reversible elite failure; the heuristic he endorses is itself part of the equilibrium that locks polarization in.

The weaker spots are the asides. The Giuliani-policing defense is asserted rather than argued (the data-driven-policing-caused-the-crime-drop claim is genuinely contested in the criminology literature, with national crime trends, lead, demographics, and incarceration all competing for credit), and Yglesias waves it through as "my cranky take" without engaging the identification problem he's so rigorous about in the gifted-education piece. The loneliness answer (tax digital ad revenue and streaming to make infinite-scroll less lucrative; linear TV's monoculture was better) is offered as deliberately "crude" and underdeveloped, and treats a contested causal claim (phones → loneliness) as settled enough to justify Pigouvian intervention. The Ottoman counterfactual is well-informed amateur history (the Bruce Masters citation on Sunni Arab elites' pre-war loyalism is real and apt) but is explicitly recreational. For roundup purposes the load-bearing material is the left-trajectory and voting-heuristic answers; the rest is texture.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

EU eases rules on GMO plants: a good move?

Euro|topics (curated debate) · 2026-06-17 · Read full article ↓

Responding to: the European Parliament's approval of deregulating plants developed via New Genomic Techniques (NGT, i.e., gene editing) — dropping most labelling requirements and environmental assessments for edited plants that could in theory have arisen through conventional breeding (no foreign genes introduced). This Euro|topics curated debate spans four outlets across an enthusiasm-to-caution gradient. France's L'Opinion (Rémi Godeau) is most bullish, framing the move as a triple "decision to be strong" — for competitiveness against rivals with a substantial lead, for self-sufficiency via drought- and disease-resistant crops, and for cutting-edge research — and likening gene-edited plants to "what AI is to the digital world: a paradigm shift." France's Les Echos (David Barroux) endorses it more conditionally, urging trust in the scientific community (while conceding science can err and consensus emerges only over time) and stressing that freedom to innovate is not "carte blanche" because democracies still regulate to limit risk. Germany's Der Tagesspiegel (Ballaschk Martin) welcomes the shift away from "all or nothing" labelling, citing the promise of fewer pesticides/fertilisers and noting skeptical consumers can still opt out since NGT-1 plants remain banned in organic farming. Germany's Die Zeit (Christiane Grefe) dissents, warning the change mainly benefits large companies serving global supply chains and affluent buyers, that releasing gene-edited (including wild) plants into the environment may carry undesirable health/ecological consequences, and that the Commission and the new parliamentary majority "repeatedly and hastily downplayed potential ecological impacts."

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Claude's Response:

The instructive feature of this aggregation is that the dividing line is not the expected pro- versus anti-GMO axis but a competitiveness-and-innovation frame against a precaution-and-distribution frame — and the four excerpts let you watch the same regulatory change get narrated as three different kinds of event. L'Opinion's "decision to be strong" reads it as industrial strategy and European strategic autonomy (the AI analogy is the tell: a race Europe is losing and must enter). Tagesspiegel reads it as a maturation of consumer choice and an environmental win on the pesticide/fertiliser margin, with the organic-farming carve-out as the reassurance that opt-out survives. Die Zeit reads it as a distributional and ecological question — who actually benefits (standardised-product multinationals and affluent consumers of vitamin-enriched produce) and what gets externalised (downplayed environmental risk, including the novel step of editing and releasing wild plants).

The genuinely useful disagreement is between Les Echos and Die Zeit over the role of scientific authority, because they are arguing about epistemics, not just outcomes. Barroux's case rests on deferring to an eventually-converging scientific consensus and treating the lobby-capture worry as oversimplification; Grefe's case is precisely that the process was rushed and the risk assessment politically curtailed ("repeatedly and hastily downplayed"). That is a real, resolvable dispute — whether the deregulation reflected settled science or outran it — and it is the part of the cluster worth carrying, since it generalizes beyond GMOs to how the EU handles any innovation-versus-precaution tradeoff. As a non-Anglophone discovery item the aggregation earns its place by showing the French press treating the same decision as a geopolitical/competitiveness imperative while the German press argues internally about consumer trust versus ecological precaution — a clean illustration of how national regulatory cultures frame an identical EU vote differently.

↩ Back to TOC · ↩ Back to standalones table

Grading the internet’s dating advice

Matthew Yglesias · 2026-06-18 · Read full article ↓
