Why the Iran War Hasn't Crashed the Economy

Matt Stoller · 2026-06-16 · Original →

In early 2020, I started to panic over Covid, and our fragile supply chain situation. In February of that year, before it was consensus that Covid was even a threat to the U.S. I said I expected shortages of basic goods. I was, as far as I know, the first person to make that prediction. And sure enough, within a year, we had a lack of everything from nitrile gloves to black pipe to toilet paper. It was a crisis, and showed the perils of a just-in-time delivery system of globalization.

In February of this year, when the U.S. attacked Iran, it looked like something similar might happen. The Strait of Hormuz carries 20% of hydrocarbons, as well as adjacent products like fertilizer. Oil analysts believed that keeping that waterway closed for more than 100 days would surely bring a crisis, oil at $150 or $200/barrel at least. I had no reason to doubt that consensus, and the Covid shortages were fresh in my mind.

And yet, that didn’t happen, or at least, so far it hasn’t.

Why not? Well, one reason is we have drawn down significantly on our oil stockpiles, both private and public. There was a lot of oil in various storage tanks and floating in oil tankers when the war occurred. And there are public reservoirs as well. For instance, you might hear panicky comments today about the collapse of the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve, which is lower than it has been since 1983.

That seems bad. Except that… well, we have a Strategic Petroleum Reserve! During Covid, we didn’t have a nitrile glove reserve, or a black pipe reserve, or a toilet paper reserve. But going into this war with Iran, the government did have a lot of stored up oil - that’s the reason things didn’t collapse. We prepared for an oil shock!

When the Iran War started, there was an eery calm in the markets, which I didn’t know how to interpret. But early on in the Iran War, an analyst I know pulled me aside and told me not to panic. Oil traders, he said, look at detailed flows of hydrocarbons and make bets based on that data. They know what’s going on. And since the oil shocks of 1970s, he noted, there has been significant diversification of energy sourcing across the world. I didn’t quite realize how prepared we really were, and I didn’t have faith in our institutions to manage crises. But he was right.

Part of that diversification occurred in 1975, when Gerald Ford signed legislation creating the SPR. In 1977, Jimmy Carter began filling it up, finding a suitable location in the salt caves of the Gulf Coast. There are parallel stockpiles all over the world, with the largest one by far in China. There’s even a sort of global governance mechanism; the International Energy Agency, which was established in 1974, recommended that nations build a stockpile of at least 60 days of their net import volume, which they then amended to 90 days. The IEA coordinates global responses to oil shortages.

In other words, oil markets, unlike most other commodity markets, are intensely and explicitly governed by public institutions. Oil is actually something of a throwback; stockpiling was a core feature of anti-monopoly New Deal statecraft. Since World War I, the U.S. government stockpiled a host of different critical minerals, and important elements like helium. In the 1950s, we regularly built up stores of metals like aluminum, and our supply management system in farming ensured that the government had large warehouses of extra food.

Ironically, it took the government a lot longer to manage oil than most of these other commodities. in 1944, Franklin Delano Roosevelt’s Secretary of the Interior, Harold Ickes, proposed a strategic stockpiling of oil, but it didn’t happen. Truman thought about it, so did Eisenhower. But we had significant domestic capacity, so it seemed sort of silly to worry about oil. It wasn’t until 1970 that the U.S. became a net importer of oil, and then in the early 1970s, we had an oil crisis. The world established the SPR and its sister reserves as a response. After fifty years of diversifying and stockpiling, the world managed the shutdown of Hormuz for 100 days, incurring far less damage than expected.

One of the virtues of stockpiling, aside from safety, is that it allows for managing commodity booms and busts. If there’s too much on the market, the government buys up the extra, if there’s not enough, the government releases supply. Governments released oil from their reserves during the 1991 Gulf War, in 2005 after Hurricane Katrina, after the Lybia invasion in 2011, and during the post-Covid moment in 2022. Indeed, stockpiling isn’t rocket science, every competent civilization does it. Just read the Bible, with the story in Genesis of Joseph storing up supplies to prevent famine in Egypt.

Of course, the story here is a lot more than just stockpiling. The fracking revolution opened new sources of oil supply, and the U.S. became a major exporter of oil and natural gas. A host of countries, including Japan, built up systems to rely on coal in case of an oil shock. Pakistan and large parts of Africa affixed cheap solar panels to homes, in part buffering energy costs. Within the Gulf itself, Saudi Arabia built a reserve pipeline to move 7 million barrels of oil a day without having to go through the Strait of Hormuz.

Then there’s China. Chinese leaders are obsessed with strategic vulnerabilities, and have diversified their imports of virtually everything, as well as stockpiling the way America used to - they even have a strategic pork reserve. Chinese imports dropped dramatically during the war. That country has a set of controls on how to use petrochemicals, as well as the mechanism to substitute coal for oil in certain industrial processes.

Diversifying supply and creating stockpiles is a classic anti-monopoly approach to national security. After Covid, the U.S. government began investing in domestic production in strategic sectors, like chips, batteries, and solar energy. It’s not nearly enough to protect ourselves, but we are beginning to build our domestic supply chains in certain areas.

It’s important not to overstate the point. China has a monopoly in rare earth magnets and materials, which allows that country to shut down any industry it chooses. Though other countries, including the U.S., have been investing billions to build out an alternative supply chain for more than a year, we are still years away from having anything we can use to replace the Chinese source of supply.

Of course, economists and conservative free market types are hostile to stockpiling and diversification, because they value the efficiency of capital over safety. Neoliberals on the Democratic side hate government management of commodity flows; Bill Clinton sold off our helium reserves, and ended the last elements of supply management in agriculture. Since Ronald Reagan, the Republican Party has sought to get rid of the Strategic Petroleum Reserve, aided by various libertarian think tanks and economists. (It’s amusing that one of the authors who made this all-time terrible call, Nicholas Loris, is now a fellow at the Abundance Institute.)

One of the ideological enforcement mechanisms that exists in our media is to act like what government does is a passive and natural part of the environment, but to promote every corporation action as if it is a product of immense innovation and heroism. In the case of the Iran War, the press acts like the Strategic Petroleum Reserve is just something that America has because reasons, instead of an actual success of our democratic government that we chose to create and sustain. Yet at the same time, when there were shortages during Covid, the press blamed our political leaders, instead of the corporate institutions and economists lobbying to hobble our capacity to diversify.

The Iran War has been a catastrophe, and I don’t mean to suggest otherwise. I hope it is over. Maybe it is, maybe it isn’t. But it is important to recognize that Donald Trump’s choices damaged our society far less than they otherwise might have, because of some wise decisions by leaders in the 1970s to build reserves and diversify our supplies.

With food, chemicals, medicine, critical minerals, and anything else we can’t do without - well, we might consider learning a thing or two from that.

cheers,

Matt Stoller

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Reckoning with the ‘Binary Choice,’ After Trump’s Iran Capitulation

Andrew C. McCarthy · 2026-06-20 · Original →

I didn’t want this, but I can’t say it was unforeseeable.

“Look, it’s a binary choice.” That’s how I’ve somehow rationalized voting — not once but three times — for Donald Trump, whose second term amounts to the worst presidency of my lifetime. I still think it’s true that a presidential election is a binary choice, even if that may paradoxically be self-delusional.

I believed in January 2021, and am even more convinced now, that President Trump should not only have been impeached but also convicted and disqualified from future office. This was after his soft coup attempt to remain in office after he got drubbed in the 2020 election. It was reckless and half-baked, yes, but hardly unpredictable. So how is it possible to convince oneself to vote for a person so uniquely unqualified for the presidency that he should already have been adjudicated as disqualified to hold the office?

The venerable Thomas Sowell famously observes that there are no solutions, only trade-offs. We don’t make choices in a vacuum; it’s always a matter of “as opposed to what?”

On that score, as we pick up the pieces from Trump’s Iran capitulation, already among the worst foreign policy and national security blunders in American history, a few things are worth noting.

First, while I rue casting a 2024 ballot for Trump, that doesn’t mean I bear responsibility for the president’s malevolent or incompetent actions. I’ve been supportive when I thought he was in the right or being treated unfairly, but an unflinching detractor when — habitually — he is in the wrong. I’m a Burke to Buckley to Reagan conservative; Trump is a New York limousine liberal (when I was growing up, the breed ran my city). The most amusing epithet in modern politics is the MAGA distortion of “RINO.” What’s “Republican in name only” today is the party, not the dwindling rump of conservatives trying to hang on in what is Trump’s own smaller, louder, philosophically incoherent cult.

Yes, I voted for him in three Novembers, but Trump has never been “my guy.” In the crowded 2016 field of GOP candidates, he was the bottom rung for me. I vigorously argued against the blunder I believed his nomination would be. I assumed that he’d lose but, in voting for him, I rationalized that Hillary Clinton was a certain disaster who, among many other downsides, would lard the federal bench (including the open seat of the late Justice Antonin Scalia) with radical progressives who would gradually undermine the Constitution. Trump, on the other hand, was an untested quantity: I hoped that he might grow into the job, and there was at least a chance that he’d make good on the promise to appoint judges in the Scalia mold.

In November 2020, I was content to vote for Trump. The soft coup hadn’t happened yet. Trump the man had proved deeply flawed, but he’d surrounded himself with solid conservatives and listened to them (often kicking and screaming). He’d delegated judicial appointments to their good judgment with stellar results. Judicial appointments are a priority for me, not just because of my legal background but because judges tend to serve two, three, or more times longer than the president who appoints them, and they are critical to the preservation of our constitutional order. Finally, though the Covid-19 pandemic elucidated all his worst traits, Trump’s first term had been a net plus on the economy and national security. And the alternative? Having followed Joe Biden’s career for over 40 years, I found it obvious that he was in decline — and that was from his “zenith” as a Democratic hack never capable of more than aspiration to mediocrity. Even for a reluctant Trump supporter, the binary choice was not a hard one.

That’s the thing. In an open primary contest, one may and often does have the luxury of voting for someone one supports. But in the final election, only two candidates have a chance to win. We’re left to pick one — often, to pick your poison, what you then perceive as the lesser of two evils. That doesn’t mean we support the candidate picked or are responsible for how the elected president performs. The U.S. political system for nominating major-party candidates, which has been corrupted for various reasons over the last century, produces two candidates. That’s the choice we get, but it doesn’t mean we chose the choice.

Relatedly, it is not only possible in the abstract but now proven empirically (more than once, I’d say) that this system may produce two unqualified candidates. If I’ve been right that an election presents a binary choice, this necessarily means that, if one votes, one votes for an unqualified candidate.

It is here that I part company with friends who insist that it’s not a binary choice. I respect that position, but I’ve never come around to it (although I may be getting there, I guess).

Yes, the system does indeed give us the option of voting for someone other than the two major-party candidates. But I’ve always seen that as a cop-out. I don’t mean that patriotic people who avail themselves of this option are consciously derelict. And man, oh man, in the pique of these Trump-era days, I’d love to be able to say, “Don’t blame me, I voted for George Will.” At a gut level, though, that just doesn’t work for me. It feels like leaving to the adults the necessary decision of which excrement sandwich on offer is the less unappealing.

Everyone brings his or her own experiential baggage to this sort of thing. Mine includes 20 years as a prosecutor. I was fortunate to have a run of great cases, including a couple of historic ones. Part of what makes such cases notable, and frequently controversial, is the warts. Very often, the accused are heinous actors who are probably guilty, but the government’s evidence is not ironclad, or the investigators have comported themselves abominably in obtaining it. We leave it to twelve of our peers, who serve as jurors by legal compulsion not choice: What’s it gonna be, guilty or not guilty? There’s no third option, no escape hatch.

Okay, yes, there could be a third outcome: The jury could hang. Yet think about why that happens. The judge sternly instructs the jurors that each must make his or her own decision, and that, while they should listen respectfully to counterpoints, they must not nullify their own sincere view — guilty or not guilty — just to arrive at unanimity. The criminal justice system grudgingly accepts hung juries but always with regret, because it just means the case has to be tried all over again and that it will be left for someone else to make the tough, binary choice.

It seems to me less an imposition on voters than on jurors to demand that the hard choice be made. I could be wrong. Maybe it’s an ill-conceived analogy. After all, if we could redo November 2024, knowing what we know now, I don’t see how I could choose between the two abysmal alternatives on offer. Maybe the least bad option in such an election scenario really is to register dissent by casting a futile vote for a third person, or to decline to vote at all. But, for me, it always comes back to the same thing: Having had no patience for the occasional juror who refused to deliberate and thus subverted the justice system’s necessary function of making the hard decision, I can’t let myself off the hook so easily. (And I say that even as someone who lives in a blue state, where the outcome was a foregone conclusion — where my vote mattered only to my conscience, not to the country.)

So, I’m stuck — with an appalling Iran deal, with lawfare, with brazenly statist intercession in the economy, with a president who abominates America’s allies while indulging anti-American tyrants, with all of it. I didn’t want this. But I did vote for it. And when I look in the mirror, I can’t say it was unforeseeable.

ANDREW C. MCCARTHY is a senior fellow at National Review Institute, an NR contributing editor, and author of BALL OF COLLUSION: THE PLOT TO RIG AN ELECTION AND DESTROY A PRESIDENCY.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

AI: Hype, Governance, and Who Controls Compute

Tyler Cowen: A Dangerous Turn in AI Regulation

Tyler Cowen · 2026-06-15 · Original →

Tyler Cowen: A Dangerous Turn in AI Regulation

If the U.S. blocks AI companies from exporting their models, some countries may delay AI adoption, while others will lean toward China. The U.S. government has declared Anthropic’s AI model too dangerous for unrestricted use. (Illustration by The Free Press)

A new line has been crossed: The U.S. government has finally declared an AI model too dangerous for unrestricted use. It’s the kind of move that could cripple AI progress in the U.S. and around the world.

I had the chance to work with Anthropic’s recently released, high-powered Fable 5 model, a version of Claude Mythos (I am a member of Anthropic’s Economic Advisory Board). But now the plug has been yanked. A federal dispute with Anthropic led the government on Friday to order an export control that requires Anthropic to withhold the model from any non-U.S. citizens. The only practical way to comply with that order was to take down the model altogether.

Federal officials believed that Fable 5 had been partially “jailbroken,” meaning users had figured out how to use it for dangerous purposes that Anthropic meant to restrict. For example, Fable 5 was designed not to answer questions about biology (it kicked the queries to Opus 4.8, a less powerful model), so that it could not be used to design dangerous pathogens. Fable 5 also would refuse to cooperate if asked to build other, potentially powerful AI tools.

It is debatable how dangerous a partially jailbroken Fable 5 could be, but the Trump administration expressed concern and claimed that Anthropic was unwilling to apply necessary patches. The company does not accept that version of events and claims that the model wasn’t jailbroken to begin with.

Yet the public need not bother too much with whose version of the story is correct. What matters is that people had started working with Fable 5, and now they cannot work with it anymore. Since Fable 5 had been released only a few days before the order, the interruption of service has been only a modest problem. But it is easy to see the problem of dependence here, especially as more and more systems are built around the leading AI models. The sad reality is that the more the U.S. government decides to deploy a kill switch, the less valuable American AI becomes as a platform.

Companies will now think twice before relying too heavily on any single AI model that is outside their control. Perhaps more importantly, countries will think twice about their AI dependence, knowing that a mere dispute can lead to a major model being taken away. In such a world, the power of the U.S. government will grow considerably. President Donald Trump, for better or worse, already has shown that the U.S. can push around other nations if it wishes to. That lesson will not be lost on future administrations.

All of a sudden, “AI sovereignty” is the new catchword, most of all in Europe. And who can blame foreign nations for their apprehension? Europe is increasingly realizing that it needs to control its own future when it comes to artificial intelligence.

But sovereign AI is not so easy to achieve. It requires not only reliable, high-quality domestic models, but also sufficient energy, data centers, grid capacity, a workable permitting process for growth, a good understanding of AI applications, and semiconductor access, among other ancillary services. Europe, and indeed most of the rest of the world, is badly behind in most of these areas. Having a good and affordable European AI model would not set all of that right.

The sad reality is that the more the U.S. government decides to deploy a kill switch, the less valuable American AI becomes as a platform.

This article is featured in Tech and Business. Sign up here to get an update every time a new piece is published.

Many countries do not have much of a chance of achieving AI sovereignty. Say you are Peru, which has about 34 million people but is far from being a tech powerhouse, much less a major force in AI. Imagine a near future where models from American companies run significant parts of the central bank and much of the education system, manage receipts flowing into the treasury, and are a source of significant and ongoing productivity improvements for Peruvian companies. In what sense are we still even talking about Peru here, if so much of the “intelligence” in the country is coming from the U.S.?

I do not doubt that Peru could end up much richer and better run, but I also am not sure how Peruvians would accept this trade-off. Would it suffice to rename Claude as Claudio (or Claudia?), and have it speak Spanish with a Peruvian accent? Maybe, but it is fair to say we are in uncharted waters here. And Peru, of course, would have to worry about America applying the kill switch to even more of its AI services. That could happen as part of an attempt to bully Peru, or it could happen for reasons that have not much to do with Peru, as evidenced by the recent Fable 5 disputes. The lack of transparency over the Fable 5 dispute makes this possibility all the more worrying.

One result is that many countries will be slower to move to even partial reliance on AI systems. That will mean lower wealth, lower productivity, and less knowledge and education for much of the world than we would have had with rapid adoption. It will be worst for those countries that most need the skills and knowledge that advanced AI systems will confer.

All of a sudden, “AI sovereignty” is the new catchword.

Another result is that some countries may opt for an alliance with China and Chinese AI models, rather than the U.S. At least some of these countries are fence-sitters anyway, when it comes to choosing sides, and so lesser American reliability could tilt a few over the edge. In East Africa, for instance, current commercial connections with China are strong, and China has a reputation for not interfering in internal affairs. In this scenario, America would lose some of its global reach and influence.

Finally, many countries and companies will opt for open-source AI systems, where there is no kill switch. That will also empower Chinese AI over American AI, since the best open-source models come from China. That might sound okay, but it is still a big geopolitical loss for America, and possibly for the nations buying Chinese AI as well, if Beijing decides to exploit their dependence. Even in the absence of a kill switch, when it comes to servicing, installation, applications, and upgrades, those countries would look to China rather than to the U.S. Furthermore, the open-source models typically are worse than the best American-made proprietary models.

In sum, the inability of the U.S. government to reach an agreement with Anthropic translates into a big geopolitical loss for the U.S. It also means slower and inferior AI adoption for companies, schools, and countries around the world.

Sometimes I hear non-Americans suggest going “all in” on American AI, conditioned on finding ways to make sure the Americans are equally dependent on others in turn. That sounds nice, but given the economic heft and military might of the U.S., I am not sure that many, if any, countries could achieve that status, even if they produce some important components for AI systems, as Germany and the Netherlands do.

There is yet another huge problem behind all these first-order problems. Let us say, for instance, that France’s Mistral AI develops very nicely and serves as an EU counterpart of Anthropic and OpenAI. Well, then the other European countries will become highly dependent on the French. That may seem okay today, but it will be much less fun for the Germans if the French really do have all that extra power and leverage.

As for the French themselves, they would be highly dependent on a private company. France may end up with one such company, but it is unlikely to have three of them. So Mistral will in turn have high leverage over France, French politics, and French foreign policy. Let us hope they are up to that. The simple point is that being influenced by someone in your home country, even if it sounds more appealing rhetorically, is not always better than being pushed around by foreigners. Sometimes the foreigners are less oppressive and intrusive, if only because they care less about you.

The good news is that we probably will get back some safety-enhanced version of Fable 5 soon. The major parties involved all have an incentive to reach an agreement.

The bad news is that AI sovereignty is a crisis faced by the whole world, yet it is an invisible and slow-moving crisis that is not commanding many headlines. It is also a crisis without an easy solution, or one that can be turned into a meaningful slogan. “French AI for the French” may sound good, but it raises the questions of how to get there and what to do once you get there.

We are entering a new world, and we are not prepared to deal with it—or even to think about it in very sophisticated terms. The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Two Drinks with. . . The Trump Adviser Headed to OpenAI

River Page · 2026-06-19 · Original →

Two Drinks with. . . The Trump Adviser Headed to OpenAI

Dean Ball was advising the White House on emerging technology—and this week he took a job at the frontier lab. Of the future, he says: ‘A lot of crazy and destabilizing shit might go down.’ “Dean Ball is in such a hurry that not even his face can catch up with him,” writes River Page. “He’s 33 and could easily pass for a boyish 25.” (Photo courtesy of Dean Ball, adapted by The Free Press)

Dean Ball is running headfirst into the future. One of the most visible experts in the fledgling world of artificial intelligence policy, he spent much of 2025 advising the White House on emerging technologies before moving to the Foundation for American Innovation. Then, just yesterday, he announced he was joining OpenAI, to lead the new “Strategic Futures” team. “An incredible opportunity that came at the right time!” he texted me.

Ball sounded a bit frantic when I called him on Friday, June 12, around 3 p.m. We were supposed to meet for drinks an hour later, but I hadn’t gotten a confirmation. Something came up, he said on the phone, and he’d been dealing with it. We arranged to meet at a hotel bar in Midtown Manhattan.

He didn’t say what had been keeping him busy. But around the time we were ordering our second round of drinks—5:21 p.m. on Friday—the AI company Anthropic received a letter from the Department of Commerce informing them that two of their newest and most powerful AI models, Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5, had been placed under an export control. According to Anthropic, this meant that they were banned from allowing foreign nationals, whether inside or outside the U.S., to access either product. “The net effect of this order is that we must abruptly disable Fable 5 and Mythos 5 for all our customers to ensure compliance,” the company wrote.

This is the same model that, Anthropic said, “found thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities,” when it was first released to a limited number of companies in April, “including some in every major operating system and web browser.” Reportedly, the order came after Amazon CEO Andy Jassy told White House officials that his team had found a way to “jailbreak” Fable 5—which is, confusingly, a version of Mythos—by getting the model to give them classified information about cyber attacks.

The news broke after we parted, and Ball sent me a text: “The stuff happening now is the stuff that blew up, btw. That’s why I went radio silent for a bit.” When I asked him to tell me what he thought of the export ban, the ever-frantic Ball simply replied: “No time.”

But he later published some of his thoughts about it on his popular Substack, Hyperdimensional, where he writes posts on everything from “Anthropic and the Department of War” to “AI and Children.” “The stark reality is that making superintelligence is a profoundly political act even in the healthiest of societies, to say nothing of the filthily political world we Americans currently inhabit,” he wrote about the latest high-profile fight with Washington. “A model like Mythos goes beyond being a mere political act and implicates the sovereignty of the state itself.”

Basically, Ball argues that, because Congress hasn’t passed any legislative framework for AI, all the regulation is coming from the Trump administration, which is no fan of Anthropic. “Of course they needed to tread carefully,” he says of the company. “What the law says does not matter.” In other words, if AI companies don’t want to be kneecapped, they must play nicely with the administration. Ball is in such a hurry that not even his face can catch up with him. He’s 33 and could easily pass for a boyish 25. He speaks quickly, in long, wandering, paragraphs. He tells me that, though he taught himself to code as a teenager, once he went to college, he didn’t major in computer science or engineering. Instead, he studied history.

After graduating, he got into the think-tank world, and in 2019, he tells me, he started using OpenAI’s GPT-2 for policy research. “It didn’t really work,” he said.

Still, he kept his eye on AI development, so he noticed that the models just kept getting better. By summer 2022, Ball was working at the Hoover Institution at Stanford, near the center of the Bay Area tech scene, and recalls thinking that “something big was happening.” He decided to dedicate himself to writing about AI as much as he possibly could. He drafted essays and policy papers, and sent them to everyone he felt would be interested, including a writer who’s now a Free Press columnist. “I sent my essays nonstop to Tyler Cowen,” he tells me. “And after a month or two of nagging, he eventually hired me to be a research fellow at Mercatus Center”—a libertarian think tank at George Mason University.

By late 2024, Ball had caught the attention of D.C. It was around that time, he says, that the director of the Office of Science and Technology Policy, Michael Kratsios, “recruited me to come to the White House.”

“My primary job was going to be to write the country’s AI strategy, which became the AI Action Plan,” Ball told me.

The 28-page AI Action plan was released in July of last year, and can be described as a call to accelerate AI development—including calls to streamline the processes for building AI data centers, increase chip manufacturing, discourage state regulations on AI, and “remove bureaucratic red tape.” Critics say it was a “gift to Big Tech” that ignored environmental concerns, and could protect AI companies from liability if their tech is used to harm or discriminate against consumers.

Ball is aware that some of these positions are controversial—particularly when it comes to the construction of data centers, which have spurred resistance from communities all over the country. Nationwide, at least 75 projects have been blocked or delayed, according to Fortune.

“I think it’s a kind of populist objection to large-scale, abstract infrastructure whose benefits—in the perception of these communities—will not accrue to [them] but instead to people in New York, D.C., and San Francisco. You know, the coastal elites with their . . . contorted interests.”

Also, people want a sense of control, says Ball, and they don’t feel they’ve had one in a long time. “So much has happened over the past 20 years. There was the great financial crisis, there was the China shock, there was the rise of the smartphone and social media, and the mature internet,” he says. “No one really took a vote on any of those things. You couldn’t go to a community board meeting and stop the China shock or the financial crisis from happening.” But you can protest a data center on the edge of your town. “What people feel now is: I can stop this. I have agency about this. I can stop these damn data centers from being built.”

Perhaps this is the same reason college graduates across America booed seemingly every commencement speaker who dared to mention AI. They know the world is changing. They might not like it. But they can’t do anything about it—except make their feelings known.

But what about people who didn’t go to college?

One of the most common jobs for men without a degree is trucking. I mention the fact that, several weeks ago, PepsiCo began running a fleet of fully autonomous delivery trucks out of Texas. Trucking, Ball tells me, “can be end-to-end automated. And the country would benefit wildly from doing that.”

I note that the country might benefit, but my father, a truck driver in Texas, would not, nor would about 3.7 million other truckers in the United States. I suggested that they were going to get very angry.

Engage

“Yes,” Ball says.

Surely there must be some sort of plan for how to deal with potential job losses among millions of blue-collar men?

“What you do for truck drivers might be more of a one-off than what you do for the rest of the economy,” Ball says. He does not elaborate, but he is not a fan of one solution often floated: universal basic income, or UBI, which would give everyone a check every month to cover essentials. He says he doesn’t think people “wanted UBI.”

“I think you just gotta let it play out. We don’t have enough information to make a good judgment about what to do there,” he says. “I’m not saying that we shouldn’t develop policies.” He’s saying that “it’s too soon to know what kind of policies we’ll need.”

I suggest that perhaps this wait-and-see approach could be a bit risky. We’ve already seen a backlash to data center construction. If unemployment spikes to 10 percent or 15 percent—something Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei said was possible last year—might not voters decide to elect politicians who promise to nationalize the major AI companies, or halt development altogether?

“You shouldn’t do stuff just because some asshole might come along [and say]: ‘We’re going to nationalize the labs because they’re about to take all your jobs,’ with no evidence that’s the case,” he says. “When that happens, I’ll fight them.”

He’ll fight them because Ball believes that AI has the ability to help us understand things and solve problems that the human mind itself has never managed to figure out. For example, he says, AI has given us the ability to “predict protein structures,” which has allowed scientists to discover new information about diseases like Alzheimer’s. Maybe it will even one day lead to a cure.

Still, if it turns out that AI isn’t as transformative as they fear, a lot of people will be relieved. But also, a lot of people will be poorer. Many of the most valuable companies in America are making AI; if they aren’t worth as much as the stock market says, millions of 401(k)s are going down. And for months, there has been nearly ceaseless debate about whether or not AI is a bubble. A recent article by anti-monopoly crusader Matt Stoller explored “what the popping of the AI bubble would look like,” and drew parallels between this hypothetical event and previous crises like the 2008 recession and the dot-com bubble.

When I mentioned this article, Ball says he hadn’t read it, but did seem irritated at the idea of it. “What is Matt Stoller’s point? Do we not do AI because a recession could happen in the future?” Ball says. “Should we all just kill ourselves?”

To be clear, Ball concedes that “a recession could happen” as a result of some AI companies being overvalued. “There are certainly some companies where I worry about the valuations. Not so much the frontier labs”—like Anthropic and OpenAI—“Maybe SpaceX, a little bit; $1.8 trillion or $2 trillion, whatever it is now, for what they’ve currently demonstrated seems a little high, maybe.”

If the crash comes, does he think the government he’s recently worked for would be willing to bail out the kind of company he’s now working for? “The federal government is the guarantor of our economy,” he says. “They backstop all systemic risks to our economy. There’s no avoiding that.”

When we met, Ball didn’t say anything about going to work at OpenAI; I texted him when the news of his new job broke, a few days later, but didn’t expect to hear back. After all, he’s a man with very little time.

I still want to know: How are the two of us—who are both relatively young—supposed to prepare for the future? “I have a son. I have a wife. I love traveling. My job is great. I don’t want this to be destabilized personally,” Ball says. But: “I acknowledge that a lot of crazy and destabilizing shit might go down.”

We start talking about history, and the long, violent deaths of social systems like feudalism. We are in another industrial revolution, we agree, and like the last, we could be facing mass social displacement, revolutions, “Lovecraftian horrors” of all kinds, and God knows what else. The conversation veers into a Socratic dialogue in which we tentatively agreed that it’s possible that World War I had to happen in order to permanently disempower ancient European institutions that had developed to manage agrarian societies that no longer existed. Clearing them out helped modernity bloom.

“It might be the case that there are deeply destabilizing and awful things that simply must happen,” says Ball. “You know what I mean?” This has been Two Drinks, the weekly column where we catch up with a newsmaker, trendsetter, or power broker. Last week, River Page talked to Nick Adams, “the top salesman of America.” If you want to get the column directly to your inbox as soon as it’s published, you can sign up here!

The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

Siri AI’s Future in China Unclear Under “One Apple, Two Systems”

SW · 2026-06-15 · Original →

Apple announced long-anticipated AI upgrades to its Siri voice assistant this week at its annual World Wide Developers’ Conference. The new features are set for public launch later this year, but will not initially be available to users in the E.U. (except on Macs and Vision Pro headsets) or in mainland China due to regulatory issues.

For Apple users in China, the resulting situation is what the following meme describes as 一[苹]果两制 yī [píng]guǒ liǎng zhì, or "One Apple, Two Systems"—a pun on the official formulation 一国两制 yī guó liǎng zhì, or "One Country, Two Systems," referring to the principle by which Hong Kong and Macau exercise increasingly limited autonomy within China.

“Others are already using AI. You’re still waiting for Siri to evolve
One world, different permissions
Apple Intelligence
iOS 27 • International Edition
‘One Apple, Two Systems’
One world, two kinds of Siri.”

In the E.U.’s case, the issue is the requirement under the Digital Markets Act that third-party AI services must receive the same data and system access as Apple’s own. The company issued a lengthy statement this week arguing that this would present unacceptable privacy and security risks, and criticizing European regulators’ "extreme interpretation" of the law and refusal to accept compromises it had offered.

Curiously, Apple does not appear to have published any similarly in-depth explanation and objection in China’s case, offering only a footnote saying that "Siri AI and the other new Apple Intelligence features will not be available in China while Apple works through regulatory requirements." The company has worked with local partners to enable a Chinese launch of earlier Apple Intelligence features, which were announced in mid-2024, but these efforts have yet to bear fruit. Apple Intelligence features did appear briefly on China-based devices in March of this year, but this was reportedly an error: Bloomberg Apple reporter Mark Gurman posted on X that "it’s been ready to go for months but Apple doesn’t yet have regulatory approval."

Political guardrails will be key among the regulatory requirements in question. The Wall Street Journal’s Stu Woo noted in December that domestic chatbots must undergo internal and external safety checks related to 31 risk categories, with “incitement to subvert state power and overthrow the socialist system” topping the list. (Other risks cover less contentious ground such as promotion of violence or discrimination, or unauthorized use of others’ likeness.) One recent result is ByteDance chatbot Doubao’s refusal to explain the meaning of the English phrase "It’s my duty," which is associated with the 1989 Tiananmen protests.

Chinese users are hardly without domestic alternatives, and Apple is widely seen as lagging behind the competition even in markets where its AI features are actually available. But as illustrated by the controversial "Apple People vs Android People" discourse led by deplatformed former influencer Hu Chenfeng, the company and its products still have an aura of prestige and sophistication for many Chinese consumers. AI has also been more warmly embraced in China than in the West, so continuing to miss out on Apple Intelligence is galling for some.

For more on AI in the context of China’s online censorship, see China Media Project’s China Chatbot series, and related discussion in our interview with "Locknet" report authors Jessica Battke and Laura Edelson.
