In an age when books and ideas have lost their salience to political debate, Zemmour never misses an opportunity to quote a famous author or reference a historical event. Many academics no doubt see his ideas about French literature and history as shallow and self-serving. But whatever he lacks in sophistication he makes up for in breadth of reading and passion, both of which are evident in his 2014 book The Suicide of France, which will appear in English translation next month.

Zemmour believes that in politics, ideas matter. His understanding of French history has convinced him that a deep bond connects Catholics and Jews that could be reactivated to reinvigorate the French nation and even the West itself. But a question lingers: How Christian is Zemmour’s cultural Catholicism?

Zemmour believes that his own life story captures something about the nature of Frenchness that might not be evident in less complex trajectories. The Zemmours were Berber Jews who lived in French Algeria. In Un destin français, the politician describes his background and childhood. One day his grandfather showed him an old stamp displaying a turbaned warrior beneath the word “Zemmour,” the name of the Berber tribe from which his family gets its name—in Zemmour’s telling, one of the last to submit to France during its conquest of Algeria in the 1840s. His family had been French since 1870, when the fledgling French republic granted citizenship to Algerian Jews (a right not extended to Muslim Arabs and Berbers). Even so, his father recalled encountering antisemitism among French settlers as well as Arabs. In the early 1950s, shortly before the outbreak of the Algerian War began, the Zemmours moved to France. Long after they had settled in the metropole, Algeria remained part of the family heritage. Zemmour’s father would speak Arabic when he frequented the cafés in the Goutte d’Or, a North African neighborhood in northern Paris. His mother learned to make French dishes, like Crêpes Suzette, but still knew how to make couscous and meatballs.

Like other nationalists, Zemmour is haunted by the world he has lost. He was born in 1958 in one Parisian suburb, Montreuil, and raised in another, Drancy, which had been the site of the internment camp from which Jews arrested in France during the German Occupation were transported to death camps in the east. A gritty working-class town with a longstanding reputation for being rough, Drancy is no one’s idea of paradise. Zemmour recalls that the town “was already rendered ugly by hastily constructed buildings in the 1960s; but life was easy-going, happy, and serene.”

In his descriptions of his school days, he comes across as a bit of a goody-two-shoes. His spelling was “impeccable,” he tells us, and he internalized the rules of grammar “like a second nature.” He loved the history textbooks of Ernest Lavisse, the late nineteenth-century historian who developed a narrative that sought to suture the wounds resulting from France’s divisive modern experience. Zemmour now believes that his early years transpired between “two epochs, two worlds.” The scent of the Third Republic still pervaded institutions like schools, even as a new, consumerist society was in full swing. He now believes that he lived at the time “in the best of two worlds.” He had no idea how brief this moment would be.

The greatest experience of Zemmour’s youth was his discovery of France. Reading a book about Napoleon at age eleven sparked his obsession with French history. He also fell in love with French literature. He remembers discovering Paris, as his father drove him through the city in his Citroën DS—a car that the cultural theorist Roland Barthes called the modern equivalent of the Gothic cathedrals of the middle ages because, like them, it was “the supreme creation of an era.” Zemmour recalls that at “the Place de la Concorde, I tried to figure out the spot where Louis XVI was guillotined and where the Cossacks celebrated an orthodox mass in 1814; at the Place de la Bastille, I tried to mentally reconstruct the ancient prison; in front of Notre Dame, my eyes searched for the shadow of Esmerelda and Quasimodo.” Even as a child, Zemmour says, he “understood that France was a unique country consisting of heroes and writers, heroes who claimed to be writers, and writers who imagined themselves as heroes.” In this sense, his literary and political career has realized his childhood dream.

Yet even as he nursed this passion for France, Zemmour was raised as a practicing Jew. In elementary school, his father decided to move Éric to a Jewish school in the Buttes Chaumont, the once working-class neighborhood in northern Paris that became home to Jews fleeing eastern Europe during the interwar years. The school was private but with a state contract, meaning that it taught the public-school curriculum topped up with religious education. Zemmour recalls with wonder how Jewish and French traditions were taught side-by-side. He studied Torah and read Rashi, the medieval French Talmud scholar. But he also memorized the Marseillaise and poems by La Fontaine. At school he wore a skullcap, but teachers made sure the students removed them when they left the building—less out of fear than an “instinctive respect for a strict conception of secularism that separated public space from the private realm.” While he learned about ancestors named Abraham, Isaac, and Moses, his classmates had names like Philippe, Jean-Luc, and Yves. These are the so-called “calendar” names, a reference to French laws that mostly limited the names children could be given to the saints’ names on the Catholic calendar. He was “French in the street and Jewish at home.” And this, Zemmour believes, was the best of all possible worlds.

Zemmour’s political thought seeks to understand what made this idyllic moment possible—how French and Jewish culture were able to exist in a kind of harmony. Zemmour doesn’t deny France’s history of antisemitism, but believes that it is overshadowed by deeper connections between French Catholics and Jews. Remarkably, he does not base this compatibility on laïcité, France’s distinctive version of secularism. Instead, he locates the connection in the overlapping historical and theological identities of the two faiths. Recently, he has defined the goal of his politics as a reassertion of France’s “Judeo-Christian” character.

This religious conception of Frenchness necessarily defines its enemies in similarly religious terms. Judeo-Christian France is threatened, Zemmour maintains, by a troubling alliance between leftists whose ideology is rooted in a misconceived version of Christianity and by Muslims, whose faith Zemmour believes is essentially different from Christianity and Judaism. His political theology holds that Catholics and Jews must unite on the ground of their shared history to defend France against the Muslim-leftist alliance.

The cornerstone of Zemmour’s political philosophy is that there is no France without Catholicism. But no sooner does he assert this claim than he qualifies it—beyond recognition, some might argue. In Un destin français, he declares: “I do not believe in Christ’s resurrection nor in the dogma of the Immaculate Conception, but I am convinced that one cannot be French without being deeply steeped in Catholicism.” At its most basic level, Zemmour’s claim is that just as Catholicism played a central role in French history, it must have pride of place in French public life.

Yet Catholicism, for Zemmour, is more than just a site of memory. It’s also a political doctrine or, at minimum, a repertoire of political ideas. In this sense, Catholicism must be distinguished from Christianity. Here Zemmour draws on the work of Simone Weil, the Christian socialist philosopher of Jewish origins. For Weil, Catholicism was law and order, while Christianity was universal love. On this basis, Weil claimed to be Christian, but not Catholic. Zemmour inverts Weil’s identity. He defines himself primarily as Catholic rather than Christian. While Zemmour has a rich understanding of the Catholic faith, it is the Catholic Church as an institution that impresses him most. He sees it as inculcating a respect for law and doctrine. He admires its sense of authority. He also thinks that a shared faith is essential to nationhood.

In our email exchange, Zemmour explained his position like this: “In schools and universities, we must teach our children about the grandeur of the historical and cultural legacy we owe to Catholicism. We must protect and restore the heritage bequeathed by a thousand years of Catholicism. We must constantly repeat that we are on Christian soil. Citizens, regardless of their creed, are equal before the law. But Catholicism has a cultural birthright.”

Zemmour believes that it is Catholicism and not secularism that connects Jews to France. He maintains that the Catholic Church, with its emphasis on religious authority, sound doctrine, and proper ritual, has close affinities with Jewish orthopraxy. This is a key argument in the book about contemporary religious identities he published last spring, La messe n’est pas dite (roughly, “Mass Has Not Ended”). Zemmour fully recognizes the profound theological divergence between Catholicism and Judaism: Jews cannot accept that God became a human being, while Catholics see Jews as blinding themselves to the new revelation. Yet alongside these serious disagreements, he believes that there exists an equally profound affinity. Notwithstanding the fact that the Good Friday liturgy denounced the “perfidy of the Jews” and Jews were periodically expelled from France, Judaism, Zemmour concludes, was central to the construction of Catholic French identity. He is particularly fascinated with the way the French monarchy modeled itself on Judaism. In Un destin français, he explains how the Franks considered themselves the new “chosen people.” Royal coronation rituals were based on Samuel’s anointing of David. Zemmour calls Saint Louis (Louis IX) the “Jewish King” even as he persecuted French Jews. Bossuet, the great writer and churchman, taught young Louis XIV the secrets of royal rule with lessons from the Old Testament. Inspired by the literary scholar René Girard, Zemmour suggests that Catholic hatred for the Jews was fueled by rivalry—by their admiration for the older faith and their aspiration to imitate crucial elements of it.

Zemmour’s position is not just that Catholicism and Judaism overlap creatively, but that the tensions between them have benefited French identity. The genius of the French monarchy, he writes in Un destin français, “is to have reconciled the irreconcilable … to have corrected the temptation of Jewish national self-isolation by Catholicism’s openness to the world and contained the dissolving potential of Christ’s pacifism by [drawing on] the sacred egoism of Jewish nationalism.”

To fully understand Zemmour’s cultural Catholicism, one must consider one of the more idiosyncratic aspects of his oeuvre: his fondness for French conservative thought of the late nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. This body of thought cultivated skepticism about democracy and held the nation was the ultimate source of political virtues. Two of its leading figures played key roles in the rise of political antisemitism. One, Maurice Barrès, was a turn-of-the-century littérateur who abandoned the narcissistic aestheticism of his youth, denounced the “uprooted” character of republican culture, and ultimately conceptualized a form of nationalism based on reverence for “the earth and the dead.” Zemmour quotes him in his book. He also cites Charles Maurras, the poet turned politician, who transformed royalism into a modern political movement that, he believed, could reverse the decadence to which France had succumbed. In order to do so, however, it would have to be combined with Catholicism, reconceived as a mechanism for ensuring social order. Both Barrès and Maurras were classic “Catholics from the outside.”

Perhaps the most important influences on Zemmour’s thought were the historians Hippolyte Taine and Ernest Renan. Zemmour told me that he rediscovered the two thinkers fifteen years ago, though he already knew from his university studies that they were the two “masters of late nineteenth-century conservative thought.” Taine’s best-known work is The Origins of Contemporary France, which Zemmour described to me as “an unsurpassable analysis of the French Revolution’s nefarious consequences on France’s destiny.” Thanks to Taine, Zemmour says, he “no longer sees the French Revolution with the same eyes.”

But Renan is the thinker to whom Zemmour returns most frequently. It is hard to overstate how odd this is: Renan is better known today for the schools and streets named after him than for his ideas or books. Like many writers of his age, Renan had a religious crisis in his youth that led him to abandon Catholicism and undertake a scientific study of religious history. His Life of Jesus, one of the most scandalous books of its time, provoked religious sensibilities by describing Christ as an “incomparable man.” Renan’s study of ancient religions also led him to embrace a form of antisemitism. In 1855, he wrote that he “recognized that the semitic race, compared to the Indo-European race, really represents an inferior combination of human nature.”

Yet in Un destin français, Zemmour argues that Renan became increasingly critical of the German fascination with race in the years after the Franco-Prussian war. He told a German friend that while his country had subscribed to a “politics of race,” France had pursued a “policy of nations,” and this was the right choice. Renan believed that in modern times, “race” had become an almost mythological concept, since most modern peoples were complex blends of different ethnic and linguistic groups. In a famous 1882 lecture, Renan argued that modern nations cannot be defined by an underlying empirical reality, such as race, language, or religion. Rather, nations are intentional entities, defined by a “spiritual principle,” a “rich legacy of memories,” and what he called a “daily plebiscite”—a constantly reiterated renewal of vows, whereby citizens commit to keep the nation going.

Though Zemmour can talk like Barrès and Maurras, emphasizing an almost ontological conception of the nation, his vision aligns fundamentally with Renan’s. Zemmour writes: “In the political quarrel between past and present, heritage and consent, conservatives and democrats … Renan is closer to the former, but he extends his hand to the latter.” The modern nation is a construct, an outcome of human choices, not a natural or primordial entity. But this is precisely why spiritual unity and a sense of the past are crucial. This is the premise of Zemmour’s Jewish cultural Catholicism, and the reason he embraces another idea he claims to have found in Renan: “Judeo-Christianity.” The Christian-Jewish relationship that Zemmour considers central to French identity is not simply the result of shared religious principles, but a shared history full of glory and heroism, but also hatred and cruelty. Zemmour’s cultural Catholicism emerges as a form of Christian nationalism.

Renan famously argued that while a nation’s citizens must share things in common, they must also “have forgotten many things.” “Every French citizen,” for instance, “has to have forgotten the massacre of Saint Bartholomew.” Zemmour neither advocates for a cynical erasure of the past, nor does he believe that past conflicts can be superseded through truth and reconciliation. His point seems to be, rather, that conflict and even oppression are significant experiences in the past whose impact on the present must be properly gauged. Without denying the Catholic persecution of Jews, Zemmour thinks that this history cannot be separated from the underlying affinities between Jews and Catholics and historical experience that, even by virtue of their conflicts, they have shared. Recognizing the tensions between the different communities within a nation is necessary if one is to avoid the dangers of the nation conceived in racial terms.

Zemmour believes that Renan understood something about nationhood that eludes the modern left. In its outlook, spiritual unity and reverence for the past have become abhorrent. As Zemmour puts it, Barrès’s “earth and the dead have become ‘nazified.’” The left is also convinced of the superiority of “diversity” over “unity.” The only way it can conceive of the nation is as the sum of its differences, with no underlying unity. This is why the defense of immigrants mobilizes the left, and why it has become allergic to assimilation. Zemmour thinks both these positions can be seen as offshoots of what he calls “Christianity” as opposed to “Catholicism.” As he explains in Un destin français, Christianity has, in the past fifty years, become an “insane machine for loving the ‘Other,” one that has untethered “Christ’s message of universal love” from “divine law and church teachings.”

The resulting politics are, Zemmour contends, profoundly misguided. The left fails to recognize, in his view, that Nazism abandoned “spiritual unity” in favor of a racial conception of the nation. Paradoxically, from this perspective, the left’s idea of diversity has more in common conceptually with Nazism than the notion that nations are defined by an overarching spiritual principle. He also believes that the political ideal of universal love is rarely conceived as applying to fellow citizens, leaving the left empty-handed when it comes to explaining why nations exist. Christian love, he writes, has been “instrumentalized to serve the destruction of nations and Christian civilization in Europe.”

Zemmour’s conception of a Judeo-Christian France informs his view that Islam can have no place in French society. I told Zemmour that Islam seems to have many of the qualities that he admires in Catholicism: order, the primacy of law, respect for tradition. He responded with a quote from Renan: “the Jew has the right to challenge dogma but not to eat pork; the Christian has the right to eat pork but not to challenge dogma.” “Muslims,” he added, “are not allowed to eat pork or to challenge dogma.” He also told me that while Renan’s Life of Jesus was deemed scandalous in its day, “the Christian world now fully accepts that which still shocks the Islamic world”—namely, the idea that a revered religious figure could be considered human. Inspired by Renan and Taine, Zemmour worries that the legacies of the French Revolution and Muslim immigration have been combounded into a toxic mix that “is in the process of tragically shaping France’s fate.”

Strange though it might sound to say of a man who delights in nineteenth-century literature and admires the most religiously austere moments in France’s past, what emerges from a consideration of Zemmour’s thought is his peculiar modernism. He has no problem saying “c’était mieux avant” (“things used to be better”), a stock phrase generally used to mock grumpy old timers who long for the days of their youth. But the point of Zemmour’s politics is neither nostalgia nor faith. The biologist Thomas Huxley once described Auguste Comte’s positivist religion as “Catholic organization without Catholic doctrine, or in other words, Catholicism minus Christianity.” Something similar could be said of Zemmour.

When I asked him if he had perhaps exaggerated the gulf between Catholicism and Christianity, he replied: “Saint Paul preached universalism … but he did so in the Greek language, not in Chinese or Arabic.” For Zemmour, the key point is that Christianity took root on certain ground. “This evangelical message was rooted in a land, Europe, and was transformed by the inhabitants of this territory, who built a culture—music, painting, architecture, law, the state, ideas of the individual, men and women.” This message is not oriented towards the past, but the present and, indeed, the future. Zemmour does not seek to return France to some mythical primordial state, but to find a way in which the country can once again draw inspiration and political energy from its past and the fact that it is a nation. Renan wrote that “to have performed great deeds together, to wish to perform still more—these are the essential conditions for being a people.” This is why, for Zemmour, nations still matter.

Michael C. Behrent is a professor of history at Appalachian State University and the editor and translator of Towards a Conservative Left: Selected Writings of Jean-Claude Michéa.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Coleman Hughes: The Case for Progressive Patriotism

Coleman Hughes · 2026-06-19 · Original →

Coleman Hughes: The Case for Progressive Patriotism

There’s a reason the groups most valued by progressives—people of color, the poor, residents of the ‘global South’—want to migrate to America. “If we compare America to the world as it actually is, it is one of the most progressive cultures on Earth,” writes Coleman Hughes. (Zahab Zaribaf/AFP via Getty Images)

America’s 250th birthday is sure to be an occasion for competitive displays of patriotism emanating from every corner of the country. But while many Americans find that patriotism comes effortlessly, others struggle with it mightily. Every July 4, Frederick Douglass’s 1852 speech “What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?” circulates on what people used to call “Black Twitter.” At the time, Douglass’s speech was a much-needed call to action and a stinging rebuke of America’s hypocrisy in tolerating slavery while claiming to be a nation devoted to liberty. Now slavery is long gone, but the speech remains as a symbol of the fact that for many African Americans, the historical fact of slavery makes patriotism complicated. Can we celebrate the birth of American liberty when we were not included in it?

Back in 2019, The New York Times released the The 1619 Project, by Nikole Hannah-Jones. It is remembered by its admirers as an ambitious and necessary attempt to retell the American story with white supremacy and slavery at its center. It is remembered by its critics as an exercise in ahistorical nonsense—in particular for the falsehood, put forth in Hannah-Jones’ lead essay, that the American Revolution was fought in order to preserve slavery.

What is less remembered is that the thesis of that essay was a progressive case for American patriotism—albeit a bizarre and twisted one. Hannah-Jones argued that African Americans, more than any other group of Americans, can claim ownership of this country’s great ideals. “Our founding fathers may not have actually believed in the ideals they espoused,” she wrote, “but black people did.” In fighting for our freedom and equality, she argued, we have made the American ideals of freedom and equality true for everyone.

Her backdoor case for patriotism, in other words, was that the Founding Fathers were ideological frauds whose poor handiwork in creating America 1.0 was luckily bailed out by African Americans—who built the America 2.0 that we all enjoy today. Nor was Hannah-Jones eager to share credit for this accomplishment with any other group of people. In her words, African Americans “fought back alone” against the forces of white supremacy that plagued the country prior to the civil rights movement. Thus, black people should be the most patriotic flag-wavers of all.

It is hard to know which part of her thesis is more troubling. The American Founding Fathers were born into a world in which slavery existed all over the world and had existed for thousands of years unchallenged. Having a slave was as unremarkable as having a nanny is today. But a revolution in morality took place on the issue of slavery during their lifetimes, and that revolution was in large measure spurred on by the spirit of ’76—the new obsession with liberty that the American Revolution created. It is not an accident that the oldest abolitionist society in the world was founded in Pennsylvania in 1775.

As far as the notion of black Americans “fighting back alone,” the leaders of abolition societies all across the North—like William Lloyd Garrison, Wendell Phillips, and the Grimké sisters, who were instrumental in organizing pressure campaigns to end slavery—would have something to say about that. So would the founding members of the NAACP—the key legal organization which brought down segregation—more of whom were white or Jewish than African American. According to the Jewish Virtual Library, Jews made up half of the white Northern volunteers in the 1964 Mississippi Freedom Summer project and half of the civil-rights attorneys active in the South during the 1960s. And that’s not to mention the two Jewish activists—Andrew Goodman and Michael Schwerner—that were actually murdered in Mississippi for fighting for the rights of African Americans.

There is no reason, other than pure racial chauvinism, to argue against all this evidence that black people fought “alone” to secure our rights. And any racially harmonious future cannot be based upon the erasure of those who sacrificed so much for our benefit.

The ability to hoist an American flag without embarrassment ought to be the easiest way to find that common ground.

The tortured logic and historical falsehoods embedded in Hannah-Jones’ argument, however, reflect a broader issue with the progressive intellectual project: It is very difficult to make a straightforward and compelling case for American patriotism if you start from progressive premises. Indeed, this is reflected in polls, which find that 92 percent of Republicans are “extremely or very” proud to be American, but only 36 percent of Democrats say the same.

This article is featured in U.S. Politics. Sign up here to get an update every time a new piece is published.

For progressives, how wealth is created to begin with has never been a big concern—only how evenly or unevenly wealth is spread. How the idea of equal rights came to be is not a big concern—only the fact that equal rights have not come to every group at the same time. How a nation based on ideas, rather than on blood and soil, was revolutionary is not interesting—only the fact that the ideal was not immediately realized and still struggles against the more tribal element of human nature. Indeed, progressives generally dislike the idea of human nature itself, preferring the idea that we are infinitely moldable creatures who can be changed into any conceivable form via societal expectations and norms.

Put simply, progressive ideology takes for granted what are in fact precious gifts that most people on Earth still don’t have or have only partially—societal wealth, reliable law and order, political rights—and focuses instead on the ways in which these things are imperfectly or unequally realized. From this vantage point, there is very little to congratulate America on. After all, everything good about our country is to be expected. Everything bad about our country, on the other hand, cries out for a special explanation.

But as we celebrate America’s 250th birthday, it is worth considering whether we can make a case for patriotism that might be compelling outside the bubble of conservatives and independents for whom such a case would be preaching to the choir—a case that requires neither denigrating the Founding Fathers nor rewriting whole swaths of American history. Why is this important? Democrats and Republicans hate each other today more than they have at any point in the last 50 years. It seems to me that the ability to hoist an American flag without embarrassment ought to be the easiest way to find that common ground. And if we do not find some common ground, we are headed down a dangerous road indeed.

Engage

I do not believe that patriotism should be automatic. You should not be patriotic simply because you were born where you were. We should demand reasons to be patriotic—and liberals and progressives will understandably want reasons that align with their values. To that end, the best progressive reason for patriotism is that America remains the number one desired destination for migrants all around the world.

According to Gallup’s 2025 data, 15 percent of would-be migrants around the world would prefer to come to America. The next highest countries on the list are Canada and Germany, at 9 percent and 5 percent respectively. Among aspiring sub-Saharan African migrants, the number is even higher, with 24 percent saying that America is their first choice. Among aspiring migrants in Latin America, 28 percent list America as their first choice.

Largely because of President Donald Trump, America has received terrible press abroad in recent years—much of it related to our alleged racism and xenophobia. But it’s important to keep that in perspective. People vote with their feet—and their choices about where to live capture their true priorities. Around the world, the groups of people most valued by liberals and progressives—people of color, the poor, residents of the “global South”—most of all want to live here because they believe that our society, more than any other in the world, is the most compatible with their individual flourishing. According to the world’s revealed preferences—not simply according to our own self-estimation—we are still the best country.

Another reason why progressives, and African American progressives in particular, should love the United States is that America is among the most racially progressive societies on Earth. That may come as a shock to those who focus only on the worst of American headlines. But if you compare us to the rest of the world as it actually is, America comes out looking excellent on questions of racism and bigotry more generally.

Let’s compare America to Europe. Arguably the best way to measure racial discrimination is through callback studies: sending résumés to employers that are identical except for racially or ethnically coded names. One of the largest ever callback studies, a meta-analysis that aggregated about a million résumés, found that Europeans exhibited more racial discrimination against black applicants than America did.

Or consider the following comparison between the U.S. and our Latin American neighbors. Like the U.S., many countries in the Americas have a minority African-descended population. But the United States is among the few that has had a democratically elected, African-descended head of state. Brazil, for instance, has never elected a black head of state. Nor have Argentina, Bolivia, Chile, Costa Rica, Ecuador, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua, Paraguay, Peru, and Uruguay. If electing a black head of state is meaningful at all in the progressive worldview—and I believe it is—then it must also be meaningful that America has reached that milestone sooner than most of our similarly placed neighbors.

Finally, consider Africa and the Middle East. One need only look at Pew data on tolerance of gays and lesbians in Africa to understand that America is a progressive haven by comparison. As for the Middle East’s attitude toward progressive values, consider that the word abeed, literally meaning “slave,” is still used to refer to blacks in the Middle East today. As recently as 2017, an AP article noted that the black neighborhood in Jerusalem is referred to by many Palestinians in Arabic as habs al-abeed, or the “slaves; prison.”

If we compare America to an imagined utopia, then it is a racist, exploitative hellscape. But if we compare it to the world as it actually is, America is one of the most progressive cultures on Earth.

There is a famous monologue given by the protagonist of Aaron Sorkin’s The Newsroom in which a “sorority girl” naively asks the panel to explain in one sentence why America is the greatest country in the world. His answer, which is meant to be mic-drop moment, goes:

. . . there is absolutely no evidence to support the statement that we’re the greatest country in the world. We’re seventh in literacy, 27th in math, 22nd in science, 49th in life expectancy, 178th in infant mortality, third in median household income, number four in labor force, and number four in exports. We lead the world in only three categories: number of incarcerated citizens per capita, number of adults who believe angels are real, and defense spending, where we spend more than the next 26 countries combined, 25 of whom are allies. Now, none of this is the fault of a 20-year-old college student. But you nonetheless are without a doubt a member of the worst period generation period ever period. So, when you ask what makes us the greatest country in the world, I don’t know what the fuckk you’re talking about!

This is what I could call a dumb person’s idea of a smart argument. International rankings are not great indicators of how healthy a country’s institutions are because they are always comparing apples to oranges. Populations differ in so many ways that to say that America ranks “22nd in science”—whatever that means, exactly—does not tell you anything of significance about how good our scientific institutions actually are.

But more importantly, such rankings miss the forest for the trees. Ultimately, each of these domains of life—math, science, infant mortality, exports—is one small tributary that feeds into a much larger river. And it is that larger river that we are judging when judging a country’s greatness. The sum total of Americanism—warts and all—has built a better society for the groups of people that progressives care most about. How could that be true if Americanism is not a force for good? The Free Press is for free thinkers. Send this to someone who values open debate and curiosity.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

Leonard Cohen’s Dark Faith

M.G. Scott · 2026-06-19 · Original →

Leonard Cohen was, and remains, an enigmatic figure. In his life, he toed an unsteady line between rockstar-dom and cerebral, ascetic hermitude. His work, a shockingly coherent alloy of influences from Blake and Wordsworth to the Beat poets to Zen Buddhism and Frankian antinomianism, is shot through with a brilliance that led Bob Dylan to call him the “number one songwriter of our time,” yet he is perhaps best remembered in the popular imagination as the guy who wrote that one Shrek song, “Hallelujah.” Cohen was notoriously polygynous, a man who, at thirteen, became a master of hypnosis so as to induce the family maid to remove all of her clothing. But later in life, he renounced sex to live as a celibate for seven years in a Zen Buddhist monastery where he became an ordained monk.

Cohen had the poetic authority and the ethereal, romantic touch of a Dylan or a Paul Simon, but unlike these contemporaries, the most influential part of his career came when he was well into his seventies, forced to embark on a world tour after discovering that, while he was marinating at the Mt. Baldy Zen Center, his manager had been robbing him blind. Long before Dreamworks blockbusters, before world tours, before crowds of hundreds of thousands at the Isle of Wight, he said that he wrote for “inner- directed adolescents, lovers in all degrees of anguish, disappointed Platonists, pornography-peepers, hair-handed monks, and Popists.”

Cohen’s talent was obvious when he was still very young. As an undergraduate at McGill University in the early fifties, he was folded into a small set of writers that was composed of men like Hugh MacLennan, author of the seminal 1945 book Two Solitudes; F.R. Scott, McGill’s Dean of Law; and Irving Layton and Louis Dudek, who pioneered new strains of modernism in poetry and laid the groundwork for a revival of Canadian poetry. They constituted Montreal’s intimate literary scene, mostly meeting in each other’s homes.

F.R. Scott, a man whose interests ranged from poetry to law to civil rights, was a great extrovert and entertainer who oversaw the gestation of the Co-Operative Commonwealth Federation, Canada’s first democratic socialist party, in his living room. The poets met in his home at 451 Clarke Avenue in Westmount, a gilded suburb of Montreal. “451,” as they called it, had once been a boarding house, but its sixteen rooms were now scrubbed free of their former Victorian veneer thanks to Scott’s wife, Marian, a modernist painter who tore out the old ornate fireplace and replaced it with a block of Bauhaus concrete. A Tiffany lampshade, worth thousands of dollars, was left decomposing in the backyard, and Scott installed a large cannonball from the Plains of Abraham, where Wolfe defeated Montcalm in the Battle of Quebec, in the front yard.

It was here that Leonard came to know F.R. Scott’s son, Peter, the poet and academic best known for popularizing the term “deep state.” He also wrote prose and poetry inspired by a sweeping vision of the cultural evolution of the West. Peter is my grandfather, so my first exposure to the Leonard Cohen mythos was delivered first-hand over Chinese food on Tuesday evenings.

Leonard and Peter were two of the three junior members of this group of ten or twelve. Leonard was nineteen, Peter five years his senior, back at McGill to finish his dissertation after two years at Oxford. Much later on, Peter described the experience of being sandwiched between the eminence of his father, above him, and Leonard’s rising brilliance beneath: “above all your shadow Leonard/ you were five years my junior yet already/ envied by me for being/ a lady’s man with a car/ while completing your first book of poems/ already larger than life.”

Rivalries aside, the acquaintance blossomed into a friendship. Leonard, long-time car-owner that he was, taught Peter to drive on a trip down to the states. At one point, Peter mustered up all of his 27-year-old seniority to scold Leonard for his extracurricular pursuits, saying, “you’re a serious poet—don’t waste your time down there in those nightclubs with your guitar.” Leonard declined to take the advice.

For both men, it was a time of intense work. “We really wanted to be great poets,” Leonard said in an interview with Liam Lunson. “We thought every time we met it was a summit conference. We thought it was terribly important what we were doing.” This was on the eve of Allen Ginsberg’s Howl, a period of reckoning with the horror of the holocaust and Hiroshima, as the West struggled to strangle the strange new forces that had wriggled out of the Enlightenment’s box of rationally ordered civilization. As Peter later put it, he and Leonard were “inspired by the idea that the world was a terrible mess, that they were all teaching the wrong kind of poetry in the universities, and that we with our poetry were going to change things.”

Around this time, Robert Lowell described poets as belonging to one of two camps: the “raw” and the “cooked.” The “cooked” were East Coast people who came out of Harvard; this was F.R. Scott’s camp. The “raw” were the Beats; this was Irving Layton, who became Leonard’s closest mentor. These two camps could have a rough parallel with what Peter would later call the yin and yang opposition internal to the evolution of Western culture. “Yang,” in his taxonomy, corresponds to Enlightenment rationalism, to that which is knowable and communicable, can be touched by language, and so forth; “yin” is the countervailing force of religion, mysticism, of poetic thought, proper, of that which resists propositional knowing.

Peter left Montreal for good in 1956, off to a career in the Canadian foreign service that would take him to hidden imperial loges in Viennese palaces, then to a teaching post at the University of California, Berkeley. There, he taught Dante, Virgil, and Dostoyevsky, rallied faculty members in support of the Free Speech Movement, wrote volumes detailing the CIA’s involvement in international drug wars, and struck up a friendship with the great Polish poet Czesław Miłosz.

Leonard, meanwhile, lived the life of a Bohemian troubadour; holed up on the Greek island of Hydra in a whitewashed house with a wrought-iron bed, sampling varieties of pot, speed, and acid, writing a “sexy, phantasmagoric” novel called “The Favorite Game” and poems that would later appear in a collection entitled Flowers for Hitler. While Peter moved in a set that included figures like Noam Chomsky and Daniel Ellsberg, Leonard rubbed shoulders with Patti Smith, Lou Reed, Jimi Hendrix, Janis Joplin, and, of course, Bob Dylan. “I took the yang path, and he took the yin path,” said Peter. “I joined a university and he became a rock star.”

The men lost touch after Peter first left Montreal, and for forty years, they had no contact. They finally reconnected in the late nineties, when Leonard reached out for permission to use some of Peter’s father’s work on an upcoming album. What began as a logistical interaction soon burgeoned into a full-fledged reunion, which was well underway by the turn of the millennium. When Peter published his long poem, Minding the Darkness, in the year 2000, Leonard flew to Berkeley from Los Angeles to hear him read it.

Leonard immediately took to Peter’s wife Ronna. Both of them were westward-wandering East Coast Jews with a serious interest in Buddhism. The three of them would share long meals, which Peter describes as being “very, very intense,” where “Leonard would say the sorts of things that made you wish you were taping him.” At one of those dinners, at a Greek restaurant in Los Angeles, Leonard shocked Ronna’s modern orthodox sensibilities by ordering a large plate of calamari, which he and Peter devoured while she looked away.

This period of reacquaintance took place while Leonard was touring. Peter and Ronna attended seven or eight concerts as his guest, once flying all the way to London to hear him sing. Meeting again so much later in life, after walking two very different paths, gave the renewed friendship a special quality. “By the time we knew each other again,” Peter said, “he was no longer a rock star and I was no longer an academic. We were on the same ground.”

Still, the yin and yang polarity resurfaced in their final exchange. In October of 2016, just nineteen days before his death, Leonard released his final album, You Want it Darker. It was the coup d’éclat of the man sometimes called the “godfather of gloom.” In the title track, Leonard sings “Hineini Hineini/I’m ready my Lord.” Hineini is Hebrew for “Here I am,” which is Abraham’s response to God’s demand that he sacrifice his son, Isaac. Here, Leonard, like Abraham, prepares to bend to the will of a violent power that eludes his faculties of understanding. Rolling Stone described the album as having a “realistically grim, spiritually radiant and deeply poetic worldview, generally spiked with a romantic thrum and an existential wink.”

Around the same time, Peter was finishing his own book of poetry, entitled Walking on Darkness, which he sent to Leonard via a friend. In a hurry, before handing it off, he scribbled the following note in the jacket of the book:

If you want it darker This book is not for you I have always wanted it lighter And I think God does too.

To Peter’s great surprise, Leonard answered, by email on October 3, 2016, with a poem of his own—which may be the last poem he ever wrote.

who says “i” want it darker? who says the “you” is me? god saved you in your harbor while millions died at sea you and god are buddies you know his wishes now here’s broken Job all bloodied who met him brow to brow there is a voice so powerful so easily unheard those that hear may hate it all but follow every word if you have not been asked to squat above the dead be happy that you’re deaf not something worse instead he will make it darker he will make it light according to his torah which leonard did not write

The next day, Peter replied:

Who says I know God’s wishes? I’ve not met brow to brow never had a chance to glimpse him and never hope to now But we who were raised in harbors while others burned from war have been free to choose which voices made us what we are.

To which Leonard responded, signing off with his Hebrew name:

That was great fun. Be well, dear friends. Much love, Eliezer.

On November 6, a long-time friend of Leonard’s texted him a photo of Peter at breakfast with her daughter. Leonard replied with another text, quoting the Beatitudes: Blessed are the peacemakers: for they shall be called the children of God. Twenty-three hours later, he was dead.

Peter said that he has known three great men in his life: Miłosz, Daniel Ellsberg, and Leonard. (Later on, he added the poet Denise Levertov to the list.) I asked him what it was about these men that made them great. He told me that all three were able, by the intensity of their commitment to what they were doing, to put a dent in history. Ellsberg, with Watergate, was able to do so at the level of the immediate history that would show up, for instance, in The New York Times, but all three worked at a deeper level of history, the sort of history that Peter calls “ethogeny,” the history of ideas, of our cultural evolution.

Underlying the notion of ethogeny is the idea that “when something is progressing, something else is regressing.” This is the push and pull between yin and yang, between the Dark Ages and the Enlightenment. “When we get off the path as we are right now, then the dialectic kicks in and something happens to get us back on the path,” Peter says.

Leonard’s 2004 album, Dear Heather, included a track entitled A Villanelle for Our Time, which was a poem by Peter’s father, F.R. Scott, which Leonard set to music and recited. Repeated throughout are the lines:

Through bitter searching of the heart We rise to play a greater part

F.R. Scott wrote the poem in a period of self-examination, after he had renounced his pacifist convictions to persuade his left-wing political party to support the war against Hitler in 1939. I asked Peter why he thought Leonard might have chosen this particular poem, what it was about it that spoke to him. “Leonard would like the word bitter,” he said. “There’s pain and that pain equips us to be more than we are.” This darkness, this bitterness seems to be a fundamental constituent of Leonard’s genius. Peter said that “great creativity is the right marriage of yang and yin.” It would be reductive and silly to directly equate yin/evil and yang/good—what is significant here is the generative dialectic, which existed in Leonard’s work as in Peter’s. In those long, free-ranging dinner conversations, “evil came up a lot.”

Leonard, iconoclast though he was, remained committed to the Judaism into which he was born. Peter’s religious path was more complex. Baptized into the Anglican Church and deeply interested in medieval Christianity, he began to practice Zen Buddhism in the 80s, and later entered into a serious engagement with Judaism through Ronna, adding another point of connection with Leonard. Once, when Peter and Ronna were arriving in Los Angeles, late to Sabbath, Peter left his yarmulke in the car. Leonard said, “here, take mine.” The next day, at breakfast, Peter tried to return it. Leonard said, “no, keep it.” Peter still wears it, every Sabbath.

Today, Peter describes his faith as “postsecular.” He references Thomas Merton, who maintained that the affirmation of one religious praxis did not entail the negation of the others. This refusal to be confined within the bounds of a single tradition is, perhaps, why he and Leonard were able to see eye-to-eye despite their doctrinal differences. They were both attempting to peer through the prism of praxis to the deeper, ineffable thing underneath.

Of their final exchange, Peter said that “I think part of him did want it darker. It was so permissive. If the world is so dark and there is no settled order that we need to live by, then you can live the life that Leonard lived.” Peter, on the other hand, accepts the necessary presence of darkness without giving over to it entirely. “My last word to him was that you’re talking about your reality as if it was reality,” Peter says. “I am suggesting that each of us respond to voices that shape the reality that we want to live in.”

Leonard also alluded to the quiet power of these voices when speaking to The New Yorker’s David Remnick not long before his death: “I know there’s a spiritual aspect to everybody’s life,” he said, “whether they want to cop to it or not.” He went on: “What I mean to say is that you hear the Bat Kol.’ The divine voice. ‘You hear this other deep reality singing to you all the time, and much of the time you can’t decipher it.’” Fifty years earlier, in his novel Beautiful Losers, he had written: “A saint does not dissolve the chaos…it is a kind of balance that is his glory. … Something in him so loves the world that he gives himself to the laws of gravity and chance.”

It is this interpenetration of light and dark, of good and evil, that makes Leonard’s life, work, and ethics difficult to reckon with. Yet perhaps, it is also this ability to reckon with it that equips him and the other greats to cut to the heart of the ambiguity of our time, and to speak directly to our innermost desire, as Peter says, to be more than we are.

M.G. Scott is an actress and writer.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

The Death and Life of American Exceptionalism

Steven Watts · 2026-06-18 · Original →

Across the first two centuries of US history, the idea of American exceptionalism was unexceptional. Politicians of nearly every stripe, and citizens supporting nearly every political persuasion, endorsed the idea that the United States, the product of a republican revolution, held a unique place in the world as a beacon of liberty, equality, and opportunity. The phrases Novus Ordo Seclorum (“a new order for the ages”) and Annuit Coeptis (“Providence favors our undertakings”) were affixed to the Great Seal of the United States in 1782, and leaders ranging from George Washington to Franklin Roosevelt, Abraham Lincoln to Ronald Reagan would affirm the uniqueness of the principles proclaimed in the Declaration of Independence and codified in the Constitution. Critics of the prevailing American order, from Elizabeth Cady Stanton to Martin Luther King, Jr., agitated for the country to uphold and fulfill its lofty ideals rather than challenging the ideals themselves.

Since the 1960s, however, particularly on the political left, the notion of American exceptionalism has become toxic. Many progressives now see the American experience not as a saga of advancing freedom and opportunity at home and around the globe— a halting process laden with struggle, partial achievements, and frustrating failures—but rather as a dark tale of oppression. Since its inception, in this view, the United States has been guided by systemic racism, colonial settlement, rampant greed, persistent misogyny and patriarchy, imperialist aspirations, elite domination, and unrelenting injustice.

Thus as we approach the 250th anniversary of the nation’s founding, a question hangs over our public life: Can we still embrace American exceptionalism as part of our common creed, or must we reject it as a lie concealing the victimization of countless groups at home and abroad? An examination of the history of this ideal suggests that, despite its detractors, a strong case still can be made for the enduring truth and power of American exceptionalism, albeit in chastened form.

American exceptionalism received full-throated affirmation 200 years ago with an extraordinary event. The two men most responsible for the Declaration of Independence—Thomas Jefferson, its author, and John Adams, its most stalwart advocate—died within a few hours of each other on July 4, 1826, the fiftieth anniversary of the nation’s founding. Astonished Americans saw this simultaneous passing of the Revolution’s beloved patriarchs as a sign of the divine favor bestowed on the young nation.

Soaring newspaper eulogies proclaimed that this providential event confirmed the Declaration of Independence as “the word of God” while President John Quincy Adams, son of one elder statesman and protégé of the other, described their deaths as a visible sign of “divine favor.” Daniel Webster spoke for many when he portrayed these co-joined deaths as a ratification of the founders’ glorious achievement in creating a new polity.

The public remained largely unaware, however, that Adams and Jefferson, over the previous decade and a half, had debated at length the future of the American republic that its citizens now so joyfully celebrated. After bitterly clashing in partisan contests, the two ex-presidents had reconciled in 1812 and commenced a correspondence that lasted the rest of their lives. While they discussed a wide variety of topics, the issue that preoccupied them was the nature and fate of the American experiment.

Jefferson promoted what would become known as American exceptionalism: the idea that the United States has a special destiny to fulfill in human affairs by spreading its unique commitment to liberty, equality, and opportunity. Convinced that the founding of the American republic had inaugurated a new epoch in human affairs, he envisioned the growth of an “empire of liberty” sending out “the signal of arousing men to burst the chains, under which monkish ignorance and superstition had persuaded them to bind themselves.” As the Virginian wrote to the New Englander, “the flames kindled on the Fourth of July, 1776, have spread over too much of the globe to be extinguished by the feeble engines of despotism.”

Adams demurred. He shared Jefferson’s belief in a special republic founded on a sturdy foundation of reason and natural rights, but worried that flawed human nature and social pressures stood restlessly in the wings, ready to subvert this dramatic New World experiment in liberty. He believed history showed that aristocratic power-mongering, human selfishness and ambition, moral failings, and factional disputes might overcome republican “virtue,” or the capacity to put the common good above self-interest. Some might believe, Adams sarcastically noted to Jefferson, that “Our pure, virtuous, public spirited, federative republic will last forever, govern the globe and introduce the perfection of man,” but he suspected otherwise.

As the exuberant public response to their linked deaths demonstrated, Jefferson triumphed over his New England colleague in their argument over American destiny. For most of the next two centuries, American exceptionalism became a bedrock belief of American public life, informing nearly all political parties and movements. What Robert Bellah called the nation’s “Civil Religion” fused republican idealism and religious fervor to proclaim the special destiny of the United States as a beacon of liberty, human rights, equality before the law, and socioeconomic opportunity that would inspire the liberation of mankind from the bonds of unjust power. It became central to Americans’ view of themselves and the world.

In his study of the young nation’s values and institutions, Democracy in America, Alexis de Tocqueville confirmed the pervasiveness of the exceptionalist mindset. Noting the country’s vibrant civil society and enthusiasm for politics, he concluded that Americans’ “passion for equality is ardent, insatiable, eternal, and invincible.” Their “irritable patriotism” brooked no divergence from praising the country’s values and institutions and served as “a kind of religion.” This unshakeable belief that the United States embodied liberty and equality meant, in Tocqueville’s words, that the “position of the Americans is therefore quite exceptional, and it may be believed that no democratic people will ever be placed in a similar one.”

Many actions and events in the nineteenth century illustrated the power of the exceptionalist creed. In 1803, President Jefferson doubled the size of the United States with the Louisiana Purchase, a massive procurement of land between the Mississippi River and the Rocky Mountains. “By enlarging the empire of liberty,” he explained, “we multiply its auxiliaries, and provide new sources of renovation should its principles, at any time, degenerate.”

Two decades later, Secretary of State John Quincy Adams masterminded the “Monroe Doctrine,” a proclamation warning European powers to refrain from interfering in the affairs of the Western Hemisphere and positioning the United States as the dominant force in the region. Promising to protect fledgling New World republics from Old World corruption, Adams justified this enterprise with the Declaration of Independence, “a new fabric, destined to cover the surface of the globe. It demolished at a stroke the lawfulness of all governments founded upon conquest. It swept away all the rubbish of accumulated centuries of servitude . . . . [America’s] glory is not dominion, but liberty.” By mid-century, a chest-thumping version of American exceptionalism emerged with figures such as newspaper editor John O’Sullivan and President James K. Polk, who trumpeted the nation’s “manifest destiny” to expand across the North American continent and beyond.

In a twentieth century dominated by global warfare, American exceptionalism consistently fueled the nation’s course of action. President Woodrow Wilson defined American entry into World War I as a crusade to “make the world safe for democracy,” part of the nation’s global duty as “one of the champions of the rights of mankind.” Nearly twenty-five years later, Franklin Roosevelt explained America’s participation in World War II as an extension of the nation’s “Four Freedoms”—for speech and worship, from want and fear—to people “everywhere in the world.” As the Cold War took shape in the following decades, the struggle against communist expansionism tapped the wellsprings of its exceptionalist tradition.

President Harry Truman delineated a conflict between a “way of life based upon the will of the majority, and distinguished by free institutions, representative government, free elections, guarantees of individual liberty, freedom of speech and religion” and a tyrannical system determined to subvert all those qualities. President Dwight Eisenhower spoke similarly, defining the Cold War as a struggle for the “soul of man,” a “war of light against darkness, freedom against slavery, Godliness against atheism.”

The 1960 election marked a high point of exceptionalist appeals. Richard Nixon, the Republican candidate and sitting vice-president, had confronted Soviet premier Nikita Khrushchev in the much-publicized “Kitchen Debate” in Moscow, proclaiming that consumer abundance illustrated the superiority of the “American Way of Life.” He asserted the superiority of American liberty and prosperity over socialist collectivism and authoritarianism on the global stage and predicted that Khrushchev’s “grandchildren will live in freedom.” Senator John F. Kennedy, the Democrat candidate, surpassed Nixon in his endorsement of American exceptionalism. In the soaring rhetoric of his inaugural address, JFK paid homage to the “revolutionary beliefs for which our forebears fought” and promised: “We shall pay any price, bear any burden, meet any hardship, support any friend, oppose any foe to assure the survival and success of liberty.” Two decades later, Ronald Reagan provided a capstone to the long tradition of American exceptionalism. In dozens of speeches and commentaries, he paid fulsome tribute to the idea of the United States inspiring the world as a special exemplar of democratic values and economic opportunity. He often referred to America as a “shining city on a hill” or “a place in the divine scheme of things that was set aside as a promised land.”

At the height of America’s wealth and global power in the 1960s, however, powerful challenges to American exceptionalism emerged. Critics argued that the Vietnam War revealed the nation’s proclivity for imperialist adventure while the Watergate scandal provoked disillusionment in the political class. Powerful dissenting movements—the New Left’s critique of capitalism and repression, the counterculture’s rejection of materialism and success-seeking, the civil rights movement’s denunciation of systematic racism, feminism’s castigation of patriarchy—came together to question the special mission of American republican principles.

Political figures on the left began to disparage the exceptionalist paradigm as naïve, arrogant, and untruthful. In 1972, George McGovern trumpeted “Come Home, America” and urged the nation to withdraw from global engagements and focus on fixing its own rampant problems of poverty, racial and gender prejudice, and government malfeasance. A few years later, facing economic stagnation and foreign policy failure, President Jimmy Carter proclaimed an “age of limits.” In his famous “Malaise Speech,” he dissected a national “crisis of confidence” and chastised Americans for leading “empty lives” devoted to “self-indulgence and consumption.” This degeneration, he maintained, had shattered American belief that “we were part of a great movement of humanity itself called democracy, involved in the search for freedom . . . . [J]ust as we are losing our confidence in the future, we are beginning to close the door on our past.”

After a brief revival of liberal patriotism in the 1990s—Bill Clinton, for example, liked to say, “There is nothing wrong with America that cannot be cured by what is right with America”—the Democratic Party returned to equivocating over American exceptionalism. President Barack Obama claimed, “I believe in American exceptionalism, just as I suspect that the Brits believe in British exceptionalism and the Greeks believe in Greek exceptionalism . . . . [I also believe in] recognizing the value and wonderful qualities of other countries, or recognizing that we’re not always going to be right.” By the 2020s, leftist stars such as Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez abandoned such careful parsing for condemnations of American “fascism,” “concentration camps,” “tyranny,” “white supremacy,” and “genocide.” As Peter Beinart noted of such efforts in The Atlantic, “the left is mounting a serious challenge to American exceptionalism.”

If post-1960s leftist politicians questioned or waffled over American exceptionalism, the nation’s intellectual class dismissed it outright. In 1975, the noted sociologist and political theorist Daniel Bell, announced “The End of American Exceptionalism” in The Public Interest, writing that it “has vanished with the end of empire, the weakening of power, the loss of faith in the nation’s future.” Noam Chomsky, public intellectual and darling of the progressive left, excoriated American exceptionalism as echoing the doctrines of “the worst monsters: Hitler, Stalin, the conquistadors” while providing cover for the machinations of a “rogue state” pursuing profits and “imperial power.” Yale historian Paul Kennedy’s 1987 bestseller The Rise and Fall of Great Powers argued that the United States did not stand apart, but faced the same historical forces of economic stagnation and “imperial overstretch” that brought down other great nations.

Historians led the intellectual exodus from American exceptionalism in the late twentieth century. By the 1970s, social history, labor history, feminist history, racial history, and the “new” political and foreign relations history replaced belief in America’s unique national agenda with narratives of oppression, greed, racism, and victimization. By the early twenty-first century, the vogue for global, transnational history administered the historiographical coup de grâce. Its focus on the importance of international trends and crosscurrents took aim at the very idea of an American history separate from, let alone standing above, that of other countries. America appeared as just one nation among many—no better, no worse. As one of its leading practitioners admitted, “The legacy of exceptionalism can only be laid to rest” by adopting “alternative transnational approaches.”

These developments in academic history bore fruit among popular historians who pilloried the exceptionalist paradigm for a mass audience. Foremost among them stood two highly-publicized books: Howard Zinn’s A People’s History of the United States (1980), which recast American history as the story of marginalized groups struggling to wrest social and economic justice from exploitive elites, and The 1619 Project: A New Origin Story (2021), in which journalist Nikole Hannah-Jones reframed the nation’s founding by placing slavery and racism as its motivating factor.

The upshot of such political and intellectual trends was predictable. The notion of an “unexceptional America” became embedded in universities, primary and secondary schools, the mainstream press, the communications industry, the publishing world, and most liberal churches. Several generations of American students imbibed the idea that not only does the United States fail the test of being a beacon of freedom and opportunity but it likely stands as the enemy of such values.

In a recent Atlantic article, psychologist Jean Twenge charted “The Death of American Exceptionalism” among the nation’s youth. For the last forty-five years, she reports, US high school seniors have been asked whether they agree that “Despite its many faults, our system of doing things is still the best in the world.” In the early 1980s, 67 percent agreed with the statement. By 2022, only 27 percent agreed. A recent poll conducted by the Democracy Fund reveals even deeper erosion. It asked Americans if the Founding Fathers are “better described as villains or as heroes.” Only 10 percent of Baby Boomers chose “villains” but 40 percent of Zoomers did so. For many modern American youth, Adams and Jefferson do not just fall short of earning the mandate of heaven. They embody wickedness.

As we arrive at the 250th anniversary of the founding, does the exceptionalist paradigm still have life or is it a historical relic? Does it retain any power to shape Americans’ perception of themselves and their nation’s destiny, or is it a mark of national delusion and shame? Do the nation’s republican institutions and traditions explain its power and influence in the world, or is such status the tawdry result of unexceptional greed mongering, imperialism, and opportunism? Or perhaps just a manifestation of dumb luck, justifying Otto von Bismarck’s reputed quip that “God has a special providence for fools, drunks, and the United States of America.”

Despite the challenges it has faced, American exceptionalism retains considerable power as a national narrative and inspirational hope in the early twenty-first century. To believe otherwise demands a form of historical amnesia. It requires ignoring the unique qualities of the American Revolution, which first overturned traditional barriers to political liberty and social freedom. Unlike later revolutions in France and Russia, which resulted in mass murder, social chaos, and authoritarian political projects, America went on to ratify a Constitution that enshrined its founding principles in a stable republic of laws.

It requires ignoring the fact that throughout its existence, millions of people from around the world have lined up to emigrate to the United States to find a better life—northern Europeans in the early 1800s, southern and eastern Europeans in the late 1880s and early 1900s, Latin Americans in the late 1900s and early 2000s. It requires ignoring that America played the leading role bringing down two of the most murderous authoritarian regimes in human history: Nazi Germany in World War II and the Soviet Union in the Cold War.

In other words, while the United States shares some things with other modern nations, it also stands apart in crucial ways. Yet as the United States navigates the twenty-first century as the most powerful economic and military power in the world—a far cry from the infant republic nurtured by the Founding Fathers and the rough-hewn adolescent nation of the nineteenth century—probity, wisdom, and the national interest demand a modern adaptation of American exceptionalism molded in a chastened, humble form.

Such a version appears most clearly in the profound reflections of Abraham Lincoln. Throughout his life, the sixteenth president viewed Americans as a special people inhabiting a special polity built upon the Declaration of Independence and its proclamation of public and personal liberty. Moreover, he remained convinced that such principles were destined to have a universal impact.

In his first important public speech delivered to the Springfield, Illinois Lyceum Society in 1838, the young orator embraced the heritage of the Founders embodied in “a system of political institutions, conducing more essentially to the ends of civil and religious liberty, than any of which the history of former times tells us . . . . Let it become the political religion of the nation.” Twenty years later, he likewise insisted that the Declaration of Independence “set up a standard maxim for a free society, which [was] constantly spreading and deepening its influence, and augmenting the happiness and value of life to all people of all colors everywhere.”

However, the Civil War, with its horrifying levels of death and destruction, led Lincoln to revise his earlier, unequivocal endorsement of American exceptionalism. He viewed the fratricidal conflict as a test of the American experiment in liberty, a renewal of the Founder’s “great struggle” to protect “the liberties of the people.” Ruefully describing his quarreling countrymen as the Almighty’s “almost chosen people,” he came to believe that Providence played a role in the nation’s affairs by not just affirming but judging its worthiness for special dispensation. Lincoln’s God first stood in judgement of America’s failure to live up to its own ideals much like the admonition in the book of Luke: “For unto whomsoever much is given, of him shall much be required.” Providence, as a prod for the United States to fulfill its special destiny, punished the nation when it fell short. In his Second Inaugural Address, Lincoln interpreted the carnage of the Civil War as America’s penalty for the sin of slavery. The “mighty scourge of war,” he declared, threatened to continue “until all the wealth piled by the bondsman’s two hundred and fifty years of unrequited toil shall be sunk and until all every drop of blood drawn with the lash shall be paid by another drawn with the sword.”

Lincoln’s mature version of American exceptionalism fused humility and hope, punishment and redemption. In his eyes, the nation’s experiment in liberty, if it was to be successful, must be constantly put to the test and its standard of individual freedom, equality before the law, and economic opportunity reaffirmed. America, a blessed nation, must continually earn that blessing to keep it and accept providential punishment if it failed.

Thus, Lincoln’s final vision of America’s exceptional role in the world combined Jefferson’s Enlightenment idealism and Adams’ Puritan skepticism and imbued the whole with sublime moral meaning. This New World nation, as the Virginian believed, should push forward its principles of freedom as an empire of liberty, but it must remain vigilant, as the New Englander warned, against the temptations of corruption and human failing.

In the hands of the Great Emancipator, American exceptionalism became both a settled fact and an ongoing aspiration. He illuminated what was at stake during the dark wartime days of 1862. “Fellow-citizens, we cannot escape history,” he declared. “The fiery trial through which we pass will light us down, in honor or dishonor, to the latest generation . . . . We shall nobly save, or meanly lose, the last, best hope of earth.” As we begin another 250 years of the great American experiment, like Lincoln, only we can determine which of these outcomes will occur.

Steven Watts is professor of history emeritus at the University of Missouri and author of The Magic Kingdom: Walt Disney and the American Way of Life and Citizen Cowboy: Will Rogers and the American People.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response

How America lost the art of association

Luke Burgis · 2026-06-16 · Original →

“In democratic countries the science of association is the mother science; the progress of all the others depends on the progress of that one,” wrote Alexis de Tocqueville after touring Jacksonian America. The French writer had seen that Americans seemed particularly good at forming civic associations that incorporate both freedom and responsibility; in doing so, those associations formed citizens who were capable of governing themselves and flourishing in every domain of human life, without being consumed with the kind of political zero-sum games that threaten not only life and happiness, but progress, too.

Nearly two centuries later, do we Americans retain the talent for association? The signs aren’t promising. We belong to fewer congregations, clubs, unions, and lodges than our grandparents did. We report fewer close friends, and a rising share of us claim none at all. Time spent alone has reached record highs, and the Surgeon General has declared loneliness a public-health emergency. The genius for association that Tocqueville admired has not been abolished, so much as left to atrophy.

The breakdown in association results in what I call the problem of “the one and the 99” — the direct and unnatural relationship between a human person and a large group. It is a perennial problem, but current conditions have accentuated it dramatically because our technologies are built to connect the one directly to the millions, dissolving everything in between. The platforms that promised to bring us together did so by hollowing out the associations that once stood between the person and the mass. A man scrolling alone is plugged into a crowd of strangers — exposed to their moods, their fears, their judgments — with no friend group or congregation to mediate the encounter. We have never been connected to so many while accompanied by so few.

Its political manifestation is the self set against the state, with no intermediary institutions or forms of association between them — except a few good group chats, if you’re lucky.

That is not only a lonely and somewhat pathetic way to live. It is also dangerous. The human quest for community does not disappear in these atomized environments; it simply gravitates toward more totalitarian forms of government and belonging.

History has tried to warn us about this danger — and it also shows us the way out.

[

More from this author##### The scapegoating of Peter Thiel

By Luke Burgis](https://unherd.com/2021/09/how-peter-thiel-became-a-scapegoat/?edition=us&ref=refinnar)

In the gray hush of 1970s Czechoslovakia, where even silence seemed state-issued, there was a band called The Plastic People of the Universe. Its music was raw, dissonant, and defiantly Western. The band didn’t shout slogans or call for revolution, but played loudly and boldly. For that, its members were arrested by the Communist government in 1976.

Among those watching was a playwright and dissident named Václav Havel, who would eventually become president of post-Communist Czechoslovakia. Galvanized by what he saw, Havel wrote a 1978 manifesto in samizdat (self-published) form that passed from hand to trembling hand across the country, under the title The Power of the Powerless.

“Everyone understood that an attack on the Czech musical underground was an attack on a most elementary and important thing,” he wrote, “something that in fact bound everyone together: it was an attack on the very notion of ‘living within the truth,’ on the real aims of life.”

Havel’s essay centers around a memorable image — a hypothetical greengrocer who places a sign in his shop window that reads “WORKERS OF THE WORLD, UNITE!” The greengrocer doesn’t really have a strong belief that the workers of the world should unite. He places the sign in the window because it is the easiest thing to do. “The poster was delivered to our greengrocer from the enterprise headquarters along with the onions and carrots,” Havel wrote. It gets put up because it is the default thing to do, like unpacking the onions and carrots and putting them on the shelf.

Acts like that, Havel suggested, are the lifeblood of what he called post-totalitarianism, in which people aren’t coerced by force into doing things; they voluntarily do what they think is expected of them. They don’t want to cause any trouble, so they quietly and perhaps even unconsciously engage in performative acts that signal belief in the status quo.

“We have never been connected to so many while accompanied by so few.”

Havel’s poster has not disappeared; it has migrated. Today, it is the reflexive repost, the slogan in the bio, the conviction adopted because the people nearest us already hold it. Few of us examine these gestures any more than the greengrocer examined his sign. We perform them because performing them is the path of least resistance — and because everyone around us is performing them, too.

That is social contagion: the unconscious transmission of belief and desire from one person to the next, the reason a slogan or a fear can move through a crowd faster than any argument can. Post-totalitarianism is simply social contagion left unchecked — a society in which the pressure to perform runs strong and widespread because people do not have the kinds of friends, or civic associations, that would immunize them from it. If our society were a human body, it would have high blood pressure which, left untreated, would damage its vital organs. The way to treat this societal disease is not through medication, but through exercise: in our case, the exercise of healthy association that acts as a check on the pressures of power.

Power rarely comes from one source. It often comes from the way people relate — through trust, fear, stories, and rules. “It is in the basic associations of men that the real consequences of political power reveal themselves,” wrote the sociologist Robert Nisbet in the 1950s. Nisbet understood that political health depends on the strength of intermediate bonds that lie between the individual and the state — families, congregations, and local associations that give shape to common life. Once these bonds dissolve, the individual and the state meet face-to-face, naked and unmediated. That is the crisis of modern politics: not too much government, but too little community.

Havel saw a way to reconstitute the civic bonds that helped people live with more integrity, not according to the lies that falsely held his post-totalitarian society together. What if the compliant greengrocer chooses to change his behavior? “Let us now imagine that one day something in our greengrocer snaps and he stops putting up the slogans merely to ingratiate himself,” he wrote. “He stops voting in elections he knows are a farce. He begins to say what he really thinks at political meetings.” The greengrocer, Havel continued, “has not committed a simple, individual offense, isolated in its own uniqueness, but something incomparably more serious. By breaking the rules of the game, he has disrupted the game as such. He has exposed it as a mere game.”

[

Suggested reading##### The myth of Western crisis

By Michael Lind](https://unherd.com/2026/02/relax-about-western-civ/?edition=us&ref=refinnar)
