The specific things that he says, one, are that it really depends who the woman is, and he does lib out in that one moment that there are differences among women. But secondly, he’s like exactly what I was saying earlier about, like, yeah, you have to improve yourself. You need to be someone who people would want to hang out with.

And that is, I mean, that’s like maybe if it’s just, like, OK, motivational, you need someone who you admire to tell you that so that you’ll be willing to do it. Maybe we just need Barack Obama telling more people to, “Hey, like, you need to be attractive and you need to work hard or whatever.” I think he does say stuff like that, but maybe we need more of those clips floating around the internet.

But I just think that, like, I want to distinguish here between, broadly, advice that’s meant to motivate people to be the best version of themselves — and then to pursue relationships or dating or sex or whatever it is — and then what I see most of the time on the internet. Which is actually, both, in my, you know, just normal social media scrolling, but also, I created today a, you know, incognito mode, made a YouTube Gmail account as, like, a man and started looking for dating advice for men too.

There’s a ton of this stuff which is just, like, actually not just saying, “Hey, when you approach a woman, you should be polite, here are potential questions you could ask, or here’s, like, random piece of advice, whatever,” to give. It’s also just, like, “Women want money and they want power and they need you to dominate in a conversation and they won’t want to not make a decision. They want to be the one who, like, feels taken.”

I’m like, “None of this is — this kind of reifying of gender stereotypes is actually completely, completely separable from this other question of, like, “Can you give broad pieces of advice of how to chat up girls at a bar?” Like, these things don’t have to go hand in hand.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, but I mean, I do think there’s some relationship between them.

Jerusalem Demsas: Sure.

Matthew Yglesias: And I also just, I mean, I’m concerned about the politics of this. Like, another guy who I saw — who I, unlike Tate, I’d never heard of — was somebody called Dan Bilzerian. And I saw one thing of his and he was advising men, like, “If a girl texts you, wait at least 5 minutes before texting her back.”

I have no idea. I’ve not, you know, run a randomized controlled trial on this idea that you should try to provoke anxiety in this other person rather than coming across as overly eager to text her back. But, you know, it’s a hypothesis. It’s out there.

When I was in this mode of my life, we didn’t have as much texting, but there were similar things about how soon should you call people back, you know, blah blah blah blah blah.

Jerusalem Demsas: I’ve seen Friends, don’t worry.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. This comes up in The Rules, you know, there’s a lot of this kind of discourse. I don’t know who’s right or wrong about this.

It just, it also turns out that this guy has, like, totally insane far-right and also super antisemitic political opinions. And I would be really upset if somebody came across some of his little psychological gamesmanship ideas and was, like, watching a couple of these and next thing you know, they’re, like, into these conspiracy theories about the Elders of Zion.

And I felt a little bit more comfortable in the world where I was a young man, where you could get this kind of stuff. One, literally, he has one bit where he’s talking about “If you meet a girl, you should ask her, you should, like, challenge her to describe three good things about herself other than her good looks.” And this is something he’s just plagiarizing from Neil Strauss. This was, like, an old bit of his that I remember from when I was young, right?

And the theory, again, I don’t know if any of this works, but the theory of that is, A) You’re complimenting her, but backhandedly by, like, saying, “You’re not allowed to talk about how good looking you are.” And then you’re just shifting the frame of the conversation away from one in which you’re super agreeable to one in which you’re, like, putting her on her toes and you’re making the interaction more interesting.

I don’t know, is that a good idea? I don’t know. But it’s interesting. It’s something to try out there. And I just think it should be possible for people who are feeling a little shy and confused and they don’t know what to do to get material that they can engage with that is not politically correct or whatever, but also isn’t coming from far-right sociopaths.

We need to rebuild, you know, the sensible center of the pickup artists and just, like, exist in society.

Jerusalem Demsas: I want to separate out two things. One is that I think I agree with you that the — I mean, this is something that, I don’t know, has dominated left discourse for the last four years. It’s just like, “Why aren’t there left-wing podcast bros?” And then there are some left-wing podcast bros, people like Hasan Piker, who obviously is getting a lot of attention these days. I’m hearing about him more and more. I don’t know if you are.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes.

Jerusalem Demsas: No, but I mean...

Matthew Yglesias: But the thirstiness around Hasan is telling, though, because, as some of the Piker skeptics have pointed out, his show is just, like, not that popular compared to — I mean, it’s maybe more popular than our show, so, you know...

Jerusalem Demsas: Recommend it to your friends.

Matthew Yglesias: Exactly. Spread the word. Get out there. We need, you know, not everything needs to be far-left socialism. You know, Joe Rogan is incredibly, just, like, wildly super duper duper popular. And, you know, it — but it tells us something, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think my point is...

Matthew Yglesias: And the point is he’s handsome and strong, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, I mean, I think the thing that I’m saying is that there’s — it is obviously the case that the advice of — and, you know, I’ve got a lot of my thinking on this from my friend Rachel Cohen, who’s currently writing a book about personal agency — but a lot of the personal agency advice-giving side of the internet has gotten a little bit more right-coded because there’s been a lot of criticism of the idea of personal agency as being really important or relevant towards how people’s lives turn out.

Matthew Yglesias: Right, so...

Jerusalem Demsas: In that structural ideas or structural forces are what are going to be to blame. So, whether or not you end up in a good school or get a good job and get a good partner, these are not really things that you can tinker through your own self-effort.

And I don’t think that this argument was made super explicitly by a lot of people. I think that often, at the individual level, many people don’t actually believe this, and they do work very, very hard.

But I agree with you on that personal advice base, but I just, again, I think that there’s this, now, this effort, as we’re, like, having a rebound reaction to the woke era, to just completely course correct rather than say, like, there are things to have taken from this period of time that were valuable and convincing and persuasive to a lot of people.

And that, yes, getting personal advice to young people who want to figure out how to meet other people is totally fine and reasonable. And also, not only is it reductive and bad to gender stereotype, it’s actually going to lead you astray. A lot of these pieces of advice that I come across — again, I can’t speak for every individual person. There are, like, billions of people on this planet who have different views on stuff.

Matthew Yglesias: How many people?

Jerusalem Demsas: I think 8 billion now? How many are we at?

Matthew Yglesias: See? Throwing you off base. You’re off your game.

Jerusalem Demsas: Fact-checking in the middle of this pod. But, I mean, there are billions of people, but I just want to say, I think it is not true that it is genuinely good to spend a lot of your time thinking “How can I throw a girl off-kilter so that I can get her to think of me in a more flirtatious mindset?” It’s just, like, I actually don’t find that to be positive advice that I would give to a lot of my friends if they were trying to pick up women.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, well, you’re supposed to say, like, “You don’t ask a fish how to catch fish,” right? Something like that. The — I forgot what it is — but no, I do think that it’s true, right, it’s true that people do not have perfect self-knowledge, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: On these kind of things. And I think that that is an important aspect of dating and relationships that, you know, sort of has to be out there. What people say they want and what they actually respond to in real time can be quite different, you know, and that’s, like, a sort of important thing.

There’s a lot of — I mean, when thinking of, like, advice focused on men, I think mostly falls into these kind of two buckets, right? There’s this fundamentals-based stuff that’s, like, “You need to get more jacked and have more money,” which I think is, on the one hand, I think actually tends to get overblown. You know, there’s too much indexing actually on those two characteristics versus “It’s helpful to cultivate certain kinds of interests that you’re more likely to find something in common with women who you talk to. And also you should think about, because people are individuals, who shares interests in common with you,” right? And, like, actually try to pay attention to what you’re doing.

And then there’s this kind of tactical stuff, right, which is about — which is interesting because, again, my wife and I met on Match.com, but I spent — when that was new and when internet dating was very marginal — and I spent many years before that out in the wilds of pre-internet dating.

And I feel like this tactical stuff was really big then, right? That there was some before-times when, I don’t know what, like, you know, either the village elders were arranging marriages for you, or, in America, you know, people were meeting at work, they were meeting through church, if applicable, they were meeting in college, maybe something like that.

And then we had this new world in which people were supposed to meet people in bars or meet people at parties. And there was a lot — and some people were really good at that. I think especially people who are just very extroverted, and others, like me, not as good at that. And there was a lot of advice focused on, sort of, how do you close the deal? How do you turn a fleeting interaction into a date? Something like that.

Now I feel like people just don’t do that as much, right? And we have this internet dating economy that I feel like people are very unsatisfied with.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think that — I mean, I met my husband on Hinge, “designed to be deleted.” So we’re both, I guess, the faces of internet dating. But...

Matthew Yglesias: But you guys are like the exceptions. Well, you’re not the exceptions because...

Jerusalem Demsas: Everyone meets their partners on Hinge.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, I mean, everyone who is married, who’s young, met their partner on Hinge.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: But also, like, way fewer people are getting married. And I have the perception that it’s, like, these apps have, like, ruined society or something.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think this is an overblown thing. I think that the apps “exist in the context of everything that came before them.”

Matthew Yglesias: Mmm, the apps didn’t fall from the coconut tree.

Jerusalem Demsas: They did not fall from the coconut tree, no.

But I think, specifically on this question of types of advice, I thought was very interesting is how different the advice for women is and the advice for men. I sorted the advice for women that I see into three categories: One is sort of the “if he wanted to, he would”-type advice, which is largely the idea that if a guy doesn’t do things that you want him to do, like text you back, make plans for a date, whatever, that means he doesn’t like you. That means he’s not into you.

There’s an earlier iteration of this from this movie He’s Just Not That Into You that came out when I was in middle school. But it’s a book...

Matthew Yglesias: That’s from a book, Jerusalem.

Jerusalem Demsas: I was talking about the movie adaptation from my childhood, but yes, it is from a book. Again, this is a very longstanding strain of advice, which is, the presumption is that a lot of women are in this position of just wondering whether men are into them and then having to decode signals about their behavior, and really encouraging a lot of, I think, neuroticism in women to decode this is kind of the baseline way that women are expected to interact.

And so, He’s Just Not That Into You is meant to be kind of, like, freeing, like, OK, don’t even worry about it. If a guy is into you, he’ll make it so obvious. He’ll be so obsessed with you that you don’t have to do anything.

And this is just, like, I think, as I was saying, factually incorrect. I think there are a lot of men who are into women all the time who do not make it extremely obvious that they’re into them or know that there are these things that maybe they should do to make it clear. Like, you know, “Hey, you should open the door for her.” Or there’s, like, random rules that pop up which are, like, you know, you should stand on the outside of the sidewalk while she’s walking so you’re the one by the cars or whatever.

And, again, there’s just a proliferation of these rules. Like, this advice was originally quote unquote “meant to free women from having to deal with all this neurotic thinking about whether men are actually into you based on how they’re calling or talking or shooting signals,” but instead it formed an entire plethora of new rules where, like, “Oh, well, the way that you actually know is if he fulfills all these other sets of rules.” And that’s one category of...

Matthew Yglesias: I actually felt like there were two. I mean, maybe this is the same as what you’re saying, but the things you showed me, it seemed to me, fell into two different categories.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, that’s just one category. The second category is sort of, like, around, like, “Don’t let yourself be embarrassed”- type stuff, which is — the presumption here is, I mean, I think this is kind of related to the first thing, but it’s this presumption is, you know, “You are at risk as a woman and the risks are that a man will cheat on you. The risks are that he will, you know, you’ll get pregnant and he’ll leave you or that he won’t get married to you or that, predominantly, that he will embarrass you in some way.”

This is, like, a huge theme of, like, relationship content online, is, like, men embarrassing their partners. Like, women posting the fact that, like, a man didn’t do something for Mother’s Day, and then a lot of women online will be in her comments being, like, “Divorce him, if not, don’t tell us about it.” Like, that’s kind of a main theme.

And, like, “You don’t want to end up like these women who were in these relationships with these shitty guys. And so, like, the way that you avoid this is by never texting back. If he doesn’t confirm the day of, don’t show up on the date that you plan.” Like, all these different pieces of advice. So, like, dating advice is largely “How to avoid this really bad tail risk.”

And then the third version is this kind of soft tradwife-type thing, which is sort of, like, “Oh, to attract men, you have to be, like, in your feminine energy.”

Matt and I, I tried to get Matt to take a quiz on the show about whether or not we were in our feminine energy given by this dating coach. He did not want to do that. He thought that was too far afield from the conversation. So, if you want to hear our results, let us know and we’ll do it. We’ll put it on Substack Notes.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, we’ll see.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think this third trend is the one I think is most similar to the male dating advice, which is very specific. Like, “Here’s the type of makeup you should wear if you want to look like a soft trad, you know, soft feminine energy woman. Like, this is how you should talk. You should talk very slowly. You shouldn’t nag him.”

And there was this one specific video where this woman was, like, “Don’t yell at him about not cleaning up around the house. Ask him in this very soft voice if he wouldn’t mind helping every once in a while.”

And it’s like, all of these videos, they’re kernels of true things. Like, yeah, it’s, like, probably you get more benefit out of asking someone to do something in a nice way versus in a very harsh way. But also at the same time, like, I don’t know people’s particular circumstances. Like, maybe you guys are in a fight about this, or maybe you’re kind of being unreasonable. Like, there’s not...

Matthew Yglesias: Right. But so, I mean, there’s a big asymmetry here, which is that, you know, a large share of the woman-focused content that you shared with me and that I checked out is, like, it’s not dating advice at all in the sense that I would under — it’s, like, don’t-date advice. You know, it’s warning you off all of these bad men.

And sometimes the warnings, I think, are good, right? Like, within the He’s Just Not That Into You kind of spectrum, right? Some of the examples that I saw, I would have said, you know, as a man, I would have been like, yes, correct.

Jerusalem Demsas: Like what?

Matthew Yglesias: This is the behavior of a guy who is stringing you along and doesn’t actually care about you.

Jerusalem Demsas: Like the not texting you thing or what? Men, text back, that’s what Matt is trying to say.

Matthew Yglesias: I thought the best example of that from one of them was that if he won’t travel to see you.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: Right? That, you know, that’s, like, if he liked you, he would want to go spend time with you, right?

But then at the other end of the spectrum, one of them, she was advertising as the bare minimum. And she was just rattling stuff off that, like, clearly isn’t the bare minimum.

Now it’s fine, a person is allowed to set whatever kind of standards they want, right? But it’s kind of...

Jerusalem Demsas: You can set prices high.

Matthew Yglesias: In a practical sense, it’s, like, anti-dating advice, right? It’s telling women to be less accepting of the foibles and failures that one just often encounters in the experience of interacting with other people.

Jerusalem Demsas: And it’s also to interpret certain things as failures, whether or not those are actually true. So, like, someone not texting you back quickly could be a signal of lack of interest, or it could be, like, they were at work and they can’t look at their phone. But I mean, there’s a real bias on this advice of, like, interpret this stuff as…

Matthew Yglesias: But it also just could be that — it can both be true that some behavior is, like, non-optimal and also that it’s just worth putting up with. Because the flip side of your whole point, like, “people are different,” it’s like, yeah, people are different.

Like, you are probably never going to meet someone who is perfect in every way. You know what I mean? And there’s a kind of a baseline question of, like, “What do you make of that?”

I thought Noah’s piece, right, the core thesis that he was operating with was to try to talk men out of a male version of heteropessimism, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Heteropessimism is this idea that, like, people who are very, very, I guess it’s really in the name, pessimistic about heterosexuality. It’s largely this performed in a way that it’s women kind of being very down on the way that straight relationships harm women and that they’re really toxic, but yeah...

Matthew Yglesias: Right, but there’s also, I mean, there’s a kind of a female-oriented sort of feminist version of heteropessimism. And there’s a male-oriented anti-feminist version.

And, you know, so in the male-oriented one, you’re saying “Women are really only attracted to, like, 10 to 20% of men out there. It’s hopeless for everybody else. Maybe some girl will string you along to exploit you for free meals or something.”

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s like incel ideology, yeah.

Matthew Yglesias: And so Noah’s saying, like, “No, that’s not true, that average women want to date and marry average guys. You just gotta, you know, get out there, give it your best shot, blah blah blah blah blah blah.”

I basically agree with everything that he’s saying out there. What is true, though, and that you opened my eyes to, is that there is this fair amount of not — not, like, dogmatic, you know, political lesbianism — but this kind of, you know, telling women that they should be much more unforgiving of — not, like, abusive partners or cheating or, you know, just things that anybody would be like, “stand up for yourself, girl, don’t put up with that” — but just kind of, like, I don’t know, I get why these things could bother a person, but speaking to the larger question of like “Do we all want to die alone? Or do we want to find somebody to spend our lives with?”

Jerusalem Demsas: This is my thing; my big core problem with so much of this is that so much dating advice is given by people who don’t seem to be interested in helping people achieve their long-term happiness. And, like, long-term happiness of people who are interested in getting married, having a family, whatever, want some sort of romance in their lives, requires risk.

It is a risky thing to do to decide that you’re going to take a chance on someone, that if they’re going to make a mistake early on, that you will forgive them and let it happen again.

And, like, this culture that I think on both sides alienates genders from each other. On the female side, there’s this idea that every mistake is a hint of this person’s long-term ability to destroy you. Whether it’s, “Hey, this is as good as it’s going to get” kind of stuff. Where it’s like, “OK, he didn’t — you come over to his place and it’s not clean? That means that you’re gonna have to clean up after him for the rest of your life.”

And, you know, on the male side too, I mean, I think it’s, like, so toxic, this idea that these women are just only able to be attracted to these, like, specific characteristics, that they’re not full and total people.

And I just think that, like, if Mr. Darcy in Jane Austen’s famous book Pride and Prejudice, had had one of these dating influencers yapping in his fucking ear after Elizabeth Bennet rejects him the first time. Like, he does not go back to her when she’s clearly had more of a change of heart and re-pledge himself to her, and then they end up married and, in my head, happily ever after, right?

And, to me, romance and these kinds of relationship choices that are risky are often only read as positive when they work out, and you can’t know that at the front. So, like, you do have to risk a lot.

You have to risk getting rejected as a guy going up to a bunch of different girls and saying, like, “Hey, I would love to talk to you about whatever the fuck your interests are. Like, talk about them,” because that’s what you want at the end of the day. Because if you are selecting for women who aren’t interested in what you want to talk about, five years from now, you’ll be in a relationship with a woman who’s not interested in talking to you about what you care about.

Matthew Yglesias: OK, but...

Jerusalem Demsas: This online advice that’s, like, “Hey, you should definitely go up to her and, like, neg her into telling you what she’s actually good for so that she’ll be, like, interested in you.” But, like, now you’ve, like, somehow gotten all of these dates with girls who can’t actually fulfill your long-term — what was the point?

Matthew Yglesias: OK, but this is where I feel like fundamentally the greatest social value of this kind of, like, game stuff dedicated to men is exactly what you were saying, that, like, there — with no risk, there’s no reward.

And for whatever the flaws of the specifics and the details and so on and so forth, the basic message of that kind of content is that, like, you should go and try, and with the understanding that it will not always work. That people who, you know, you talk to them for two seconds and they seem interesting and hot or whatever, like, you might just not like them, you know? And if you try to ask a girl out, like, she might shoot you down. She also just — she might have a boyfriend, you know? She might say she has a boyfriend, but actually she’s shooting you down. And, like, these things all and nothing will happen in life if you don’t try, right?

And that that’s, I think, a really, it’s an obvious message, but it’s an important message. And I think it’s increasingly important because we are not in, I was gonna say Victorian England, but Jane Austen is set earlier than that, but I forget when.

Jerusalem Demsas: Regency, right?

Matthew Yglesias: Regency, that’s what they call it, yes. It’s like Napoleonic Wars, whatever.

So, we used to have a society that was just much more built around telling everybody all the time that they need to get married, right? And there’s a lot of downsides to the structure of 19th-century society, but it was very heavily, you know, sort of, like, you know, everyone was, like, on tracks leading to that sort of thing. If people go to a dance, right, like, the point of the dance is that somebody’s gonna ask you to dance, and if you have a good time, it’s like, you might get married next week.

And the modern world is not like that at all, in mostly good ways, but it does mean that, I think, people require more information and ideas and encouragement, or else you get, you get no couples, you get passivity, you get things not happening.

Jerusalem Demsas: I just actually think declining couple rate has quite literally nothing to do with what’s going on, like, online dating advice. But I think the thing I would say is: On the margins, if I was thinking of a person who is looking for a relationship and having trouble and they don’t know why, and I don’t know anything about this person, my advice would be “Go to someone who cares about you and knows a lot about you and try to get advice about why you’re not able to be partnered from them.”

I think the idea that I would say, like, turn to, like, random, like, YouTube or Twitter or TikTok influencers is basically next to zero. And I think that the advice, on average, to me, leads people astray.

I agree that having more people share pieces of information about, you know, what has worked for them would be positive. And, again, lots of advice people get about dating and relationships is often, like, not explicitly advice. It’s, like, things you pick up from witnessing successful relationships.

Like, you see your buddy has a girlfriend. How does he treat his girlfriend? What do they do? How does it go when they get into arguments? Like, what occurs? Like, that kind of thing is, like, things you absorb through osmosis.

You see through your parents, you see people in your family, how do they engage with each other? Like, what does this tell you about relationships and how they work in the long term? Your siblings, your friends.

And I think, like, what messages are being sent here — we moved, really positively, I think, in the 2000s and, you know, 2010s, away from a view that you could really, really reduce people down to, like, extreme — women in particular — down to extreme stereotypes.

At the same time, we started, I think, shifting the stereotypes associated with men in ways that were really, really negative and harmful. And now, again, as we’re, like, “OK, this wasn’t the — we did not achieve the peak of feminism and egalitarianism in the 2010s,” I really don’t want the reaction to that to be, like, “We should go back to the ‘90s.” Because, to be clear, there was a lot of angst around dating and relationships in the ‘90s. There were lots of concerns about, like, you know what I mean?

Matthew Yglesias: Absolutely, people are always concerned.

Jerusalem Demsas: But, I mean, even then, it wasn’t like people were, like, “Oh, great, this dating advice is all leading us into a positive direction here.”

Matthew Yglesias: Sure. There’s limits to stereotyping and big problems. It’s also true that there are real measurable gender differences in personality that I think it is worthwhile for people to be aware of.

Jerusalem Demsas: But you’re not date — this is one of the things where I’m like, there are things that are obviously true. It is obviously true that when you look at studies of Big Five personality traits and you map people based on whether they’re male or female, you see differences, on average, here. But you’re not dating on average. You’re dating an individual person.

Matthew Yglesias: I agree, but I mean, yes, but a person needs to understand, a heterosexual person needs to understand, knowing that they themselves are an individual, and then anybody they may date will also be an individual...

Jerusalem Demsas: But that’s, I think, actually not focused on...

Matthew Yglesias: I agree, I agree, but I mean, but you also do need to situate yourself, you know, in the landscape of averages, right? So, like, you know, in the uncontroversial realm of physical attributes, like, most women prefer to date men who are taller than them. So, fair enough.

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s very controversial actually.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, OK, but that’s just true. And, also, you know, if you’re a woman who is 5’10”, you need to be aware that you are substantially taller than the average woman. And the set of men who are taller than you is a relatively small number of people. Now, you can do with that what you will, but you should know that factually. And I do think that everybody knows that factually.

Where I think people sometimes lose sight of it, right, is, like, women experience more anxiety and depression on average than men do.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, women are diagnosed more.

Matthew Yglesias: No, no, no, no, no. I mean, not just clinical, right? But just, you know, fleeting anxiety is just more commonly experienced.

Jerusalem Demsas: I just mean, like, there’s, like, a dispute about whether it’s in the survey data because men aren’t admitting to it or whether it’s literal experiences.

Matthew Yglesias: OK. Yes, there is. And, but, you know, again, in the survey — And men get angry more than women, right? And so, again, not everybody all the time, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah.

But, in most heterosexual couples, it is just likely if you are a typical woman that you will experience more anxiety than the man that you are dating.

Jerusalem Demsas: But I think this is actually the point. Like, this is an example of the way in which these averages can actually confuse things because, first, I mean, again, it is true, literally true, what you’re saying that when you look at surveys, men will report anger more than women, etc. There’s a lot of evidence for this.

I think often this is also, like, a labeling exercise. Like, what is anger? Like, what does it look like? How does it function? What does it mean? I mean, these are things that are often at some level — it’s difficult to even categorize what another person’s experience of an emotion is. So there’s that issue.

Matthew Yglesias: Yes, philosophy is challenging.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yes, but even across genders, too, there’s obviously socialization here. The way that women express anger versus men, men are going to — we know that men have more testosterone, so that can lead to rates of aggression that are different than women, on average.

I think if you’re in a relationship, or you’re trying to date people, and in your head you have, “Well, men are more angry than women on average. Women are more anxious than men on average,” how does that help you get to a relationship in a better way?

I think, in general, what it does is it actually has you be less curious about the individual you’re interacting with than more curious. You’re, like, “Oh, he expressed anger, that’s what guys do. No need to ask him questions about, like, why he’s angry. Like, the men are — men be angry.” Like, I think that’s what —

I think, in general, that’s what stereotypes do, is they flatten individuals to the point where people don’t need to be curious about their causes. “I don’t need to be curious about why my wife’s anxious about cleaning. She’s a woman. Women are anxious about cleaning.”

Like, it’s just one of those things where I’m, like, what? If you were instead, like, “Oh, I’m approaching Matt as an individual person. He seems really angry. Why are you angry?” Like, that’s just a different way of relating to the world. And, as someone who spends most of my life in population averages and in stats, I think it’s so useless as a way of relating to individual people.

Matthew Yglesias: But, like, go back to your content, right? Where it’s, like, all these women and they’re stressing out about, you know, the details of, like, “when did this guy text them?” or something, right? And the game that those content creators are playing, right, is they are trying to, like, exacerbate the anxieties of anxious people in order to get them continually clicking and scrolling and so on and so forth.

And it might be useful, I think, to women to know that just most men, on average, are just more casual in their way of doing things than most women are, and that there is probably no signal in the noise. And that that’s just, like, an op — a possibility that you should open yourself to, that, like, the nature of, you know, a heterosexual relationship is that it is probably going to pair one person who is physically larger than the other person, is probably gonna pair one person who is more, like, you know...

Jerusalem Demsas: For our people on audio, Matt made a very interesting face and waved his arms about.

Matthew Yglesias: More, like, worried about what the exact meaning of a social interaction was than a man was. And that’s not just true of the heterosexual interactions themselves.

Like, as a married person, you know, I hear, like, what women say about their interactions with other women, and they just perceive there to be more going on than most of the men I know in their interactions with other people. And that, you know, it’s fine if you’re not like that, if you’re an exception, like, that’s great. But that if you were just having a typical experience of the world. People have this kind of binary quality, and everyone could relax about it a little.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think we agree on the, like, end state of, like, how someone should approach dating as, like, “Hey, people are very different and you should approach them that way.” But I don’t really get how someone knowing gender stereotypes outside of, like, the way that...

We all know gender stereotypes. We, like, live in a culture and a society where there’s, like, movies and, like, gender is obviously entirely, like, culturally constructed. Gender, Matthew, not sex.

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t, I forget what the difference is.

Jerusalem Demsas: We’ll do this in another pod on what gender is. But I think that I don’t really get what these stereotypes are benefiting. When you’re, like, a young man trying to get a date, like, what does it help you to know that, like, women are anxious about...

Matthew Yglesias: Well, I don’t know. I mean, you opened my eyes to some of this, like, pathological woman-focused content, which I felt would have benefited at some points from just, like, a more clear discussion of what kinds of gender differences we are dealing with out there.

Again, you know, in terms of, you know, people set standards for themselves, and that’s great, and that’s fine, but that you should know, you know, how unusual is what you’re looking for out there, right? I’ve said, you know, men you always...

Jerusalem Demsas: There’s a form of content like this, right? Where they’re like, I think it’s, like, a lot of manosphere guys will do sort of, “OK, if you’re looking for a 6-foot man who earns six figures, who has a job and hasn’t been to jail or whatever, you’re talking about X% of the population,” there’s a lot of content about that.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure, yes, yes, yes. There’s versions of that, and usually in this kind of particular — but I guess, people will be stressed out about their girlfriend or whatever, and it’s, like, “Oh, why can’t she be more chill and whatever?”

And I hear you on that, but also, it’s just, if you’re a typical man, the odds that you will meet a woman who is, like, as quote-unquote “chill” as you are just very, very low. You know what I mean? Like, there’s just a — there is a gap.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think we’ve found a real argument here, which is that I think it would be extremely bad advice to tell your male friend, “Well, yeah, women be neurotic, so chill out.” Like, I think that probably you should not engage with your relationships that way.

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t know, but I mean, it’s like, I think you have to — I don’t think you have to — but I think it is...

Jerusalem Demsas: You should accept the neuroticism of women because that’s just going to be true for heterosexual men to deal with.

Matthew Yglesias: That, like, it’s just the way that things are going to be, you know, unless you’re in a real statistical outlier relationship, which some people are.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think the way that we’re talking about this indicates that we think, or that you think maybe, that, like, most women are these, like, really neurotic.

Matthew Yglesias: No!

Jerusalem Demsas: We’re talking about small, small gaps.

Matthew Yglesias: I mean, yes, the gaps are not gigantic or anything, you know, in the absolute scheme of things, but they do structure people’s experiences of each other. Or the flip side, right? Like, the ones where it’s like, some of your, your Instagram women are, like, “The man should be, like, planning all this stuff for you,” right? And it’s like, I don’t know why, you know?

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, men need to — Isn’t this male content, too, where it’s like, you need to plan a date, you need to have an idea of what dinner is and what’s for — where you’re gonna go after. Like, women like men who have a plan.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, no, no, no, I mean, it is, right? And, you know, but so, when you look at the extroversion personality construct, men and women are about equal, right? But women score higher on certain things related to, like, interpersonal warmth, and men score higher on, like, sensation-seeking, right? So...

Jerusalem Demsas: OK. What does that mean? Like, sensation seeking?

Matthew Yglesias: Well, it’s like trying to come up with stuff that’ll be, like, cool and exciting.

Jerusalem Demsas: OK.

Matthew Yglesias: Right. So, and we all in life, I think, free ride to some extent on people who are, like, more sensation seeking than we are, right? Like, somebody who gets you to go do something that you wouldn’t have done on your own, but then it turns out to be fun, right?

And so, like, on average, men are more likely to be like that than women are, which is I think part of the reason why this kind of stereotype-based script arises.

Jerusalem Demsas: I don’t think that’s true at all.

Matthew Yglesias: That a man should be coming up with all this amazing fun stuff to do.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think it’s mostly that...

Matthew Yglesias: And as a very boring person, personally, I struggle with this.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think largely what’s happening there is this idea, women will be, like, oh, on Hinge or whatever, hey, a guy will ask them out and they’ll be, like, “OK, what do you want to do?” And the guy will be like, “yeah, what do you want to do?” It’s like, “you make the plan.”

And it’s like, often a result of the guy trying to be, like, really easygoing and chill, but really he’s just having the woman do all the planning effort of, like, finding a bar, saying, like, we should meet here and at this time, and making the reservation.

And that, like, often there’s this — actually, I think that part of what’s going on in a lot of why female-focused dating content is so focused on how to prevent you ending up in a bad relationship, is that, like, you know, you’re trying to figure out if, like, this guy you’re dating is going to turn into the guy who’s going to make you do everything around the house and, like, isn’t going to be a good partner to you and all the logistical parts of life.

And, like, one of the big signals of this early on is, like, when you’re asked out on a date, are you asked out and, like, the guy has, like, the capacity to call and make a dinner reservation. To figure out where you live and is it near you? Is it, like, equidistant to you, or is it, like, near your work? Or is it at a good time? Has he, like, checked ahead to make sure the — whatever, like, all these things. And I think it’s, like, much less about, like, sensation seeking and more about, like, can you do the mundane things about life that are boring?

Matthew Yglesias: Ah, but see, well, yes and no. I mean, I agree, you know, you’re trying to display some competence, right? That that’s, like, out there, so on and so forth. But, I mean, part of the question — I mean, this was in Noah’s piece. It’s like, what are people looking for out of each other, right? Like, why do people want to be in relationships at all?

Because we’re not in a world of strict economic complementarities, you know, like we used to be, right? It’s — people have companionate marriages where they’re supposed to get along and spend time with each other and bring joy into other people’s lives. I thought that whole, like, feminine energy thing was, like, kind of silly, almost deliberately so.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think they’re very serious.

Matthew Yglesias: But there’s, like, something to the idea of, like, what is it that you are, like, bringing to the table, right? Like, why is there a man and a woman in this couple? Like, what are we doing?

Jerusalem Demsas: I think it actually really explains why so much of coupling is going down, is that there is kind of a reconsideration of, like, what is the value that each of us are bringing to each other’s lives?

And, like, you know, part of why you see we see it going down most with non-college-educated men, particularly ones who have been to jail, is that, like, if there’s not even, like, this, like, material benefit that you’re adding to someone’s relationships, then even more so it becomes, like, a big question mark of, like, “Well, are you making — Are we reading books together? Like, are we, like, watching movies together? Are we, like, are we enjoying something else here?

And, like, that’s just not the kinds of questions people were asking because, like, to be clear, marriage was largely about, like, having a family, and then you need two people to create children and then take care of them together.

And, like, if that is no longer, like, those things aren’t so closely linked, like, I think it does become very hard to justify, like, well, is the marginal marriage actually that beneficial for the couple that’s getting together without that, like, larger cultural script around family rearing?

Matthew Yglesias: So that’s what I thought was interesting and a little saddening about the female-focused dating content, which is that it did mostly seem to me to presuppose that, like, you, like, don’t necessarily — like, not that you don’t want to meet somebody and get married, but that you mostly should view dating as this, like, minefield of asymmetrical information in which you are being warned off various landmines...

Jerusalem Demsas: Which kind of feels true, I don’t know...

Matthew Yglesias: ...No, I mean, there’s some truth to it. I wasn’t like, “I don’t understand what this is for,” exactly, but it was kind of sad. I thought.

Jerusalem Demsas: It is sad. I mean, this is how I feel looking at all — I mean, this is why my original take was just so, like — I mean, Matt had to half convince me to do this podcast because I was just like, “I find this thing so depressing,” because I just felt so much through the 2010s that a lot of what had happened is that I had become very alienated against men as an entity.

I was like, “These are, on average, until, like, they’ve proven differently, they’re bad people. And, like, they have these...” And, like, it was very essentialist. It was very much like, “There’s something wrong with them. They have this structural power imbalance. They’ve been coded to believe that women are gonna, like, have to serve them, have to do all these sorts of things I was witnessing.”

And, like, we were obviously seeing, as a culture, all of this stuff around how men had exploited women in all these various industries. All these very true things that happened, and a lot of men who not maybe didn’t participate in it, but covered it up, or made jokes about it, or whatever it is.

And I just think that this sort of engagement with one another, even when there are truths underneath it, is only going to serve to really tear apart men and women from being able to understand each other and be friends.

And we live in a society. You will always live with men and women near each other. That’s just going to happen because that’s how procreation occurs. That’s how you get more people.

Matthew Yglesias: That’s a very optimistic take. I’m not...

Jerusalem Demsas: I think that there’s, like, it would be really, really bad if we just end up in a situation where people really do believe that these stereotypes are correct, especially as coupling rates are going down. And so there’s no way to actually disprove this in the long term.

If you’re, whatever, men, when they’re in their 20s, if they have this view of women, and women in their 20s have this view, but then they end up married anyway, and then they realize, “Hey, this is a man who is very different from averages in certain respects even if they’re similar in other respects,” then that can falsify that viewpoint.

And I think you see this often when people have children of the opposite gender. You see this with men who have daughters end up being much more positive on various egalitarian policies around women’s suffrage, women’s ability to access high levels in the workplace, competence, etc.

You need these close relationships with people of the opposite gender to, like, see their humanity. And, like, I worry that this kind of content is, like, pulling us apart rather than bringing us together.

Matthew Yglesias: Should we do some peer review?

Jerusalem Demsas: Let’s peer review it.

Matthew Yglesias: One thing that we know brings men and women together is fracking. Previous papers that we’ve talked about show — because fracking on the producer side has a kind of gendered impact. It’s mostly men working out there.

But this paper by Lucas W. Davis is titled “How Much Has Shale Gas Saved U.S. Consumers?“ And so, it was just looking at the question of, on the consumption side, like, what is the benefit to Americans of this increase in natural gas production?

They are able to just look, pretty cleanly, at price differences between the U.S., Europe, and Japan, because even though gas is traded globally, there’s a physical constraint on how much gas can be exported. So, the gas has become quite a bit cheaper in the United States.

And they find it is saving American consumers about $164 to $227 billion per year, which seems like a lot, a lot of money. And, I mean, this is a major — people have been talking a lot about the sort of divergence between the American and European economies. And this is, like, a non-trivial slice of that pie; is just, like, we’ve got this cheap gas.

And I don’t know, I feel like in progressive circles, nobody wants to talk about it. This unfolded largely during the Obama and Biden presidencies, also during Trump’s first term.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, I mean, I think the reason people don’t want to talk about it, or at least — I don’t know if it’s not “don’t want to talk about it,” but don’t view it as uncomplicatedly positive is because there are fossil fuel implications of the natural gas industry.

Matthew Yglesias: Oh. What’s a fossil fuel?

Jerusalem Demsas: I mean, the shale revolution, in many ways, is like a technological revolution where they figure out how to, I guess, frack down and then to the side, but then they also figure out how to jet stream water down and that pushes the oil out — sorry, the natural gas out.

I don’t actually — Any physicists out there?

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t think that’s how it works.

Jerusalem Demsas: I think it is how it works. What do you mean? I think it’s, yeah, it’s, like, they pump and pressurize water and then —

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, to fracture the shale.

Jerusalem Demsas: Yeah, and then, yeah, that’s what natural, that’s what shale gas is. But anyway, apologies for my lack of shale knowledge.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, I don’t know. I’ve never drilled.

Jerusalem Demsas: Someone who’s drilled in the comments, let us know who was closer, me or Matt. But anyway, I mean, I think that there’s actually a really interesting debate about whether this was positive or negative for the environment.

That has to do with, like, what did natural gas displace? Like, what would we have done with energy without that?

But also, I think a much more difficult, like, calculation problem, which is methane gets released when you are producing natural gas. And so, like, methane is way worse for the environment than carbon emissions because it just traps heat more potently. And so, it heats up the Earth faster if you were just gonna put it, if you had just as much methane.

Matthew Yglesias: But it dissipates fast.

Jerusalem Demsas: It does, but, like — this is my point, it’s a very difficult calculation, because you have to measure these short-term versus long-term. You have to measure how much methane leaking is happening in different scenarios. What happens if there is a natural gas disaster or when a bunch of it leaks out? If there’s an accident or whatever.

But then, on top of that, too, what is the technological trade-offs? One idea is, like, OK, well, a bunch of investment — and this is a kind of like Acemoglu-esque argument — around, like, a bunch of investment goes into natural gas technology and hydraulic fracturing, then does that displace the type of innovation, whether it’s the capital or it’s literally the people, the scientists, the energy experts, who would otherwise have been focused on more climate-friendly green tech.

And, like, this seems, like, more likely in recent years to have panned out for the pro-natural gas side because it’s helped us figure out geothermal, which is using a lot of the same technologies as drilling.

Matthew Yglesias: Oh wait, let me talk about these environmental impacts though, ‘cause, like, I think this is really important, right? So, like, natural gas...

Jerusalem Demsas: My point is just, like, it’s a difficult calculation. I think you can, like, you can...

Matthew Yglesias: See, I actually don’t think it’s difficult. I think it’s, like, an easy calculation that has been made to seem difficult by bad actors, right? But...

Jerusalem Demsas: Like me.

Matthew Yglesias: Well, just, natural gas, on its own, has modestly lower carbon dioxide emissions than coal. It has dramatically fewer particulate emissions, you know, for air pollution, things like that. Natural gas is also, has a lot of complementarity with wind and solar because the whole deal with, you know, it’s, like, the sun doesn’t always shine, right?

Jerusalem Demsas: Famously.

Matthew Yglesias: But natural gas plants are easy to turn on and off compared to coal and nuclear plants. So, you know, this expansion of shale gas not only saved American consumers a lot of money, but it displaced a tremendous amount of coal off the grid, and it reduced carbon dioxide emissions, it reduced particulate emissions.

So, you’ve then had this other concern about the methane leaks. And now, I agree, you know, ideally you want to minimize methane leaks, but methane only counts as a more potent greenhouse gas agent if you’re using a short time horizon. And the whole argument for caring about climate change is that we should care about a long time horizon.

So, I think you’ve got a thing where, like, it was a big win for consumers, and it seems like a modest win for the environment, but it happened over the objections of the environmental movement that, like, got fracking banned in New York State and, you know, other places like that, and, like, all of Europe and stuff like that.

And it was just, like, a mistake. And there’s been a real unwillingness to own up to that.

But, you know, when I think back toSilent Spring and, like, what’s right and what’s wrong in there. Like, what was right was doing the empirical work on, like, what is the impact of large-scale pesticide spraying? And what’s wrong is this is kind of like, “Oh, change is bad.”

And here’s one where, you know, sometimes doing the empirical work supports the conclusion that change is bad. Yeah. But in this case, you know, green groups, like, they really stuck with “change is bad” in the face of the empirical evidence.

Jerusalem Demsas: So I don’t obscure the fact, which is that at the macro level, Matt and I largely agree about the shale revolution being likely positive for CO2, but I don’t want to understate the negative case, which wasn’t just about methane, which you’re correct, if you’re taking the long view, the methane leaks will wash out.

But there is this question about short-run CO2 goes down, but cheap gas kills the economic case for a lot of green technologies right at the moment that they were beginning to scale. And the counterfactual there, I think, is genuinely — was, at least at some period of time, was genuinely difficult to weigh out. I think it wasn’t clear what was going to happen with a bunch of these green technologies.

And now we’re seeing a revolution, I think, in part because of China’s investment in various green technologies, in part because of the IRA [Inflation Reduction Act], you know, some of these tax credits that have invested in things like battery and different, you know, climate change technology.

But I’m just saying, like, the case was — I want to make sure we’re not, like, strawmanning — the case was not just about methane. It was also about, like, the counterfactual here is difficult to know given how much capital and, you know, fixed costs, like, were built up in the fossil fuel sector during the shale revolution.

Matthew Yglesias: Yeah, although, even there, right, so a lot of promising clean tech, right, like, our aforementioned Chinese electric cars and stuff, right, is electrification. And part of the case for electrification is that an electric car, even if it’s powered by coal, is cleaner than a gasoline-burning car.

And similarly with home heat, right, because these little — you know, a little home furnace or, like, an engine that can fit in your car is just low efficiency compared to a power plant. And so, again, driving down the price of electricity has complemented some of these clean technologies.

Now, maybe it’s slowed the deployment of wind turbines and solar panels, although I’m skeptical, but I see what that argument is about. But it just seems, even on the technological side, like it’s mixed bag, right? Like, accelerant to geothermal. I don’t know — should we tell people what geothermal is? No?

Jerusalem Demsas: Go ahead.

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t know.

Jerusalem Demsas: You don’t know?

Matthew Yglesias: No, this is the one where I’m only faking that I know. It’s like there’s hot stuff underground. So, if you can drill, you can put water through the drilled hole, and it will boil because the rocks are hot.

Jerusalem Demsas: This is incredible.

Matthew Yglesias: I don’t know.

Jerusalem Demsas: No, I think that’s right. But I don’t know. I mean, like...

Matthew Yglesias: So, you need better drills.

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s the heat beneath the Earth~~‘s crust~~, and we’re getting it out somehow. There’s tons of energy. There’s a reason why, in Iceland, there’s tons of geothermal. It’s because, if you’ve been to Iceland, a lot of volcanoes.

Matthew Yglesias: I have been to Iceland. Have you been to Iceland?

Jerusalem Demsas: I have been to Iceland. I visited the geothermal and carbon capture and storage facility. It’s very exciting.

Matthew Yglesias: Wow, that’s fancy. I went to the Blue Lagoon, which is just a giant swimming pool.

Jerusalem Demsas: It was closed because of a volcanic eruption when I was there. But getting back to the original point of the paper, I do think it’s underrated how much the environmental movement has been in contention with, like, cheap electricity prices. Because, like, there’s this, like, obscuring of this fact in many respects, like, because people were trying to convince themselves, us, everyone, that the price of green energy had gone down.

And, like, that is true. And, like, it is true that in some specific situations, like, the price of solar is cheaper than the price of given fossil fuel or whatever. And, like, we see this on the Texas grid all the time.

But I think that, at a macro level, it is the case that if you, like, stopped drilling for all fossil fuels, energy prices would go up. And, like, this conversation got very confused.

And so, I think there are actually a lot of people out there who are, like, unaware of the massive, massive shitstorm, politically, we’d be in if we just had not had the shale revolution.

Matthew Yglesias: Well yeah, I mean, the distinction, right, is oftentimes the cheapest thing to do is to deploy renewables. It’s never the case that, like, making fossil fuels illegal is gonna save you money because you just wouldn’t do it, right?

Like, the environmental impact of banning fossil fuels is coming from preventing them being deployed in cases where that would be a cheap option. And it doesn’t mean you should never do it, right?

And I get that people don’t like to own up to trade-offs, and so on and so forth, but at a certain point, if you start taking all your advice and information from people who won’t ever own up to trade-offs, you’re going to really struggle to govern correctly.

And, you know, I again, like, not to, you know, play dumb. I understand why Democrats — senators — have mixed feelings about the fact that the economy of the Obama and Biden years was substantially powered by the growth of shale gas. But it’s factually true that that’s what happened. And that being unwilling or unable to say that — you know, narrowly, who cares? More broadly, like, if you want to be effective stewards of the economy, you need to be able to have, like, accurate factual information about what’s happening. And I worry increasingly about, you know, whether we’re there or not.

Jerusalem Demsas: Well, now that we’ve started greenbashing, I think that’s a good time to close out the podcast. We got that in.

Matthew Yglesias: Sure.

Jerusalem Demsas: If you enjoy the podcast, like, comment, subscribe. We’re new, we’re growing. Tell your friends; it’s the best way that podcasts grow.

Matthew Yglesias: If you meet somebody at a bar, be like, “Have you heard this new podcast The Argument?”

Jerusalem Demsas: It’s a great pickup line. It’s worked on many, many people.

Matthew Yglesias: It’s guaranteed to work.

Jerusalem Demsas: This is the new game, The Argument. Well, thanks. See you next week.

Matthew Yglesias: Bye.

↩ Back to article summary · ↪ See Claude's response · ↩ Back to standalones table

Index by Author

Author Title Publication Summary
Scott Alexander Preliminary Thoughts On The Midjourney Scanner ACT Alexander argues the much-hyped MidJourney whole-body ultrasound scanner solves no current diagnostic gap and is at best a bet that AI will improve ultrasound specifically.
Scott Alexander Waiting For The Miracle ACT Alexander recounts traveling to Medjugorje and proposes a detailed optical-physiological mechanism — dimmed-sun afterimages plus UV chromatopsia — that explains the famous Fatima/Medjugorje "spinning sun miracle" without the supernatural.
Scott Alexander Never Cross a River Four Feet Deep on Average ACT Putilin shows a hyped 2023 brain-entrainment learning study fails to replicate and was probably never real — its headline effect driven by a few bored participants and obscured by averaging.
Michael C. Behrent A Jewish Frenchman’s Case for Christian Nationalism Compact Behrent reconstructs Éric Zemmour's "cultural Catholicism" as a Renan-derived nationalism in which a non-believing Jewish Frenchman defends Catholic France as a shared historical inheritance — order without doctrine, "Catholicism minus Christianity."
Ray Boshara The Case for Trump Accounts: A Debate Commonplace Boshara argues Trump Accounts are worth supporting despite likely raising inequality because the right question is whether poor kids are better off with an account than without one — and the evidence on stock exposure, community contributions, and "asset effects" says yes.
Luke Burgis How America lost the art of association UnHerd (publication) Burgis argues that the atrophy of intermediary civic associations leaves "the one" directly exposed to "the 99," and that small differentiated fellowships — not mass movements — are the antidote to social contagion and creeping post-totalitarianism.
Tyler Cowen Tyler Cowen: A Dangerous Turn in AI Regulation Free Press Cowen argues the US government's first export-control kill switch on a frontier AI model (Anthropic's Fable 5) is a self-inflicted geopolitical loss: it makes American AI a less trustworthy platform, pushes fence-sitting nations toward Chinese and open-source models, and exposes that "AI sovereignty" is unachievable for most countries anyway.
Tyler Cowen How research in math will change (from my email) Marginal Revolution Cowen relays a working mathematician's account that human-guided AI is already accelerating real math research — testing conjectures, salvaging half-finished proofs, and soon overhauling peer review.
Tyler Cowen Labor market effects of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act Marginal Revolution Cowen flags new evidence that the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act measurably raised labor-force participation and employment, using state-level tax variation for identification.
Tyler Cowen Adrian Wooldridge on Sweden and liberalism Marginal Revolution Cowen endorses Wooldridge's account of Sweden as a working model of liberalism: fiscal rectitude plus pro-market reform plus a sharp restrictionist turn on immigration and crime.
Dominic Cummings How to end the Brexit shitshow UnHerd (publication) Cummings argues that rejoining the EU is Westminster's evasion of state failure, and that only a Farage-led DOGE-style assault using the PM's unwritten-constitution powers can fix Britain.
Freddie deBoer Google Made a Sad Boomer Mark Out of Me and There's Nothing I Can Do About It deBoer deBoer turns a personal customer-service ordeal—Google's discontinued smoke detectors and a Gemini-recommended scam link—into an argument that tech giants use LLMs to offload all accountability onto powerless consumers.
Freddie deBoer Like All Good Things, Sports Are Owned by the Rich Now deBoer deBoer uses the Knicks' championship and its courtside parade of attention-seeking billionaires to argue that all good things—seats, neighborhoods, cities—eventually flow entirely to the rich.
Euro|topics (curated debate) UK: Burnham one step further in power struggle Euro|topics (discovery) Euro|topics aggregates British and Italian press on Andy Burnham's by-election win clearing his path to challenge Keir Starmer, with commentators split between "his time hasn't come / he's a careerist springboarding" and "he offers the hope Starmer can't."
Euro|topics (curated debate) EU eases rules on GMO plants: a good move? Euro|topics (discovery) Euro|topics aggregates French and German press on the EU Parliament deregulating gene-edited (NGT) crops, splitting along a competitiveness/innovation-versus-precaution axis: French papers cheer it as a strategic-autonomy paradigm shift, German papers split between cautious approval and an ignored-ecological-risk warning.
Niall Ferguson Niall Ferguson: It’s Too Soon to Call This a U.S. Surrender Free Press Ferguson concedes the Iran MOU is a "lousy," Suez-like document with carrots more enticing than Obama's 2015 deal, but argues it is too soon to call it surrender because peace is made by unforeseen second-order consequences, not pieces of paper.
Francis Fukuyama The Art of the Non-Deal Persuasion Fukuyama argues Trump's celebrated Iran "deal" is a total U.S. capitulation that merely restores the pre-war status quo he himself destroyed.
John Fund Socialism Is on the March National Review Fund argues the Democratic Socialists of America have become the country's fastest-growing political force — a radicalized, machine-like entity filling the vacuum left by liberals' failure to govern cities.
Loic Gapin The CFA Franc Is Not a Currency. It Is a Constitutional Constraint African Arguments (publication) Gapin argues the CFA franc debate is misframed as economics: the prior question is constitutional — by what right fourteen sovereign African states anchor monetary policy to Paris — and the arrangement endures not by French trickery but because it serves the local elites empowered to leave it.
Nils Gilman The University As We Know It Is Finished Persuasion Gilman argues AI dissolves the glue holding the research university together, forcing its breakup into separate research, residential, and teaching institutions—and a return to the classical liberal-arts core.
Rebeccah Heinrichs The MOU Gives the Momentum to Iran National Review Heinrichs argues the US-Iran MOU hands Iran the Strait of Hormuz, billions in oil revenue, and post-war momentum — trading a winning military position away for a 60-day oil-price reprieve.
Coleman Hughes Coleman Hughes: The Case for Progressive Patriotism Free Press Hughes argues progressives can and should make a values-based case for American patriotism, grounding it in revealed preference (America is migrants' top destination) and comparative data showing the US is among the world's most racially progressive societies — not against an imagined utopia but against the world as it actually is.
Ken Klippenstein Why Military Was at the UFC White House Event Ken Klippenstein Klippenstein argues the real scandal of the White House UFC event was the dozen military units lending "faux dignity" to a partisan, corporately branded spectacle — and that no one noticing is the most alarming part.
Declan Leary What Not to Do About Gerontocracy Commonplace Leary argues that Samuel Moyn's anti-gerontocracy program would worsen the problem because America's boomer elite is not "conservative" but a generation refusing to age, and the cure is restoring family and limiting migration, not socialist redistribution.
Michael Lind Against Trump Accounts: A Debate Commonplace Lind argues Trump Accounts (and all "baby bond" child trust funds) are a bad idea because middle-class security should come from wages and social insurance, not capital ownership, and the accounts are a regressive Trojan horse for privatizing Social Security.
Edward Luttwak Can Britain's military be saved? UnHerd (publication) Luttwak argues that Britain's historically rare discipline of funding actual combat readiness over bases, bureaucracy, and weapons-procurement is now being killed by welfare spending and defense-industry capture, with Healey's resignation as the warning shot.
Andrew C. McCarthy Reckoning with the ‘Binary Choice,’ After Trump’s Iran Capitulation National Review McCarthy concedes the Iran deal is among America's worst foreign-policy blunders but defends his three Trump votes as the unavoidable cost of a binary electoral choice he can't bring himself to dodge.
Robinson Meyer Trump’s War on Gigawatts Heatmap (publication) Heatmap argues the Trump administration's latest paid cancellation of offshore wind leases is both a war on wind and a self-defeating attack on power-constrained coastal economies.
Douglas Murray Douglas Murray: How Trump Fell Into the Iranian Trap Free Press Murray argues the Iran MOU is a trap that front-loads $300 billion and sanctions relief to a serial-lying regime in exchange for back-loaded, unenforceable nuclear promises — repeating the JCPOA error Trump spent three campaigns denouncing.
Jørgen Møller The Myth of Authoritarian Efficiency Persuasion Møller and Skaaning marshal the comparative-politics literature to argue that "benevolent efficient dictatorship" is a mirage—democracies outperform autocracies on war, growth, disaster-avoidance, and climate.
River Page Two Drinks with. . . The Trump Adviser Headed to OpenAI Free Press In a drinks-interview profile, AI-policy insider Dean Ball — author of Trump's AI Action Plan, now joining OpenAI — defends accelerationist AI policy and a wait-and-see stance on mass job displacement, conceding "a lot of crazy and destabilizing shit might go down" and that some catastrophes "simply must happen."
Eboo Patel What Obama Meant Persuasion Patel argues "Obama-ism"—a governing philosophy elevating the optimistic civic-bridgebuilding social sector—collapsed when nonprofits adopted Ta-Nehisi Coates's slavery-defined narrative, and that path led not to revolution but to Trump.
Emily Pontecorvo Anthropic Is Buying Carbon Removal — But Not Clean Power Heatmap (publication) Heatmap argues Anthropic's new carbon-removal commitment highlights, rather than resolves, the AI firm's near-total absence from clean-power procurement despite its high-carbon-intensity data centers and ethical reputation.
Brian Potter Converting Coal Plants to Natural Gas Construction Physics (Potter) Potter explains the engineering and economics of coal-to-gas plant conversions and argues the era is largely over despite the Trump administration's pro-coal push and AI-driven power demand.
Iskander Rehman The Fall of Fortress Singapore: Three Lessons from the Collapse of Britain’s Great Asian Bastion War on the Rocks (publication) Rehman argues the 1942 fall of Singapore prefigures America's "simultaneity challenge" — that intense force concentration in one theater (Iran) silently decrements the capacity to defend Taiwan.
Juan David Rojas The Fujimori Restoration Compact Rojas argues a Keiko Fujimori win, though carried by the diaspora and won by the dynasty most responsible for Peru's decade of instability, may paradoxically be the country's best near-term hope for restoring order.
Noah Rothman How Do America’s Enemies Think About the Iran War? National Review Rothman argues that America's stunning tactical performance in the Iran war is undone by the MOU's strategic surrender, which teaches Beijing and Moscow that US military prowess won't be sustained — inviting miscalculation over Taiwan and the Baltics.
Matthew Schmitz The Revolutionary Significance of Trump’s White House UFC Fight Compact Schmitz reads Trump's White House UFC event as a genuine political ritual — combat sport displacing baseball's republican ideal and football's managerial empire to enact Trump's agonistic, anti-elite "revolution."
M.G. Scott Leonard Cohen’s Dark Faith Compact Scott reads Leonard Cohen's late "dark faith" through her grandfather Peter Dale Scott's lifelong friendship with him, framing Cohen's bleak submission to a violent, inscrutable God as one pole of a generative yin/yang dialectic between darkness and light.
Nate Silver How Trump conquered the Republican Party Nate Silver Silver revisits his 2015 failure to predict Trump's nomination, arguing he over-trusted the "Party Decides" elite-endorsement model and mistook a small-sample narrative for an iron law.
Noah Smith Iran is Trump's Katrina Noahpinion Smith argues Trump's Iran war is a genuine, unambiguous U.S. military defeat — not merely a strategic mistake — because Iran's drone-enabled Hormuz blockade forced America into concessions worse than the pre-war status quo.
Noah Smith Will China, Inc. be zombified? Noahpinion Smith argues China is replaying Japan's 1990s "zombie company" trap — and that state control of the banks makes it worse, not better, because zombies still hoard labor, materials, and energy from healthy firms.
Noah Smith Does anything I write matter anymore? Noahpinion Smith reflects that punditry's influence is waning along three axes — populism makes leaders deaf to ideas, Substack monetization silos writers away from each other, and AI's flood of good-enough content reduces every post to skimmable noise — while holding out hope that writing "for the AIs" is a new channel of influence.
Matt Stoller Why the Iran War Hasn't Crashed the Economy BIG (Stoller) Stoller argues the Iran war didn't crash the U.S. economy because fifty years of public stockpiling and energy diversification — a New Deal-era anti-monopoly statecraft the press refuses to credit — absorbed the Hormuz shock.
Matt Stoller Monopoly Round-Up: The Pope and a Silicon Valley Trillionaire Fight Over God BIG (Stoller) Stoller argues Musk's trillion-dollar SpaceX valuation is manufactured through self-dealing and rigged markets, and uses Pope Leo XIV's anti-AI encyclical to frame the deeper conflict as efficiency-worship versus human limits.
SW Siri AI’s Future in China Unclear Under “One Apple, Two Systems” China Digital Times (discovery) A China Digital Times digest reports that Apple's new AI Siri will skip mainland China (as well as most of the EU), spawning the meme "One Apple, Two Systems" and exposing how Beijing's political-content safety rules — not market readiness — gate Western AI's entry.
Alex Tabarrok Colorado’s Funeral Mistake Marginal Revolution Tabarrok argues Colorado is wrongly relicensing funeral workers in response to gruesome abuses, since the abuses weren't actually worse under delicensing and licensing doesn't prevent the behavior that scandalized the public.
Alex Tabarrok Cuba Marginal Revolution Tabarrok highlights Cuba's sweeping new market liberalization as a belated admission that six decades of central planning ruined the country.
Ruy Teixeira The Long, Slow Death of Social Democracy Commonplace Teixeira argues social democracy died because its economic model, its working-class base, and its intra-Left influence all collapsed at once, leaving a "Brahmin Left" of educated professionals that prioritizes sociocultural causes over the material interests of the workers it abandoned.
Adam Tooze Chartbook 452 Europe's Machiavellian moment? Chartbook (Tooze) Tooze argues that the fashionable "return of history" framing of Europe's crises — Luuk van Middelaar's Machiavellian "politics of events" — mistakes overdetermined, decipherable processes for blind chance, and that the trope reveals technocratic disorientation rather than realism.
François Valentin An Actually Popular British Politician Persuasion Valentin profiles Andy Burnham as Labour's likely next leader while arguing his signature "Manchesterism" brand contradicts the actual, pro-business, post-industrial record it claims to represent.
Steven Watts The Death and Life of American Exceptionalism Compact Watts argues American exceptionalism survives its post-1960s collapse if recast in Lincoln's chastened form — a blessing the nation must continually re-earn under providential judgment, not a settled boast.
Amélie Jaques-Apke and Lily Wojtowicz Macron’s Nuclear Gamble: Building a European Deterrent Faster Than French Politics Can Tear Down War on the Rocks (publication) Jaques-Apke and Wojtowicz argue Macron's European-deterrent gambit is real but structurally hollow — forward presence, not nuclear sharing — and likely to die in the 2027 election before it can be entrenched.
Matthew Yglesias Against the “Positive Good” theory of Platner Slow Boring Yglesias argues the "Positive Good" theory — that Graham Platner's scandals make him a better Democrat — is incoherent cope for leftists who want economic radicalism without the cultural moderation that actually wins swing voters.
Matthew Yglesias How to fix transit construction in America Slow Boring Yglesias (framing a guest "Transit Abundance Playbook" by Will Poff-Webster and Arnab Datta of the Institute for Progress) argues America's transit problem isn't underspending but cost bloat — it spends a lot per dollar and builds little — and offers a 16-item menu of procurement, permitting, design, and state-capacity reforms to fix it.
Matthew Yglesias The real problem with “gifted” education Slow Boring Yglesias argues "gifted" education's prestige is almost entirely selection effects — admitted kids do well because they were already high-ability, not because the programs teach better — and that the real scandal is parents' total indifference to instructional efficacy versus peer composition.
Matthew Yglesias Better immigration can help fix the debt Slow Boring Yglesias argues skilled and fee-attached legal immigration is a badly underrated deficit-reduction tool — a "double dividend" via progressive-tax revenue and a larger debt-to-GDP denominator — that fiscal hawks ignore because they treat budget concerns as a culture-war bludgeon, not a problem to solve.
Matthew Yglesias What New York used to be Slow Boring In a wide-ranging mailbag, Yglesias argues the Sanders/Mamdani left has won the discourse and personnel battles while achieving almost nothing in durable policy, and that polarization is a real, elite-driven constraint that justifies partisan voting heuristics except in low-stakes local races.
Matthew Yglesias Grading the internet’s dating advice Slow Boring In a podcast transcript, Yglesias and Jerusalem Demsas debate online dating advice — he argues liberals' aversion to stereotypes cedes "practical advice-giving" to right-wing figures, she counters that gender-stereotype-based advice is both unkind and ineffective because you date individuals, not averages.
Matthew Zeitlin FERC Has a New Plan for Data Centers Heatmap (publication) Heatmap reports FERC's bipartisan 5-0 directive ordering regional electricity markets to write their own rules for connecting data centers without shifting costs onto residential ratepayers.
Ryan Zickgraf A tombstone for Obamaism UnHerd (publication) Zickgraf reads the new Obama Presidential Center as the unintentional mausoleum of "Obamaism" — the failed faith that symbolism and narrative could substitute for structural political change.

↩ Back to TOC
